title: Ribollita
description: Ribollita is a hearty Tuscan peasant soup whose name means 'reboiled', reflecting its origins as a dish made from leftover minestrone, thickened with stale bread and enriched with cavolo nero (Tuscan black kale). Born from frugality, it has become one of Tuscany's most celebrated comfort foods. The soup features cannellini beans, root vegetables, and day-old saltless Tuscan bread, which breaks down into the broth to create a thick, porridge-like consistency. Traditionally, it is prepared one day and reheated the next, as the flavours deepen overnight.

substitutions.notes: slightly less authentic but works well

ingredients.name: chicken stock
ingredients.notes: or vegetable stock for vegetarian version

steps.instruction: If using dried beans, drain the soaked cannellini beans and place in a large pot. Cover with fresh water by 5 cm, bring to the boil, then simmer for 1 hour until tender. Drain, reserving 250 ml of the cooking liquid. If using tinned beans, skip this step.
Heat the olive oil in a large heavy-bottomed pot over medium heat. Add the onion, carrots, and celery. Cook, stirring occasionally, for 8-10 minutes until softened and translucent.
Add the garlic and cook for 1 minute until fragrant. Add the crushed tomatoes, rosemary, bay leaf, and Parmesan rind if using. Stir well.
Add the stock and two-thirds of the cooked beans. Bring to a simmer and cook for 30 minutes. Mash the remaining beans with a fork and stir into the soup to thicken it.
Add the cavolo nero and cook for 10 minutes until wilted and tender.
Stir in the torn bread and let the soup sit for at least 30 minutes (ideally overnight in the fridge). Reheat gently before serving, drizzled generously with extra virgin olive oil.

image_placeholder: A rustic terracotta bowl of thick ribollita soup with dark kale and white beans visible, drizzled with golden olive oil, served with crusty bread on a worn wooden table.
wine_pairing_notes: A young Chianti or Vernaccia di San Gimignano pairs beautifully with the earthy beans and kale.
tips: Ribollita tastes even better the next day — make it a day ahead and reheat gently for the most authentic result.
Use genuine Tuscan saltless bread if available; its neutral flavour absorbs the broth perfectly without making the soup too salty.
Store in the fridge for up to 4 days or freeze for up to 3 months.

title: Pappa al Pomodoro
description: Pappa al pomodoro is a simple yet sublime Tuscan soup that transforms stale bread and ripe tomatoes into a velvety, comforting dish. A staple of cucina povera (peasant cooking), it relies on top-quality ingredients — San Marzano tomatoes, fresh basil, and excellent extra virgin olive oil. The bread dissolves into the tomato broth, creating a thick, almost porridge-like consistency. Best served warm or at room temperature in summer, this dish celebrates the Tuscan philosophy of wasting nothing.

steps.instruction: Heat 60 ml of olive oil in a large heavy-bottomed pot over medium heat. Add the garlic and chilli flakes if using. Cook for 1-2 minutes until the garlic is golden but not burnt.
Add the crushed tomatoes and half the basil. Season with salt and pepper. Simmer for 15 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Add the torn bread and warm stock. Stir well, breaking up the bread with a wooden spoon. Cook for 10 minutes until the bread has completely dissolved into the soup.
Remove from heat. Stir vigorously to create a thick, creamy consistency. Adjust seasoning. Serve warm or at room temperature, topped with remaining basil and a generous drizzle of olive oil.

image_placeholder: A shallow white bowl of vibrant orange-red pappa al pomodoro garnished with fresh basil leaves and a swirl of green olive oil, on a sun-drenched terracotta tile surface.
wine_pairing_notes: A light Rosso di Montalcino or Morellino di Scansano complements the tomato base without overpowering it.
tips: The quality of olive oil makes or breaks this dish — use the best extra virgin you can afford.
Day-old bread works best; fresh bread will make the soup gummy rather than silky.
Can be served warm in winter or at room temperature in summer.

title: Bistecca alla Fiorentina
description: Bistecca alla fiorentina is the iconic T-bone steak of Florence, traditionally cut from Chianina beef and grilled over hot coals. The steak must be at least 4 cm thick and cooked rare to medium-rare — anything more is considered sacrilege in Tuscany. Seasoned simply with salt, pepper, and a drizzle of the finest extra virgin olive oil after resting, it showcases the pure quality of the meat. This is a centrepiece dish, often shared between two people, that embodies Tuscan simplicity at its finest.

ingredients.name: T-bone steak
ingredients.notes: at least 4 cm thick, at room temperature

steps.instruction: Remove the steak from the fridge at least 1 hour before cooking to bring it to room temperature. Pat dry with kitchen paper.
Heat a charcoal grill, griddle pan, or cast-iron skillet until extremely hot — you should not be able to hold your hand above it for more than 2 seconds.

Place the steak on the grill without any oil. Cook for 5 minutes on the first side without moving it. Flip and cook for 4 minutes on the second side for rare (internal temperature 48-50°C). Add 1-2 minutes per side for medium-rare (52-55°C).
Stand the steak on its bone edge for 1-2 minutes to render any fat near the bone.
Transfer to a warm plate and rest for 5-8 minutes. Season generously with flaky salt and pepper, then drizzle with olive oil. Serve with lemon wedges if desired.

image_placeholder: A massive T-bone steak resting on a wooden board, perfectly charred with a pink centre visible, drizzled with olive oil and scattered with flaky salt, rosemary sprigs alongside.
wine_pairing_notes: A bold Brunello di Montalcino or Vino Nobile di Montepulciano stands up to the richness of the beef.
tips: Never season with salt before grilling — it draws out moisture. Season only after resting.
Use a meat thermometer to get the perfect doneness: 48-50°C for rare, 52-55°C for medium-rare.
Charcoal or wood fire gives the most authentic flavour, but a ripping-hot cast-iron pan works at home.

title: Arancini
description: Arancini are iconic Sicilian street food — golden, crispy deep-fried rice balls filled with a rich ragù of beef and peas, and sometimes mozzarella. Their name means 'little oranges', referring to their shape and colour. In eastern Sicily (Catania), they are shaped as cones; in Palermo, as spheres. The rice is cooked risotto-style, enriched with Parmigiano-Reggiano and egg, then cooled, shaped around the filling, breaded, and fried until shatteringly crisp outside and molten inside.

steps.instruction: Make the ragù: heat olive oil in a saucepan, sauté the onion, carrot, and celery for 5 minutes. Add the minced beef and brown well. Pour in the passata, season, and simmer for 45 minutes until thick. Add the peas in the last 10 minutes. Cool completely.

Cook the rice: bring the stock to a simmer. Add the rice and cook, stirring occasionally, until all stock is absorbed and rice is tender (about 18 minutes). Remove from heat, stir in the Parmigiano and 3 beaten eggs. Spread on a tray and refrigerate for at least 2 hours.

Shape the arancini: take a handful of cooled rice (about 80g), flatten in your palm, place a spoonful of ragù and a cube of mozzarella in the centre, then close the rice around it forming a ball. Repeat to make about 12-14 arancini.
Set up a breading station: flour, beaten eggs, breadcrumbs. Roll each arancino in flour, then egg, then breadcrumbs, pressing gently to coat evenly.
Heat the vegetable oil to 170°C in a deep pan or fryer. Fry the arancini in batches for 4-5 minutes, turning occasionally, until deep golden. Drain on kitchen paper. Serve hot.

image_placeholder: A plate of golden, crispy arancini cut open to reveal the molten mozzarella and meat ragù filling, on a white plate with a sprig of basil.
wine_pairing_notes: A crisp Grillo or Catarratto from Sicily cuts through the richness of the fried rice.
tips: The rice must be completely cold and firm before shaping — refrigerate for at least 2 hours.
Fry in small batches to maintain oil temperature; too many at once makes them greasy.
Uncooked arancini freeze beautifully — fry straight from frozen, adding 1-2 minutes to cooking time.

title: Pasta alla Norma
description: Pasta alla Norma is Catania's most famous pasta dish, named after Vincenzo Bellini's opera 'Norma'. It combines fried aubergine with a garlicky tomato sauce and a generous shower of ricotta salata cheese. The aubergine is traditionally deep-fried until golden, giving it a rich, almost meaty quality that melds beautifully with the sweet-acidic sauce. It is a celebration of Sicilian summer produce at its peak.

substitutions.notes: feta is saltier, use slightly less

steps.instruction: Salt the aubergine cubes generously and place in a colander for 30 minutes to draw out excess moisture. Pat dry with kitchen paper.
Heat 50 ml of olive oil in a large frying pan over medium-high heat. Fry the aubergine in batches until golden on all sides, about 4-5 minutes per batch. Drain on kitchen paper.
In the same pan, add the remaining olive oil and garlic. Cook for 1 minute until fragrant. Add the crushed tomatoes and half the basil. Season and simmer for 15 minutes.
Cook the pasta in a large pot of well-salted boiling water until al dente. Reserve 100 ml of pasta water before draining.
Toss the drained pasta with the tomato sauce, adding pasta water as needed. Gently fold in the fried aubergine. Serve topped with grated ricotta salata and remaining basil.

image_placeholder: A plate of rigatoni alla Norma with golden fried aubergine, rich tomato sauce, and a generous snowfall of white ricotta salata, garnished with basil leaves.
wine_pairing_notes: An Etna Rosso or Nero d'Avola from Sicily mirrors the smoky aubergine and tomato.
tips: Salting the aubergine removes bitterness and excess water, ensuring it fries crisp rather than soggy.
Fry aubergine in batches — overcrowding the pan steams rather than fries.
If ricotta salata is hard to find, aged ricotta or even crumbled feta makes a decent substitute.

title: Caponata
title_en: Sicilian Sweet and Sour Aubergine Stew
description: Caponata is Sicily's beloved sweet and sour aubergine relish, a dish that traces its roots to Arab culinary influences on the island. Cubes of fried aubergine are combined with a soffritto of celery, onions, tomatoes, capers, and olives, finished with a splash of red wine vinegar and a touch of sugar. This agrodolce (sweet-sour) balance is its defining character. Served at room temperature as an antipasto or side, caponata improves significantly after resting overnight.

steps.instruction: Salt the aubergine cubes and leave in a colander for 20 minutes. Pat dry thoroughly.
Heat 70 ml of olive oil in a large frying pan over medium-high heat. Fry the aubergine in batches until golden, about 5 minutes per batch. Remove and drain on kitchen paper.
In the same pan, add the remaining oil. Cook the onion for 5 minutes until soft, then add the celery and cook for 3 minutes more.
Add the tomatoes, olives, and capers. Simmer for 10 minutes until the sauce thickens.
Dissolve the sugar in the red wine vinegar and add to the pan. Stir well and cook for 2 minutes.
Return the fried aubergine to the pan and stir gently to coat. Remove from heat. Allow to cool to room temperature. Serve garnished with basil and pine nuts.

image_placeholder: A rustic bowl of caponata with glistening aubergine cubes, green olives, and scattered pine nuts, served at room temperature with crusty bread alongside.
wine_pairing_notes: A dry Sicilian rosato or a crisp Zibibbo complements the sweet-sour profile.
tips: Caponata tastes best after resting overnight — the flavours meld and deepen beautifully.
Serve at room temperature on bruschetta, alongside grilled fish, or as part of an antipasti spread.
Keeps well in the fridge for up to 5 days; bring to room temperature before serving.

title: Tortellini in Brodo
description: Tortellini in brodo is one of the great culinary traditions of Bologna and the wider Emilia-Romagna region, served at Christmas and special celebrations. Small parcels of fresh egg pasta are filled with a mixture of pork loin, prosciutto di Parma, mortadella, Parmigiano-Reggiano, and nutmeg, then gently cooked in a rich capon or chicken broth. The broth is as important as the filling — it must be clear, deeply flavourful, and lovingly made from scratch.

substitutions.notes: slightly less silky dough but works

ingredients.name: Parmigiano-Reggiano
ingredients.notes: finely grated, plus extra for serving

substitutions.notes: use the best you can find

steps.instruction: Make the pasta dough: mound the flour on a work surface, make a well in the centre, and crack in the 4 eggs. Using a fork, gradually incorporate the flour into the eggs. Knead for 10 minutes until smooth and elastic. Wrap in cling film and rest for 30 minutes.

Make the filling: cook the pork loin in butter over low heat with a pinch of salt until cooked through, about 10 minutes. Cool. Place the pork, prosciutto, and mortadella in a food processor and pulse to a fine paste. Transfer to a bowl, add the egg, Parmigiano-Reggiano, nutmeg, and salt. Mix well. Refrigerate for at least 1 hour.

Roll the pasta to about 1-2 mm thick using a pasta machine (setting 4-5). Cut into 3 cm squares. Place a small amount of filling (about 3g) on each square. Fold in half diagonally to form a triangle, pressing edges to seal. Wrap around your finger, pressing the two corners together to form the classic tortellini shape.

Bring the broth to a gentle boil. Drop in the tortellini and cook for 2-4 minutes until they float to the surface and the pasta is tender. Serve immediately in warm bowls with the broth, topped with a sprinkling of Parmigiano-Reggiano.

image_placeholder: A wide white bowl of clear golden broth with delicate tortellini floating in it, finished with a dusting of Parmigiano-Reggiano, on a white linen tablecloth.
wine_pairing_notes: A dry Lambrusco di Sorbara (sparkling) is the traditional pairing from Emilia-Romagna.
tips: The filling must be highly seasoned — it needs to punch through the pasta and broth. Taste and adjust before shaping.
Tortellini can be frozen on a tray and stored in bags for up to 3 months. Cook from frozen, adding 1-2 minutes.
A quality homemade broth is essential — the dish is only as good as its broth.

title: Ragù alla Bolognese
description: The authentic ragù alla bolognese from Bologna is a world apart from the quick tomato-meat sauces often found outside Italy. According to the recipe registered with the Bologna Chamber of Commerce, it is a slow-cooked meat sauce built on a soffritto of onion, celery, and carrot, using a combination of beef and pork, simmered for hours with wine, tomato, and milk (or cream). The result is a deeply rich, meaty sauce with tomato playing a supporting role. Traditionally served with fresh tagliatelle, not spaghetti.

steps.instruction: Heat olive oil and butter in a heavy-bottomed pot or Dutch oven over medium heat. Add the pancetta and cook until the fat renders, about 3 minutes.
Add the onion, carrot, and celery. Cook gently for 8-10 minutes until soft and translucent.
Increase heat to medium-high. Add the minced beef and pork. Break up with a wooden spoon and cook until browned, about 8 minutes.
Pour in the wine and stir, scraping the bottom of the pot. Let it bubble until almost fully evaporated.
Add the milk and nutmeg. Simmer gently until the milk is mostly absorbed.

Add the tomato passata, tomato purée, and bay leaf. Stir, then reduce heat to the lowest setting. Partially cover and simmer for a minimum of 2 hours (ideally 3-4 hours), stirring occasionally. Add stock if the sauce becomes too thick.
Season with salt and pepper. Remove bay leaf. Serve tossed with fresh tagliatelle and topped with Parmigiano-Reggiano.

image_placeholder: A plate of fresh tagliatelle ribbons generously coated with rich, glossy ragù alla bolognese, finished with shaved Parmigiano-Reggiano on a rustic Italian ceramic plate.
wine_pairing_notes: A medium-bodied Sangiovese di Romagna or Lambrusco pairs beautifully with the rich meat sauce.
tips: The longer you simmer, the better it gets — 3-4 hours is ideal. The sauce should be rich and thick, not watery.
Adding milk before the tomato is traditional and tenderises the meat, giving the sauce a velvety texture.
Serve with fresh tagliatelle or pappardelle, never spaghetti. The broad flat pasta holds the sauce far better.

title: Piadina Romagnola
description: Piadina is the beloved flatbread of Romagna, a thin unleavened bread traditionally cooked on a hot terracotta plate (testo). Made from a simple dough of flour, lard (or olive oil), salt, and water, it is rolled thin and cooked quickly on a hot griddle. Served folded around fillings such as prosciutto, squacquerone cheese, and rocket, it is Romagna's answer to the sandwich. Quick to make, deeply satisfying, and infinitely customisable.

substitutions.notes: makes it suitable for those avoiding pork
not identical but a good substitute

steps.instruction: Combine the flour, salt, and bicarbonate of soda in a bowl. Rub in the lard with your fingertips until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs. Add warm water gradually, mixing until a soft, smooth dough forms. Knead for 3-4 minutes. Wrap and rest for 30 minutes.
Divide the dough into 4 equal pieces. Roll each piece out on a lightly floured surface to a thin round disc, about 25-28 cm diameter and 2-3 mm thick.

Heat a dry cast-iron griddle or heavy frying pan over medium-high heat until very hot. Cook each piadina for 2-3 minutes per side, pressing down with a spatula to ensure even cooking. It should develop golden-brown spots.
While still warm, fill each piadina with prosciutto, squacquerone, and rocket. Fold in half and serve immediately.

image_placeholder: A folded golden-spotted piadina filled with pink prosciutto, creamy white squacquerone cheese, and green rocket, on a marble board with a glass of white wine.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Albana di Romagna or a light Sangiovese rosato is the local choice.
tips: The dough should be soft but not sticky. Add water gradually — you may not need all 150 ml.
Roll the piadina thin for a crispier result, thicker for a softer, more bread-like texture.
Unfilled piadine can be stacked between greaseproof paper and frozen for up to 2 months.

title: Pizza Margherita
description: Pizza Margherita is the quintessential Neapolitan pizza, created (as legend has it) in 1889 by pizzaiolo Raffaele Esposito to honour Queen Margherita of Italy, with its colours — red tomato, white mozzarella, and green basil — mirroring the Italian flag. A true Neapolitan Margherita has a thin, soft, slightly charred crust with a puffy cornicione (rim), San Marzano tomato sauce, fresh fior di latte or buffalo mozzarella, basil, and extra virgin olive oil. Baked at extremely high temperatures, ideally in a wood-fired oven.

substitutions.notes: more traditional but wetter; drain well

steps.instruction: Dissolve the yeast in the warm water. In a large bowl, combine the flour and salt. Add the yeast water and mix until a shaggy dough forms. Turn out and knead for 10-12 minutes until smooth, elastic, and slightly tacky.
Place the dough in a lightly oiled bowl, cover with cling film, and leave to rise at room temperature for 8-24 hours (a longer, slower rise develops better flavour).
Divide the dough into 4 equal balls (about 210g each). Place on a floured tray, cover, and rest for 1-2 hours until puffy.
Preheat oven to its maximum temperature (250-300°C) with a baking stone or steel inside for at least 30 minutes. Season the crushed tomatoes with a pinch of salt.
On a floured surface, press each dough ball out from the centre, leaving a thicker rim. Stretch to about 25-30 cm diameter. Do not use a rolling pin.

Spread a thin layer of tomato on each base (don't overload). Transfer to the hot stone or steel. Bake for 5-7 minutes until the crust puffs and chars in spots. Remove, add torn mozzarella, basil, and a drizzle of olive oil. Return to oven for 2-3 minutes until cheese melts.

image_placeholder: A classic Neapolitan pizza Margherita with charred, puffy crust, vibrant red tomato sauce, melted white mozzarella, and bright green basil leaves, on a wooden pizza peel.
wine_pairing_notes: A light Lacryma Christi del Vesuvio or a cold Peroni beer from Campania.
tips: A long, slow fermentation (12-24 hours at room temperature) produces a lighter, more digestible, and more flavourful dough.
If you don't have a pizza stone, an inverted heavy baking tray preheated at maximum works surprisingly well.
Less is more with toppings — overloading makes the base soggy. Use just enough sauce to thinly coat.

title: Spaghetti alle Vongole
description: Spaghetti alle vongole is one of the great seafood pastas of Naples and the Campania coast, a dish of elemental simplicity and incredible depth of flavour. Fresh clams are steamed open in white wine and garlic, their juices forming a briny, savoury sauce that clings to spaghetti. The 'in bianco' (white) version omits tomato, letting the pure shellfish flavour shine. It demands only a handful of quality ingredients and a light, confident touch.

ingredients.name: fresh clams (vongole veraci)
ingredients.notes: scrubbed clean, discard any open ones

steps.instruction: Soak the clams in cold salted water for 30 minutes to purge any sand. Drain and rinse.
Bring a large pot of well-salted water to the boil. Add the spaghetti and cook until 2 minutes less than al dente. Reserve 200 ml of pasta water before draining.
Meanwhile, heat the olive oil in a large pan or wide-bottomed pot over medium heat. Add the garlic and chilli flakes. Cook for 30 seconds until fragrant but not browned.
Add the clams and immediately pour in the white wine. Cover with a lid and cook over high heat for 3-4 minutes, shaking the pan occasionally, until the clams open. Discard any that remain closed.

Add the drained spaghetti to the clam pan along with a splash of pasta water. Toss vigorously over high heat for 2 minutes until the pasta finishes cooking and is coated in the sauce. Add parsley, toss once more, and serve immediately.

image_placeholder: A twirl of spaghetti alle vongole in a deep white bowl, open clam shells scattered throughout, glistening with olive oil and flecked with parsley.
wine_pairing_notes: A crisp Falanghina or Fiano di Avellino from Campania is the classic match.
tips: Never add cheese to seafood pasta — it's considered a culinary sin in Italy.
Soak clams to purge sand, but discard any that are open before cooking and any that stay closed after.
Finishing the pasta in the clam sauce (rather than just topping) allows it to absorb the briny liquid.

title: Sfogliatella
description: Sfogliatella is Naples' most iconic pastry, a crisp, shell-shaped masterpiece of impossibly thin, layered dough filled with a sweet ricotta and semolina cream scented with candied orange and cinnamon. The 'riccia' (curly) version has shatteringly crisp, flaky layers, while the 'frolla' version uses shortcrust. Making sfogliatelle ricce at home is a serious undertaking — stretching the dough paper-thin, brushing with lard, and rolling into a tight cylinder that is then sliced and shaped. The reward is one of Italy's finest pastries.

substitutions.notes: less authentic but works for those avoiding pork

steps.instruction: Make the dough: combine flour, salt, 30g sugar, and warm water. Knead for 10 minutes until very smooth and elastic. Cover and rest for 2 hours.

Make the filling: bring milk to a simmer, add semolina while whisking constantly. Cook for 3-4 minutes until thick. Remove from heat, stir in 80g sugar, ricotta, egg yolks, candied orange, cinnamon, and vanilla. Cool completely.

Roll the dough through a pasta machine, progressively thinning to the thinnest setting until nearly transparent. Brush the stretched sheet with softened lard and roll into a tight cylinder. Wrap in cling film and refrigerate for at least 1 hour.
Cut the cylinder into 1.5 cm thick discs. Place each disc cut-side up and press your thumbs into the centre, gently stretching outward to form a cone shape with visible layers.
Fill each cone with the ricotta-semolina mixture and press the edges to seal, creating a shell shape.
Preheat oven to 200°C. Place sfogliatelle on a lined baking tray. Bake for 20-25 minutes until deep golden and crisp. Dust with icing sugar while still warm.

image_placeholder: Golden, shell-shaped sfogliatelle dusted with icing sugar, one broken open to reveal the creamy ricotta and orange filling, on a marble pastry counter.
tips: The dough must be stretched paper-thin — if you can't read a newspaper through it, keep rolling.
This is a challenging recipe best attempted after watching video tutorials of the shaping technique.
Sfogliatelle are best eaten within hours of baking; the layers lose their crunch over time.

title: Insalata di Burrata
title_en: Burrata Salad with Tomatoes and Basil
description: This modern Italian salad showcases burrata — the luxurious Puglian cheese with a mozzarella shell and a creamy, stracciatella-filled centre. Set atop ripe summer tomatoes and fresh basil, dressed simply with the finest extra virgin olive oil and flaky salt, it is a masterclass in Italian simplicity. The key is impeccable ingredients: the sweetest tomatoes, room-temperature burrata, and a peppery olive oil. Popular across Italy's modern trattorias, this elegant starter comes together in minutes.

steps.instruction: Arrange the sliced tomatoes on a serving plate. Season with flaky salt and pepper.
Place the burrata in the centre of the tomatoes. Tear open the top with your hands to let the creamy stracciatella spill out.
Scatter basil leaves over the plate. Drizzle generously with olive oil and, if desired, a few drops of aged balsamic. Serve immediately.

image_placeholder: A whole burrata torn open in the centre of a colourful tomato salad on a white plate, with basil leaves, olive oil glistening, on a sunlit terrace table.
wine_pairing_notes: A dry Puglian rosato or a crisp Vermentino is ideal.
tips: Burrata must be at room temperature — cold burrata has less flavour and a less creamy texture.
This dish is only as good as its ingredients: use the ripest, sweetest tomatoes you can find.
Serve immediately; burrata begins to lose its creamy centre once cut.

title: Risotto al Tartufo
description: Truffle risotto is one of Piedmont's most luxurious dishes, a creamy, gently flavoured risotto finished with generous shavings of truffle. Made with Carnaroli rice, good stock, white wine, butter, and Parmigiano-Reggiano, the base risotto is intentionally subtle — a canvas for the intoxicating aroma and earthy depth of truffle. Fresh truffles (white Alba in season, or black truffles) are shaved over the top just before serving. Truffle oil or paste can substitute for fresh truffles on a budget.

substitutions.notes: slightly less creamy but widely available

ingredients.name: fresh truffle
ingredients.notes: shaved, or 2 tsp truffle paste as substitute

substitutions.ratio: 2 tsp paste or a few drops oil
substitutions.notes: far less complex but adds truffle flavour on a budget

steps.instruction: Keep the stock warm in a saucepan over low heat.
Heat olive oil and 30g butter in a heavy-bottomed pan over medium heat. Add the shallot and cook gently for 3-4 minutes until translucent.
Add the rice and toast for 2 minutes, stirring to coat each grain in fat. The rice should become slightly translucent at the edges.
Pour in the white wine and stir until fully absorbed.

Add the warm stock one ladleful at a time, stirring frequently. Wait until each addition is mostly absorbed before adding the next. Continue for 16-18 minutes until the rice is al dente and the risotto is creamy.
Remove from heat. Add the remaining 30g butter and the Parmigiano-Reggiano. Stir vigorously (mantecatura) for 1 minute until the risotto is glossy and flowing. Season with salt and white pepper.
Let the risotto rest for 1-2 minutes, then serve in warm bowls. Shave fresh truffle generously over the top just before serving.

image_placeholder: A wide shallow bowl of creamy, pale risotto with paper-thin shavings of white truffle piled on top, on a dark tablecloth with a truffle shaver beside it.
wine_pairing_notes: A Barolo or Barbaresco from Piedmont is the classic pairing for truffle dishes.
tips: Mantecatura (vigorous stirring off heat with butter and cheese) is the secret to a creamy, flowing risotto — don't skip it.
Fresh truffle is shaved at the table just before eating to preserve its volatile aroma.
If using truffle oil, add only a few drops — it can easily overpower the dish.

title: Ossobuco alla Milanese
description: Ossobuco alla milanese is Milan's signature slow-braised dish — thick cross-cut slices of veal shank cooked in white wine, broth, and vegetables until the meat falls from the bone and the marrow becomes butter-soft. It is traditionally finished with gremolata, a vibrant mixture of lemon zest, garlic, and parsley that cuts through the richness. The word 'ossobuco' means 'bone with a hole' — the marrow-filled centre is considered the greatest prize. Traditionally served with risotto alla milanese (saffron risotto).

substitutions.notes: beef works but is less delicate than veal

ingredients.name: garlic clove
ingredients.notes: 1 for braise, 1 finely minced for gremolata

steps.instruction: Tie kitchen string around each ossobuco to keep its shape. Season with salt and pepper, then dredge lightly in flour, shaking off excess.
Heat olive oil and butter in a large heavy casserole over medium-high heat. Brown the ossobuco for 3-4 minutes on each side until deep golden. Remove and set aside.
Reduce heat to medium. Add the onion, carrot, celery, and garlic. Cook for 5 minutes until softened.
Pour in the wine and stir, scraping up browned bits from the bottom. Let it reduce by half.

Add the tomatoes, stock, and bay leaf. Return the ossobuco to the pot. The liquid should come halfway up the shanks. Bring to a gentle simmer, cover, and cook in a 160°C oven or on the lowest hob setting for 2-2.5 hours, turning once, until the meat is fork-tender.
Make the gremolata: combine the finely minced garlic, lemon zest, and chopped parsley.
Serve the ossobuco in warm bowls with the braising liquid spooned over, topped with gremolata. Serve alongside risotto alla milanese.

image_placeholder: A plate of ossobuco alla milanese with rich braising sauce, marrow visible in the bone, topped with bright green gremolata, alongside golden saffron risotto.
wine_pairing_notes: A refined Nebbiolo or a full-bodied Barbera d'Asti from Piedmont or Lombardy.
tips: Tie the ossobuco with string to prevent the meat from falling off the bone during the long braise.
The gremolata should be added at the very end — its fresh, zippy flavour provides essential contrast to the rich braise.
The marrow is the best part — provide small spoons or toast points for guests to scoop it out.

title: Ratatouille
description: Ratatouille is the quintessential vegetable dish of Provence, a celebration of summer produce — aubergines, courgettes, peppers, tomatoes, and onions — slowly cooked in olive oil with garlic and herbes de Provence. Traditionally, each vegetable is cooked separately to preserve its distinct character before being combined and gently simmered together. The result is a deeply flavourful, oil-rich vegetable stew that can be served hot, warm, or at room temperature as a main course, side dish, or filling for crêpes.

substitutions.notes: fresh herbs give a brighter flavour

steps.instruction: Heat 20 ml of olive oil in a large frying pan over medium-high heat. Cook the aubergine cubes in batches until golden on all sides, about 5-6 minutes. Remove and drain on kitchen paper. Repeat with courgettes.
Add another 20 ml of oil to the pan. Cook the peppers for 5 minutes until softened. Remove and set aside.
In a large heavy casserole, heat the remaining olive oil over medium heat. Cook the onions for 8 minutes until soft and translucent. Add the garlic and cook for 1 minute.
Add the tomatoes, herbes de Provence, and bay leaves. Cook for 10 minutes until the tomatoes break down.
Add all the cooked vegetables back to the casserole. Stir gently, season with salt and pepper. Cover and simmer on the lowest heat for 30-40 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Remove bay leaves. Adjust seasoning. Serve warm or at room temperature, garnished with torn basil.

image_placeholder: A rustic earthenware dish of vibrant ratatouille with glistening aubergine, courgette, and peppers in a rich tomato sauce, garnished with basil, on a Provençal tablecloth.
wine_pairing_notes: A dry Provence rosé or a light Côtes du Rhône red complements the Mediterranean vegetables.
tips: Cooking each vegetable separately before combining gives a far better result than throwing everything in together.
Ratatouille improves overnight as flavours meld — make it a day ahead for best results.
Serve as a side with grilled meat or fish, as a filling for omelettes, or on its own with crusty bread.

title: Bouillabaisse
description: Bouillabaisse is the legendary fisherman's stew of Marseille, a fragrant, saffron-infused broth teeming with mixed Mediterranean fish and shellfish. Originally a humble dish made from the catch that couldn't be sold, it has evolved into one of France's most celebrated dishes. Authentic bouillabaisse is served in two courses: first the broth with rouille-spread toasts, then the fish arranged on a platter. The key aromatics are saffron, fennel, orange peel, and pastis, giving the stew its unique Provençal character.

ingredients.name: mixed firm white fish fillets
ingredients.notes: such as monkfish, sea bass, John Dory, red mullet — cut into large chunks

steps.instruction: Heat olive oil in a very large heavy pot over medium heat. Add onions, leek, and fennel. Cook for 10 minutes until softened.
Add the garlic, tomatoes, and tomato purée. Cook for 5 minutes. Add the pastis if using and let it bubble for 1 minute.
Add the fish stock, saffron, orange peel, bay leaves, thyme, and potatoes. Bring to a boil, then simmer for 15 minutes until the potatoes are nearly tender.

Add the firmest fish pieces first (monkfish). After 3 minutes, add the remaining fish, prawns, and mussels. Cover and cook for 5-6 minutes until the mussels open and fish is cooked through. Discard any unopened mussels.
Carefully transfer the fish and shellfish to a warm platter. Ladle the broth into warm bowls with the potatoes. Serve with toasted baguette spread with rouille alongside the fish platter.

image_placeholder: A large terracotta bowl of golden saffron-infused bouillabaisse broth with whole prawns, mussels, and fish visible, with toasted bread and rouille on the side.
wine_pairing_notes: A white Cassis or a Bandol rosé from Provence are traditional pairings.
tips: Use at least 3-4 types of fish for complexity — a mix of firm and delicate fish is ideal.
Saffron, fennel, and orange peel are the three signature flavours — don't skip any of them.
Make rouille (saffron-garlic mayonnaise) separately to spread on croutons for the full experience.

title: Tapenade
description: Tapenade is a punchy, briny Provençal spread made from black olives, capers, anchovies, garlic, and olive oil, blitzed to a coarse paste. Its name derives from 'tapeno', the Provençal word for capers. Traditionally served as part of an apéritif spread with crudités, crackers, or on toasted bread, it captures the intense flavours of the Mediterranean in every bite. Quick to make and endlessly versatile, it also makes a superb condiment for grilled meat and fish.

substitutions.notes: for a milder, tangier tapenade verte
for vegetarian version, adds similar umami

steps.instruction: Place the olives, capers, anchovies, garlic, and lemon juice in a food processor.
Pulse several times until you have a coarse paste — do not over-process, it should have texture.
With the motor running, drizzle in the olive oil until just combined. Season with pepper and thyme if using. Taste — it likely won't need salt due to the olives, capers, and anchovies.

image_placeholder: A small rustic bowl of dark tapenade with a drizzle of olive oil on top, surrounded by toasted bread slices and crudités on a wooden board.
tips: Keep the texture coarse — tapenade should have body, not be a smooth purée.
Stores well in the fridge for up to 2 weeks, covered with a thin layer of olive oil.
Use as a spread on bruschetta, a sauce for pasta, or a condiment alongside grilled lamb.

title: Moules Marinière
description: Moules marinière is a classic French dish of plump mussels steamed open in a fragrant broth of white wine, shallots, garlic, parsley, and butter. Originating from the Atlantic coast — particularly Normandy and Brittany — it is one of the simplest yet most satisfying seafood dishes in the French repertoire. The mussels release their briny liquor into the wine, creating a light but intensely flavoured sauce perfect for soaking up with crusty bread. From pot to table in under 15 minutes.

ingredients.name: live mussels
ingredients.notes: scrubbed and debearded, discard any that are open

substitutions.notes: traditional Norman alternative — moules au cidre

ingredients.name: double cream
ingredients.notes: optional, for moules à la crème variation

steps.instruction: Melt the butter in a very large pot with a lid over medium heat. Add the shallots and cook for 3-4 minutes until soft. Add the garlic and cook for 30 seconds.
Pour in the wine and bring to a boil. Tip in all the mussels, cover with the lid, and cook over high heat for 3-4 minutes, shaking the pot vigorously once or twice.
Remove the lid — the mussels should be open. Discard any that remain closed. If adding cream, stir it in now and let the sauce bubble for 1 minute.
Scatter with chopped parsley and pepper. Serve immediately in large bowls with the broth and plenty of crusty bread.

image_placeholder: A large black pot overflowing with open mussels in a golden wine-butter broth, scattered with parsley, with crusty bread on the side.
wine_pairing_notes: A crisp Muscadet-Sèvre et Maine or a dry Norman cider are the classic pairings.
tips: Discard any mussels that are open before cooking and any that remain closed after — they may be dead.
Don't overcook — mussels become rubbery if cooked too long. As soon as they open, they're done.
Use the empty mussel shells as tweezers to pluck the meat from other shells — that's the traditional way.

title: Tarte Tatin
description: Tarte Tatin is the famous upside-down apple tart attributed to the Tatin sisters at their hotel in Lamotte-Beuvron, in the Sologne region. Apples are caramelised in butter and sugar in a skillet, topped with a round of puff pastry, baked until golden, then dramatically flipped onto a plate. The result is a gorgeous tangle of deeply caramelised, amber-coloured apples atop crisp, buttery pastry. Served warm with a dollop of crème fraîche, it is one of France's greatest desserts.

ingredients.name: dessert apples
ingredients.notes: such as Braeburn or Cox, peeled, cored, quartered

steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 200°C. Place the butter and sugar in a 24-26 cm ovenproof frying pan or tarte tatin tin over medium heat. Stir until the butter melts and the sugar dissolves, then cook without stirring for 5-8 minutes until it turns a deep amber caramel.

Remove from heat. Arrange the apple quarters tightly in the caramel, standing upright in concentric circles. Squeeze lemon juice over and add vanilla if using. Return to medium heat and cook for 15 minutes until the apples begin to soften and the caramel deepens.
Cut the puff pastry into a round slightly larger than the pan. Lay it over the apples, tucking the edges down around the fruit. Prick a few holes with a fork.
Bake for 25-30 minutes until the pastry is risen, golden brown, and crisp.
Let the tart cool in the pan for 5 minutes. Place a serving plate over the pan and, in one confident motion, flip it over. Tap the bottom and lift the pan away. Serve warm with crème fraîche.

image_placeholder: A golden tarte Tatin on a white plate showing deeply caramelised apple quarters in a glossy amber sauce atop puff pastry, with a dollop of crème fraîche.
tips: Don't be afraid of a dark caramel — the apples need a deep amber colour for the best flavour.
The flip is the dramatic moment — commit to a single confident motion. Hesitation leads to mess.
Best served within 2 hours of baking; the pastry softens as it sits.

title: Poulet Vallée d'Auge
title_en: Chicken with Cream, Apples, and Calvados
description: Poulet vallée d'Auge is a classic Norman dish combining chicken with the region's three most famous products: cream, apples, and Calvados (apple brandy). The chicken is browned, then braised with shallots and mushrooms, deglazed with Calvados (often flambéed), and finished with double cream. Sautéed apple slices are added at the end for sweetness and texture. It is the quintessence of Norman cuisine — rich, autumnal, and deeply comforting.

steps.instruction: Season the chicken pieces well with salt and pepper. Heat 30g butter in a large casserole over medium-high heat. Brown the chicken on all sides, about 8 minutes total. Remove and set aside.
Reduce heat to medium. Add the shallots and cook for 3 minutes. Add the mushrooms and cook for 5 minutes until golden. Add the thyme.
Pour in the Calvados. If desired, carefully flambé by tilting the pan towards the flame (or ignite with a match). Let the flames die down. Add the cider and bring to a simmer.
Return the chicken to the pot. Cover and simmer gently for 30 minutes until the chicken is cooked through.
Meanwhile, heat the remaining butter in a frying pan and sauté the apple wedges for 3-4 minutes until golden and just tender.
Remove the chicken from the pot. Stir the double cream into the sauce and simmer for 5 minutes until thickened. Return the chicken and add the sautéed apples. Serve.

image_placeholder: A cast-iron casserole with golden chicken pieces in a cream sauce with sautéed apple wedges and mushrooms, thyme sprigs visible, on a rustic Norman kitchen table.
wine_pairing_notes: A dry Norman cider or a white Burgundy (Meursault) matches the cream and apple beautifully.
tips: Flambéing the Calvados burns off the alcohol and leaves behind a concentrated apple flavour.
Use a good-quality dry cider, not sweet — it should add acidity, not sweetness.
Serve with steamed potatoes, rice, or fresh tagliatelle to soak up the cream sauce.

title: Coq au Vin
description: Coq au vin is one of France's most iconic dishes, a slow braise of chicken in red Burgundy wine with lardons, mushrooms, and pearl onions. Originally made with an old rooster (coq), today's versions use chicken but retain the long, gentle cooking that produces fall-off-the-bone tender meat in a deeply flavourful, wine-dark sauce. The chicken is often marinated overnight in the wine before braising. Finished with a beurre manié to thicken the sauce, it is the ultimate French comfort food.

steps.instruction: Season the chicken with salt and pepper. Heat olive oil in a large Dutch oven over medium-high heat. Brown the chicken in batches until golden on all sides, about 5 minutes per batch. Remove and set aside.
In the same pot, cook the lardons until crisp. Add the chopped onion and carrots, cook for 5 minutes. Add the garlic and cook for 30 seconds.
Pour in the Cognac and scrape the bottom of the pot. Add the wine, stock, tomato purée, bay leaf, and thyme. Return the chicken. Bring to a simmer, cover, and cook gently for 45 minutes.
Meanwhile, heat 10g butter in a pan. Sauté the mushrooms until golden, about 5 minutes. Cook the pearl onions in the same pan until browned, about 8 minutes.
Remove the chicken. Make a beurre manié by mashing 30g soft butter with 30g flour. Whisk into the sauce in small pieces. Simmer for 5 minutes until the sauce thickens.
Return the chicken, add mushrooms and pearl onions. Simmer gently for 10 minutes. Adjust seasoning and serve.

image_placeholder: A cast-iron pot of coq au vin with deep burgundy sauce, golden chicken pieces, pearl onions, mushrooms, and thyme, with a bottle of Burgundy wine in the background.
wine_pairing_notes: A Burgundy Pinot Noir — ideally the same wine used in the dish — is the classic pairing.
tips: Marinating the chicken in wine overnight makes a noticeable difference in flavour and tenderness.
Use a wine you'd actually drink — the sauce concentrates its flavour, so poor wine makes a poor dish.
Like most braises, coq au vin is even better reheated the next day.

title: Quenelles de Brochet
description: Quenelles de brochet are a signature dish of Lyon, light and airy dumplings made from pike fish, butter, eggs, and a panade (flour-based paste), poached and then baked in a rich Nantua sauce (cream-based crayfish sauce). The quenelle mixture — essentially a fish mousseline — must be incredibly smooth and delicate, creating dumplings that are pillowy and almost mousse-like inside. A speciality of Lyonnaise bouchons and a dish that requires considerable skill to master.

ingredients.name: pike fillets
ingredients.notes: skinned and boned, or use a mix of pike and whiting

substitutions.notes: pike is traditional but hard to find; whiting works well

steps.instruction: Make the panade: bring milk and 30g butter to a boil in a saucepan. Add the flour all at once, stirring vigorously with a wooden spoon until the mixture forms a ball and pulls away from the sides. Cook for 1-2 minutes. Spread on a plate and cool completely.

Blend the pike in a food processor until very smooth. Add the cooled panade, remaining softened butter, eggs (one at a time), 100ml cream, nutmeg, salt, and white pepper. Process until completely smooth and light. Refrigerate for 2 hours.

Shape the quenelles using two large spoons dipped in hot water, forming rugby-ball shapes. Gently lower into a wide pan of barely simmering salted water. Poach for 10-12 minutes, turning once, until they float and feel firm.
Make the sauce: in a saucepan, combine 200ml cream and tomato purée. Simmer for 5 minutes until slightly thickened. Season with salt and pepper.
Place the poached quenelles in a buttered gratin dish. Pour the sauce over, sprinkle with Gruyère. Bake at 200°C for 15-20 minutes until golden and bubbling.

image_placeholder: A gratin dish with three golden-topped quenelles in a creamy pink Nantua sauce, bubbling at the edges, on a white-tablecloth Lyonnaise bouchon table.
wine_pairing_notes: A white Burgundy (Saint-Véran or Pouilly-Fuissé) or a Viognier from the Northern Rhône.
tips: The fish must be blended until extremely smooth — any fibres will ruin the delicate texture.
Chilling the mixture well before shaping makes the quenelles much easier to form.
The traditional Nantua sauce uses crayfish butter; this simplified version with cream and tomato is more accessible.

title: Salade Lyonnaise
title_en: Lyonnaise Salad with Poached Egg and Lardons
description: Salade lyonnaise is the iconic salad of Lyon, combining frisée lettuce with crispy lardons, a runny poached egg, and a warm mustardy vinaigrette. When the egg is broken, the yolk becomes part of the dressing, enriching and binding the salad. Crunchy croutons add textural contrast. It is a beloved starter in Lyonnaise bouchons and embodies the city's reputation as the gastronomic capital of France — hearty, flavourful, and elegant in its simplicity.

ingredients.name: frisée lettuce
ingredients.notes: pale inner leaves, washed and dried

substitutions.substitute: mixed salad leaves including some bitter leaves
substitutions.notes: frisée is traditional for its bitter crunch

steps.instruction: Fry the bread cubes in a little olive oil until golden and crispy. Set aside. In the same pan, cook the lardons until crisp and golden, about 5 minutes.
Make the dressing: whisk together the red wine vinegar, Dijon mustard, and olive oil. Season with salt and pepper. Warm the dressing in the lardon pan to deglaze.

Poach the eggs: bring a pot of water to a gentle simmer and add white wine vinegar. Create a gentle whirlpool and crack each egg in one at a time. Poach for 3 minutes until the white is set and yolk is runny. Remove with a slotted spoon.
Toss the frisée with the warm dressing and lardons. Divide among plates. Top each with a poached egg and croutons. Grind pepper over the top. Serve immediately.

image_placeholder: A plate of frisée salad with golden lardons, crunchy croutons, and a perfect poached egg on top with yolk just starting to run, on a bistro checked tablecloth.
wine_pairing_notes: A Beaujolais (Fleurie or Brouilly) or a dry white Mâcon-Villages.
tips: The fresher the eggs, the neater the poached egg — the whites hold together better.
Warm the dressing slightly in the lardon pan to wilt the frisée just a little and pick up the fond.
Break the yolk at the table and toss through the salad — it becomes the richest part of the dressing.

title: Croque-Monsieur
title_en: French Ham and Cheese Toasted Sandwich
description: The croque-monsieur is a Parisian café staple — an indulgent toasted sandwich of ham and Gruyère between slices of white bread, coated with creamy béchamel sauce, and grilled until golden and bubbling. First appearing on Parisian café menus around 1910, it has become one of France's most beloved quick lunches. The béchamel — both inside and on top — distinguishes it from an ordinary ham and cheese toastie. Add a fried egg on top and it becomes a croque-madame.

steps.instruction: Make the béchamel: melt butter in a saucepan over medium heat, add the flour, and whisk for 1 minute. Gradually add the milk, whisking constantly to avoid lumps. Cook for 5 minutes until thick and smooth. Season with nutmeg, salt, and pepper.
Lightly butter one side of each bread slice. Spread the unbuttered side of 4 slices with Dijon mustard. Layer with ham and half the Gruyère. Top with the remaining bread slices, buttered side out.
Spread a generous layer of béchamel over the top of each sandwich. Sprinkle with the remaining Gruyère.
Place under a hot grill (or in a 220°C oven) for 5-8 minutes until the top is golden, bubbling, and the cheese is melted through. Serve immediately.

image_placeholder: A golden, bubbling croque-monsieur on a café plate with a side salad of dressed frisée, cutlery alongside, on a marble bistro table.
tips: The béchamel is what elevates this beyond a normal toastie — don't skip it.
For a croque-madame, top with a fried egg just before serving.
Use good-quality ham and proper Gruyère or Comté for the best result.

title: Soupe à l'Oignon
description: French onion soup is a beloved Parisian classic — deeply caramelised onions simmered in rich beef broth, ladled into oven-proof bowls, topped with a crouton and a thick blanket of melted Gruyère, then grilled until golden and bubbling. The magic lies in patient, unhurried caramelisation of the onions, which can take 45 minutes or more. Traditionally served in the early morning hours at Les Halles market to warm workers, it has become one of France's most famous soups worldwide.

steps.instruction: Melt the butter with the olive oil in a large heavy-bottomed pot over medium heat. Add the sliced onions and a pinch of salt. Cook, stirring occasionally, for 45-50 minutes until deep golden brown and very soft. Add the sugar halfway through to help caramelisation.
Pour in the wine, scraping up any caramelised bits from the bottom. Cook for 3 minutes until mostly evaporated.
Add the stock, thyme, and bay leaf. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a gentle simmer for 20 minutes. Season with salt and pepper.
Preheat the grill to high. Ladle the soup into ovenproof bowls. Top each with toasted baguette rounds and a generous layer of grated Gruyère.
Place the bowls under the grill for 3-5 minutes until the cheese is melted, golden, and bubbling. Serve immediately — the bowls will be very hot.

image_placeholder: An ovenproof bowl of French onion soup with a thick golden crust of melted Gruyère overflowing the edges, on a dark wooden table with a ladle and bread basket.
wine_pairing_notes: A dry white Burgundy (Chablis) or a light red Beaujolais.
tips: Patience is everything — don't rush the onion caramelisation. Low and slow for at least 45 minutes gives the deepest flavour.
Use a mix of yellow and white onions for the best balance of sweetness and flavour.
The soup (without cheese topping) freezes brilliantly for up to 3 months.

title: Steak-Frites
description: Steak-frites is the quintessential French bistro dish — a perfectly cooked steak (usually entrecôte or bavette) served with golden, twice-fried chips and a compound butter or simple pan sauce. In Paris, this is comfort food at its finest, found in every neighbourhood bistro. The steak should have a well-developed crust and a rosy pink interior. The chips, cooked twice for maximum crispness, are the essential companion. Simple, brilliant, and deeply satisfying.

ingredients.name: entrecôte steaks
ingredients.notes: about 250g each, 2-3 cm thick, at room temperature

substitutions.notes: more flavourful, needs to be sliced against the grain

ingredients.name: floury potatoes
ingredients.notes: Maris Piper or King Edward, peeled, cut into 1 cm thick chips

ingredients.name: unsalted butter
ingredients.notes: 30g for cooking, 30g softened for maître d'hôtel butter

ingredients.name: fresh flat-leaf parsley
ingredients.notes: finely chopped, for maître d'hôtel butter

steps.instruction: Make the maître d'hôtel butter: mix 30g softened butter with the parsley, lemon juice, and a pinch of salt. Roll in cling film into a log and refrigerate.
First fry the chips: heat the oil to 130°C. Blanch the chips for 6-8 minutes until cooked through but not coloured. Remove and drain on kitchen paper.
Increase the oil temperature to 180°C. Fry the chips a second time for 3-4 minutes until golden and crispy. Drain and season with salt immediately.

Meanwhile, heat a heavy frying pan or griddle until very hot. Season the steaks generously with salt and pepper. Add olive oil and 30g butter to the pan. Cook the steaks for 3 minutes on the first side, 2-3 minutes on the second for medium-rare (55°C internal).
Rest the steaks on a warm plate for 3-4 minutes. Top each with a slice of maître d'hôtel butter and serve with the hot chips.

image_placeholder: A perfect medium-rare steak with a glistening pat of herbed butter melting on top, beside a heap of golden crispy fries, on a white bistro plate with a glass of red wine.
wine_pairing_notes: A Bordeaux (Saint-Émilion or Médoc) or a Côtes du Rhône red.
tips: Twice-frying the chips is the secret to crispness — the first fry cooks them through, the second crisps the exterior.
Let the steaks come to room temperature before cooking and always rest them afterwards.
A compound butter (maître d'hôtel) takes 2 minutes to make and transforms the dish.

title: Crème Brûlée
description: Crème brûlée is a French classic of baked vanilla custard topped with a thin layer of caramelised sugar that shatters at the tap of a spoon to reveal the silky, trembling cream beneath. Its origins are debated between France, England, and Spain, but the French version — rich with egg yolks, double cream, and real vanilla — has become the global standard. The contrast between the warm, crackling caramel top and the cool, velvety custard is one of the great textural pleasures in dessert making.

ingredients.name: vanilla pod
ingredients.notes: split and seeds scraped, or 2 tsp vanilla extract

steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 150°C. Place the cream and vanilla pod (with seeds) in a saucepan. Heat gently until just below boiling. Remove from heat and let steep for 10 minutes. Remove the vanilla pod.
Whisk the egg yolks with 80g caster sugar and salt until pale and thick. Gradually pour the warm cream into the yolks, whisking constantly. Strain through a fine sieve.
Divide the mixture among 6 ramekins. Place in a deep roasting tin and pour hot water into the tin until it reaches halfway up the ramekins (bain-marie).
Bake for 40-45 minutes until the custard is set but still has a slight wobble in the centre. Remove from the water bath and cool to room temperature, then refrigerate for at least 4 hours.

Just before serving, sprinkle a thin, even layer of caster sugar over each custard. Caramelise with a kitchen blowtorch (or under a very hot grill) until the sugar melts and turns deep amber. Serve within 10 minutes.

image_placeholder: A ramekin of crème brûlée with a perfectly crackled amber sugar top, a spoon having broken through to reveal the creamy custard beneath, on a white saucer.
tips: The custard should wobble like jelly when done — it firms up as it cools. Overbaking makes it grainy.
Chill for at least 4 hours (overnight is ideal) before caramelising the top.
Caramelise just before serving — the sugar top begins to soften after about 10 minutes.

title: Tarte au Citron
description: Tarte au citron is one of French pâtisserie's finest creations — a crisp, buttery pâte sucrée shell filled with a vibrant, smooth lemon curd that balances sweetness and sharp citrus in perfect harmony. The filling is made from eggs, sugar, butter, and a generous quantity of fresh lemon juice and zest, cooked until thick and poured into the blind-baked shell. Often finished with a light torching of meringue or a dusting of icing sugar, it is elegant, refreshing, and universally adored.

steps.instruction: Make the pastry: pulse flour, icing sugar, and cold butter in a food processor until it resembles breadcrumbs. Add egg yolks and pulse until the dough just comes together. Wrap and chill for 1 hour.

Roll out the pastry on a floured surface to 3 mm thick. Line a 24 cm tart tin, pressing into the edges. Trim excess. Prick the base with a fork. Chill for 20 minutes. Line with baking paper and baking beans, then blind bake at 180°C for 15 minutes. Remove beans and bake for 5 more minutes until golden.

Make the filling: whisk eggs and caster sugar together. Add lemon juice and zest. Cook in a saucepan over low heat, stirring constantly, until thick enough to coat the back of a spoon (about 10 minutes). Do not boil.
Remove from heat and whisk in the cold butter pieces until melted and incorporated. Stir in the cream. Strain through a fine sieve into the baked tart shell.
Bake at 150°C for 15-20 minutes until just set with a slight wobble. Cool completely before removing from the tin. Dust with icing sugar to serve.

image_placeholder: A pristine tarte au citron with a smooth, pale yellow filling in a golden pastry shell, dusted with icing sugar, on a marble pâtisserie counter.
tips: Strain the lemon curd to remove any bits of egg white for the smoothest possible filling.
The tart is done when the filling still has a gentle wobble — it sets further as it cools.
Best eaten on the day it's made; the pastry softens overnight.

title: Mille-Feuille
description: The mille-feuille (literally 'thousand leaves') is one of the crown jewels of French pâtisserie — three layers of crisp, caramelised puff pastry sandwiched with silky pastry cream (crème pâtissière). The top is traditionally finished with fondant icing or a dusting of icing sugar. Getting the puff pastry shatteringly crisp while keeping the pastry cream light and smooth is the challenge. When executed well, each bite yields an irresistible combination of buttery crunch and vanilla-scented cream.

ingredients.name: icing sugar
ingredients.notes: for dusting or icing the top

steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 200°C. Roll out the puff pastry to about 3 mm thick. Cut into 3 equal rectangles. Prick all over with a fork. Place on lined baking trays, top with another sheet of baking paper and a second tray to keep flat. Bake for 20-25 minutes until deep golden and crisp. Dust the top of each with icing sugar and flash under a hot grill for 30 seconds to caramelise.

Make the crème pâtissière: heat the milk with the vanilla pod and seeds until just boiling. Whisk egg yolks, sugar, and cornflour in a bowl until pale. Pour the hot milk over the egg mixture, whisking constantly. Return to the pan and cook over medium heat, whisking, until thick (about 3-4 minutes). Remove from heat, whisk in butter. Press cling film onto the surface to prevent a skin forming. Cool, then refrigerate until cold.

Whisk the cold crème pâtissière until smooth and spreadable. Spread a thick, even layer on the first pastry rectangle. Top with the second rectangle and another layer of cream. Finish with the third rectangle (caramelised side up).
Dust the top with icing sugar. Refrigerate for 30 minutes to set before slicing with a serrated knife.

image_placeholder: A beautiful mille-feuille with three layers of golden puff pastry and vanilla cream between them, dusted with icing sugar, on a glass pâtisserie display plate.
tips: Weighing down the pastry while baking ensures it stays flat and evenly crisp — essential for clean layers.
The pastry cream must be completely cold and whisked smooth before assembly.
Cut with a sharp serrated knife using a gentle sawing motion to avoid crushing the layers.

title: Patatas Bravas
title_en: Spicy Fried Potatoes with Bravas Sauce
description: Patatas bravas is Spain's most ubiquitous tapa — crispy fried potato cubes served with a spicy, smoky bravas sauce (salsa brava) and often a garlic aioli. Originating in Madrid, every bar has its own version. The potatoes are fried until golden and crunchy, then drenched in the piquant tomato-based sauce, made with smoked paprika (pimentón) and cayenne. The contrast between the crispy potato, spicy sauce, and cool aioli is addictive.

steps.instruction: Make the bravas sauce: heat 15 ml olive oil in a saucepan. Add garlic and cook 30 seconds. Add tomatoes, smoked paprika, cayenne, and a pinch of salt. Simmer for 15 minutes until thick. Add sherry vinegar. Blend until smooth.
Boil the potato cubes in salted water for 8-10 minutes until just tender. Drain well and pat dry.

Heat remaining olive oil in a large frying pan over high heat. Fry the potatoes in batches until golden and crispy on all sides, about 8 minutes per batch. Drain on kitchen paper and season with salt.
Pile the potatoes on a warm plate and spoon the bravas sauce generously over the top. Serve immediately.

image_placeholder: A terracotta dish of golden crispy potato cubes drenched in a vibrant red bravas sauce, with smoky paprika dusted on top, on a tapas bar counter.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold, crisp Albariño or a glass of cold Spanish lager.
tips: Parboiling before frying ensures the potatoes are fluffy inside and crispy outside.
The bravas sauce can be made ahead and refrigerated for up to 5 days.
For extra authenticity, serve with a drizzle of garlic aioli alongside the bravas sauce.

title: Gambas al Ajillo
description: Gambas al ajillo is one of Spain's most popular tapas — plump prawns sizzled in a pool of olive oil infused with garlic and chilli. Served bubbling hot in a small cazuela (terracotta dish), with crusty bread to mop up every last drop of the fragrant oil, it is a dish of extraordinary simplicity and impact. The prawns cook in just minutes, the garlic should be golden but never burnt, and the chilli provides a gentle warmth. A true 10-minute wonder.

ingredients.name: dried red chilli
ingredients.notes: guindilla or any small dried chilli

steps.instruction: Heat the olive oil in a small cazuela or frying pan over medium heat. Add the garlic and chillies. Cook for 1-2 minutes until the garlic just starts to turn golden.
Increase the heat to high. Add the prawns and a good pinch of salt. Cook for 2-3 minutes, turning once, until the prawns are pink and just cooked through.
Add the sherry if using — it will sizzle and steam. Cook for 30 seconds. Remove from heat, scatter with parsley. Serve immediately, bubbling, with crusty bread.

image_placeholder: A small bubbling cazuela of gambas al ajillo with pink prawns in golden garlic-infused olive oil, flecked with parsley and chilli, with bread on the side.
wine_pairing_notes: A chilled fino sherry or a dry Albariño from Rías Baixas.
tips: Don't overcook the garlic — it should be golden, not brown, or it turns bitter.
Use the best olive oil you have — it IS the sauce.
Have everything ready before you start — this dish cooks in under 5 minutes.

title: Croquetas de Jamón
description: Croquetas de jamón are Spain's ultimate comfort tapa — creamy, molten béchamel studded with salty jamón serrano, encased in a crisp breadcrumb shell. Found in every tapas bar across Spain, they are often the measure of a kitchen's skill. The thick béchamel must be chilled until firm enough to shape, then breaded and fried until the outside shatters and the inside oozes. Making them from scratch is a labour of love, but the result is incomparably better than any shop-bought version.

substitutions.notes: less complex flavour but more accessible

steps.instruction: Melt butter in a saucepan over medium heat. Add flour and whisk for 2 minutes to make a roux. Gradually add the warm milk, whisking constantly, until you have a thick, smooth béchamel, about 5 minutes.
Stir in the diced jamón, nutmeg, salt, and pepper. Cook for 2 more minutes. Pour into a flat dish, press cling film onto the surface, and refrigerate for at least 4 hours (overnight is best).
Shape the chilled mixture into small cylinders (about 30g each). Roll in flour (from remaining flour), then beaten egg, then breadcrumbs. Place on a tray.
Heat oil to 170°C. Fry the croquetas in batches for 2-3 minutes until deep golden. Drain on kitchen paper. Serve hot.

image_placeholder: A plate of golden, crispy croquetas de jamón, one broken open to show the creamy ham-studded béchamel inside, on a terracotta plate in a tapas bar setting.
wine_pairing_notes: A manzanilla sherry or a crisp Cava.
tips: The béchamel must be very thick and well-chilled — at least 4 hours. Overnight is even better.
Double-breading (egg-breadcrumb-egg-breadcrumb) gives an even crispier shell.
Uncooked croquetas freeze perfectly — fry from frozen, adding 1-2 minutes.

title: Escalivada
description: Escalivada is a traditional Catalan dish of smoky, fire-roasted vegetables — aubergines, red peppers, onions, and sometimes tomatoes — peeled, torn into strips, and dressed with olive oil, salt, and sometimes garlic. The name comes from the Catalan 'escalivar', meaning to cook in hot ashes. The long, slow roasting concentrates the vegetables' natural sugars, creating a sweet, smoky character. Served at room temperature as a starter, side, or on bread (pa amb tomàquet style).

steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 200°C (or use a BBQ/direct flame). Place the whole aubergines, peppers, onions, and tomatoes on a baking tray. Roast for 40-60 minutes, turning occasionally, until the skins are charred and blistered and the vegetables are completely soft.
Place the peppers in a bowl and cover with cling film to steam for 10 minutes. Peel all the vegetables when cool enough to handle — the skins should slip off easily. Deseed the peppers.

Tear the aubergine and peppers into long strips with your hands (don't cut — tearing gives a better texture). Arrange on a platter. Dress generously with olive oil, salt, and garlic if using. Serve at room temperature.

image_placeholder: A rustic platter of torn roasted vegetables — silky aubergine strips, red pepper, and onion — glistening with olive oil on a terracotta plate.
wine_pairing_notes: A Priorat or Montsant red from Catalonia.
tips: The vegetables can be roasted directly over a gas flame for extra smokiness.
Tear, don't cut — tearing creates irregular surfaces that hold more oil.
Escalivada keeps well refrigerated for 3 days; bring to room temperature before serving.

title: Fideuà
description: Fideuà is paella's lesser-known cousin from the coast of Valencia and Catalonia — short, thin noodles (fideos) cooked in a rich seafood stock with prawns, squid, and mussels, flavoured with saffron, garlic, and smoky paprika. Legend has it that a Gandía fisherman invented it when he ran out of rice. The noodles are first toasted in oil to develop a nutty flavour, then cooked in stock until they absorb all the liquid. The crispy, caramelised noodle crust at the bottom (socarrat) is the prize.

ingredients.name: fideos (thin pasta noodles, size 0-2)
ingredients.notes: or vermicelli broken into short pieces

substitutions.substitute: angel hair pasta broken into 3cm pieces

steps.instruction: Heat olive oil in a wide paella pan or large frying pan over medium heat. Toast the fideos, stirring constantly, until golden, about 3 minutes. Remove and set aside.
In the same pan, sauté the onion for 5 minutes. Add squid and cook until lightly coloured. Add garlic, tomatoes, paprika, and saffron. Cook for 3 minutes.
Pour in the wine and let it reduce slightly. Add the toasted noodles and warm fish stock. Stir once, then let cook without stirring for 8 minutes.
Nestle the prawns and mussels into the noodles. Cook for 5-6 minutes until the mussels open, prawns are pink, and the liquid is absorbed. Discard any unopened mussels.
Let rest for 2 minutes. Serve from the pan with lemon wedges.

image_placeholder: A wide paella pan of fideuà with golden noodles, pink prawns, open mussels, and squid rings, with lemon wedges on the side.
wine_pairing_notes: A dry Penedès white or a light Garnacha rosado.
tips: Toasting the noodles first is essential — it prevents them from becoming mushy and adds nutty flavour.
Don't stir once the stock is added — this helps develop the socarrat (crispy bottom).
Serve with garlic aioli (allioli) on the side for the full experience.

title: Crema Catalana
description: Crema catalana is Catalonia's answer to crème brûlée — a silky, citrus-and-cinnamon scented custard with a crackly caramelised sugar top. Unlike its French cousin, it is thickened with cornflour and egg yolks rather than baked in a bain-marie, and infused with lemon and orange zest and a cinnamon stick. Traditionally served on 19 March for the Feast of Saint Joseph, it is one of Spain's most beloved desserts. The caramelisation is done with a hot iron (ferro) or a blowtorch.

steps.instruction: Heat the milk with the lemon peel, orange peel, and cinnamon stick over medium heat until just below boiling. Remove from heat and infuse for 15 minutes. Strain.
Whisk the egg yolks with 100g sugar until pale. Add the cornflour and whisk until smooth.

Gradually pour the warm infused milk into the egg mixture, whisking constantly. Return to the pan and cook over medium-low heat, stirring constantly, until the custard thickens enough to coat the back of a spoon, about 5-7 minutes. Do not boil.
Pour into 6 shallow terracotta dishes or ramekins. Cool to room temperature, then refrigerate for at least 3 hours.
Just before serving, sprinkle a thin layer of sugar over each custard and caramelise with a blowtorch until deep amber.

image_placeholder: A shallow terracotta dish of crema catalana with a perfectly crackled amber sugar top, a spoon resting alongside, on a rustic Spanish table.
tips: The citrus and cinnamon infusion is what distinguishes this from crème brûlée — don't skip it.
Cook the custard over low heat and stir constantly to avoid scrambling the eggs.
Caramelise just before serving — the top softens within 15 minutes.

title: Gazpacho
description: Gazpacho is Andalusia's iconic chilled summer soup — a vibrant, refreshing blend of ripe tomatoes, peppers, cucumber, garlic, bread, sherry vinegar, and olive oil. No cooking required — everything is blended raw and served ice-cold. It dates back centuries, evolving from a simple bread-and-garlic pounding to the tomato-based version we know today. The best gazpacho depends entirely on the quality and ripeness of the tomatoes. Served in glasses or bowls with diced vegetable garnish.

substitutions.notes: soup will be thinner without bread

steps.instruction: Soak the bread in a little water for 5 minutes. Squeeze out excess.
Place all ingredients in a blender. Blend on high for 2-3 minutes until very smooth. Pass through a fine sieve for the smoothest result (optional).
Taste and adjust salt and vinegar. Chill in the fridge for at least 2 hours (overnight is better). Serve ice-cold with a drizzle of olive oil and diced vegetables as garnish.

image_placeholder: A glass of vibrant red gazpacho garnished with diced cucumber and a swirl of olive oil, on a sun-drenched Spanish terrace table.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold manzanilla sherry or a dry, crisp Spanish white.
tips: Use the ripest tomatoes you can find — hothouse tomatoes in winter won't cut it.
Gazpacho thickens and improves overnight as flavours meld.
Serve in chilled bowls or glasses with ice cubes for maximum refreshment.

title: Salmorejo
description: Salmorejo is gazpacho's richer, thicker cousin from Córdoba in Andalusia. Made with just tomatoes, bread, garlic, olive oil, and sherry vinegar — no peppers or cucumber — it is blended until ultra-smooth and creamy, with a gorgeous orange-pink colour. The bread gives it body, and a generous amount of olive oil creates an emulsion that's almost velvety. Traditionally served topped with diced jamón serrano and hard-boiled egg, it is pure summer in a bowl.

steps.instruction: Soak the bread in the chopped tomatoes for 10 minutes.
Place the tomato-bread mixture, garlic, vinegar, and salt in a blender. Blend on high speed. With the motor running, slowly drizzle in the olive oil until the mixture is completely smooth and creamy.
Chill for at least 2 hours. Serve in bowls topped with diced jamón and hard-boiled egg, with an extra drizzle of olive oil.

image_placeholder: A bowl of thick, creamy salmorejo with a gorgeous orange hue, topped with diced jamón and egg, drizzled with olive oil.
wine_pairing_notes: A chilled fino or amontillado sherry from Jerez.
tips: More bread = thicker salmorejo. Adjust to your preference.
Drizzle the oil slowly while blending to create a proper emulsion.
Unlike gazpacho, salmorejo should be thick enough that a spoon stands up in it.

title: Pescaíto Frito
description: Pescaíto frito is Andalusia's beloved platter of lightly battered, deep-fried small fish — a coastal tradition from Cádiz to Málaga. Small fish such as boquerones (fresh anchovies), calamari rings, red mullet, and baby sole are dredged simply in seasoned flour and fried quickly in very hot olive oil until shatteringly crisp and golden. No heavy batter — just a light flour dusting that lets the delicate fish flavour shine. Served with lemon and a cold beer, it is summer eating at its finest.

ingredients.name: mixed small fish
ingredients.notes: such as fresh anchovies, small red mullet, baby squid, cleaned

steps.instruction: Pat the fish completely dry with kitchen paper. This is essential for a crispy result.
Season the flour with salt. Toss the fish in the flour, shaking off any excess.
Heat the olive oil to 180°C in a deep pan. Fry the fish in small batches for 2-3 minutes until golden and crispy. Don't overcrowd the pan. Drain on kitchen paper.
Season with a final sprinkle of salt. Pile onto a platter and serve immediately with lemon wedges.

image_placeholder: A generous platter of golden fried small fish and squid rings piled high, with lemon wedges and a cold beer, on a sunny seaside table in Cádiz.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold manzanilla sherry from Sanlúcar de Barrameda or a crisp Verdejo.
tips: The fish must be bone-dry before flouring — moisture creates steam and prevents crispiness.
Fry in small batches to keep the oil temperature high.
Use olive oil for frying if you can — it's traditional and gives the fish a subtle flavour.

title: Pintxos de Bacalao
description: Pintxos de bacalao are one of the Basque Country's most popular bar snacks — crispy, golden fritters of salt cod in a light batter, served on cocktail sticks as pintxos (the Basque version of tapas). Salt cod (bacalao) has been central to Basque cuisine for centuries, brought back by Basque fishermen from the North Atlantic. The cod is soaked to remove excess salt, then mixed into a batter and deep-fried into light, fluffy morsels. Best served hot with a glass of txakoli.

ingredients.name: salt cod
ingredients.notes: soaked for 24-48 hours, changing water 3-4 times

steps.instruction: Drain the soaked cod, pat dry, and shred into small flakes, removing any bones or skin.
Make the batter: whisk egg yolks, milk, flour, baking powder, garlic, and parsley until smooth. Whisk the egg whites to stiff peaks and fold gently into the batter. Fold in the cod flakes.
Heat oil to 170°C. Drop spoonfuls of the batter into the oil and fry for 3-4 minutes until golden and puffed. Drain on kitchen paper. Serve hot with cocktail sticks.

image_placeholder: A plate of golden salt cod fritters on cocktail sticks, with a glass of txakoli wine, on a zinc bar counter in a Basque pintxos bar.
wine_pairing_notes: A glass of txakoli (Basque white wine) or a cold Pilsner.
tips: Soak the salt cod for at least 24 hours, changing the water 3-4 times, to remove excess salt.
Folding whisked egg whites into the batter makes the fritters light and airy.
Fry in small batches to maintain oil temperature.

title: Marmitako
description: Marmitako is the Basque fisherman's stew — a hearty one-pot of fresh tuna, potatoes, peppers, onions, and tomatoes, originally made aboard fishing boats in the Bay of Biscay. The name comes from 'marmita' (cooking pot). The potatoes break down slightly, thickening the broth, while chunks of fresh tuna are added at the end and gently poached until just cooked through. Flavoured with choricero peppers (or their pulp), it is deeply satisfying comfort food from the sea.

ingredients.name: potatoes
ingredients.notes: peeled, cut into chunks (break rather than cut for starchier broth)

steps.instruction: Heat olive oil in a large casserole over medium heat. Sauté the onion and green pepper for 8 minutes until soft. Add garlic and cook for 1 minute.
Add the tomatoes and paprika. Cook for 3 minutes. Add the wine and let it reduce slightly.
Add the potatoes and fish stock. Bring to a boil, then simmer for 20-25 minutes until the potatoes are tender and starting to break down.
Season the tuna cubes with salt. Nestle them into the stew. Cover and cook over low heat for 5-6 minutes until the tuna is just cooked through (still slightly pink inside). Serve immediately.

image_placeholder: A rustic cazuela of marmitako with chunks of tuna and potatoes in a rich reddish broth, on a wooden table with crusty bread.
wine_pairing_notes: A txakoli or a Rioja white (Viura-based).
tips: Break (don't cut) the potatoes — the rough edges release starch and thicken the broth naturally.
Add the tuna at the very end and don't overcook — it should be just barely cooked through.
This stew is even better the next day (reheat gently to avoid overcooking the tuna).

title: Txangurro
description: Txangurro is the Basque Country's most celebrated seafood dish — spider crab meat mixed with a rich sofrito of onions, tomatoes, leeks, and brandy, spooned back into the crab shell, topped with breadcrumbs, and grilled until golden. A dish of indulgent elegance, it showcases the Basque passion for the finest seafood. While traditionally made with spider crab (centollo), brown crab or any good-quality crab meat can be used. It is a classic of Basque alta cocina.

ingredients.name: cooked spider crab or brown crab
ingredients.notes: about 500g each, or 400g picked crab meat
