image_placeholder: A plate of golden æbleskiver spheres dusted with icing sugar, with a bowl of red jam, on a cosy Danish Christmas table.
tips: An æbleskiver pan is essential — the half-sphere indentations create the signature shape.
Fold the egg whites gently to keep the batter light and fluffy.
Turn the balls with a knitting needle or skewer — the technique takes a little practice.

title: Tabbouleh
description: Tabbouleh is Lebanon's most iconic salad, a vibrant celebration of flat-leaf parsley that forms the bulk of the dish rather than the bulgur wheat, which plays a supporting role. Originating in the mountains of Lebanon, authentic tabbouleh demands an abundance of finely chopped fresh parsley, ripe tomatoes, spring onions, and fresh mint, dressed simply with extra virgin olive oil and lemon juice. The fine bulgur wheat (#1 grade) is merely soaked rather than cooked, providing a subtle nutty undertone. Traditionally served on young vine leaves or crisp lettuce cups, it is a cornerstone of any mezze spread.

ingredients.name: flat-leaf parsley
ingredients.notes: about 4 large bunches, very finely chopped

ingredients.name: fine bulgur wheat (#1)
ingredients.notes: soaked in cold water for 15 minutes, drained

substitutions.notes: not traditional but works for gluten-sensitive palates

ingredients.name: spring onions
ingredients.notes: white and green parts, thinly sliced

steps.instruction: Soak the fine bulgur wheat in cold water for 15 minutes. Drain thoroughly in a fine sieve, pressing out excess water. Set aside.
Wash and thoroughly dry the parsley (a salad spinner works well). Remove the thick stems and finely chop the leaves. You should have about 3 packed cups.
Finely dice the tomatoes, removing seeds to prevent the salad from becoming watery. Thinly slice the spring onions and finely chop the mint.
In a large bowl, combine the drained bulgur, parsley, tomatoes, spring onions, and mint. Toss gently.
Whisk together the olive oil, lemon juice, salt, and allspice if using. Pour the dressing over the salad and toss thoroughly. Taste and adjust seasoning. Serve immediately or chill for 30 minutes.

image_placeholder: A vibrant bowl of tabbouleh with finely chopped parsley, diced tomatoes, and bulgur, served on a white ceramic plate with young lettuce leaves and lemon wedges on the side.
wine_pairing_notes: A crisp, dry Lebanese white wine such as Château Musar Jeune Blanc or a Provençal rosé pairs beautifully with the herbaceous freshness.
tips: The key to authentic tabbouleh is the ratio: parsley should dominate — at least 3 parts parsley to 1 part bulgur by volume.
Use fine (#1) bulgur that only needs soaking, not cooking. If you can only find coarse bulgur, cook it briefly and cool completely before using.
Remove tomato seeds and salt the diced tomatoes briefly on a paper towel to prevent a watery salad.

title: Fattoush
description: Fattoush is a beloved Lebanese bread salad that transforms day-old pitta into a crunchy, vibrant dish. The salad combines crisp fried or toasted pitta shards with a colourful medley of seasonal vegetables — cucumbers, tomatoes, radishes, and cos lettuce — all dressed in a tangy sumac and pomegranate molasses vinaigrette. Sumac provides a distinctive sour, fruity note that is the hallmark of this dish. Unlike tabbouleh, fattoush celebrates variety and texture, with each bite offering a different combination of crunch, juiciness, and herbaceous freshness.

substitutions.notes: adds acidity but not the same fruity tartness

steps.instruction: Tear or cut the pitta bread into bite-sized pieces. Heat olive oil in a frying pan over medium-high heat and fry the pitta pieces until golden and crispy, about 3-4 minutes. Drain on kitchen paper. Alternatively, brush with oil and bake at 200°C for 8-10 minutes.
Prepare the vegetables: roughly chop the lettuce, dice the tomatoes and cucumber, thinly slice the radishes, and slice the spring onions. Combine in a large salad bowl.
Add the chopped parsley and torn mint leaves to the vegetables.
Whisk together the olive oil, lemon juice, pomegranate molasses, sumac, minced garlic, and salt to make the dressing.
Just before serving, add the crispy pitta to the salad and pour over the dressing. Toss well. Sprinkle extra sumac on top and serve immediately.

image_placeholder: A colourful fattoush salad with crispy golden pitta shards, red radishes, diced tomatoes and cucumbers, topped with a generous dusting of sumac, served in a rustic ceramic bowl.
wine_pairing_notes: A mineral-driven Lebanese white from the Bekaa Valley or a light, dry rosé complements the tangy dressing perfectly.
tips: Always add the pitta just before serving to maintain crunch — once dressed, it softens quickly.
Sumac is essential and not merely decorative — it provides the characteristic sour, fruity flavour that defines fattoush.
For the best results, use ripe summer tomatoes and purslane (baqle) if you can find it — it is the traditional green used in fattoush.

title: Hummus bi Tahini
description: Authentic Lebanese hummus is a world apart from the dense supermarket versions. Made from dried chickpeas soaked overnight and cooked with bicarbonate of soda until completely tender, then blended with generous tahini, fresh lemon juice, and garlic until impossibly silky. The secret lies in over-cooking the chickpeas, using ice cubes during blending for an ethereally smooth texture, and generous tahini. Served with a well in the centre filled with olive oil, a sprinkle of paprika, and warm pitta bread.

ingredients.name: dried chickpeas
ingredients.notes: soaked overnight in plenty of water

substitutions.notes: faster but less creamy — simmer tinned chickpeas for 20 minutes with a pinch of bicarb

ingredients.name: bicarbonate of soda
ingredients.notes: 1 tsp for soaking, 0.5 tsp for cooking

ingredients.name: ice cubes
ingredients.notes: added during blending for silky texture

steps.instruction: The night before, place the dried chickpeas in a large bowl with 1 tsp bicarbonate of soda and cover generously with cold water. Soak for at least 12 hours.

Drain and rinse the soaked chickpeas. Place in a large pot with fresh water to cover by 5cm and add 0.5 tsp bicarbonate of soda. Bring to a boil, skimming off foam and loose skins. Reduce heat and simmer for 45-60 minutes until very soft.
Drain the chickpeas, reserving 100ml of the cooking liquid. While still hot, rub handfuls gently to loosen and discard as many skins as possible.
Add the tahini, lemon juice, garlic, and salt to a food processor. Blend for 2 minutes until very smooth and pale.
Add the hot, skinned chickpeas and ice cubes. Blend for 4-5 minutes until extremely smooth. If too thick, add reserved cooking liquid a tablespoon at a time.
Spread on a plate using the back of a spoon to create a well. Drizzle with olive oil, dust with paprika or sumac, and serve with warm pitta bread.

image_placeholder: A plate of velvety smooth hummus with a well filled with golden olive oil and a sprinkle of paprika, surrounded by warm pitta bread triangles on a rustic wooden table.
wine_pairing_notes: A crisp Sauvignon Blanc or a Lebanese Arak with water make excellent partners for hummus.
tips: Use dried chickpeas and overcook them with bicarbonate of soda. Tinned chickpeas will never achieve the same silky texture.
Blend the tahini and lemon first without the chickpeas — this creates a lighter, fluffier base.
Remove as many chickpea skins as possible for extraordinary smoothness.

title: Baba Ghanoush
description: Baba ghanoush is a smoky aubergine dip that is a staple of Lebanese mezze. The aubergines are charred directly over an open flame or under a grill until the skin is blackened and the flesh is completely soft. The collapsed flesh is mixed with tahini, lemon juice, and garlic to create a dip with a distinctive smoky flavour. The texture should remain chunky and rustic — not blended smooth like hummus.

steps.instruction: Prick the aubergines with a fork. Char directly over a gas flame, turning with tongs every 3-4 minutes until the skin is completely blackened and the flesh is soft and collapsed, about 15-20 minutes. Alternatively, grill under a very hot grill for 25-30 minutes.
Place the charred aubergines in a colander over a bowl. Slit them open and let the bitter juices drain for 15 minutes.
Scoop out the soft flesh, discarding the charred skin. Roughly chop the flesh — do not blend.
Combine the aubergine flesh with tahini, lemon juice, garlic, and salt. Mix with a fork, keeping some texture.
Transfer to a serving plate, drizzle with olive oil, and garnish with chopped parsley and pomegranate seeds.

image_placeholder: A rustic bowl of smoky baba ghanoush topped with olive oil, pomegranate seeds, and fresh parsley, surrounded by warm pitta bread on a wooden board.
wine_pairing_notes: A lightly oaked Chardonnay or a dry Lebanese rosé pairs well with the smoky, creamy flavours.
tips: For the best smoky flavour, char the aubergines directly on a gas hob.
Draining the bitter liquid from the charred aubergine is crucial.
Do not use a food processor — the texture should be chunky and rustic.

title: Kibbeh bil Sanieh
description: Kibbeh bil sanieh is the beloved baked version of Lebanon's national dish. Layers of spiced lamb and fine bulgur wheat dough sandwich a filling of pine nuts, caramelised onions, and seasoned lamb mince, all baked until golden with a crisp crust. The kibbeh shell is made by kneading finely minced lamb with soaked fine bulgur, onion, and Lebanese seven-spice until smooth. Traditionally scored into diamond shapes before baking, each diamond topped with ghee for a rich, golden finish.

ingredients.name: fine bulgur wheat (#1)
ingredients.notes: soaked in boiling water for 15 minutes, drained

ingredients.name: lean minced lamb (for shell)
ingredients.notes: very lean, ask butcher to mince twice

substitutions.notes: traditional is lamb but beef works

substitutions.substitute: allspice, cinnamon, and black pepper in equal parts

steps.instruction: Soak the fine bulgur in boiling water for 15 minutes. Drain thoroughly.

Prepare the filling: heat olive oil in a pan. Sauté the diced onions until softened. Add the filling lamb, cinnamon, allspice, salt, and pepper. Cook until browned. Stir in pine nuts and pomegranate molasses. Cool.
Make the shell: process drained bulgur, lean lamb, grated onion, seven-spice, and salt until a smooth paste. Keep ice water nearby.
Preheat oven to 190°C. Grease a 30x20cm dish. With wet hands, press half the kibbeh into the base.
Spread the cooled filling evenly over the kibbeh base.
Flatten remaining kibbeh between wet palms and lay over the filling, sealing gently. Smooth the top.
Score the top into diamond shapes. Dab each with melted ghee. Bake for 40-50 minutes until deep golden brown.

image_placeholder: Golden-brown baked kibbeh scored into diamond shapes in a baking dish, with visible layers of spiced lamb and pine nut filling.
wine_pairing_notes: A medium-bodied red such as Château Musar or a Lebanese Cinsault-based blend complements the spiced lamb.
tips: Keep a bowl of ice water nearby when shaping — wet hands prevent sticking.
Ask your butcher to mince the lamb twice through the fine blade.
Can be assembled a day ahead, refrigerated, then baked before serving.

title: Shish Taouk
description: Shish taouk are iconic grilled chicken skewers found at every Lebanese restaurant and street food stall. Boneless chicken thighs are marinated in yoghurt, lemon juice, garlic, tomato paste, and aromatic spices, then threaded onto skewers and grilled until charred and juicy. The yoghurt tenderises the meat while the garlic and spices create a deeply flavourful crust. Traditionally served in a pitta wrap with garlic sauce (toum), pickled turnips, and fresh vegetables.

steps.instruction: Combine the yoghurt, lemon juice, garlic, tomato paste, olive oil, and all spices. Add chicken and coat evenly. Refrigerate for at least 2 hours, ideally overnight.
Thread the chicken onto skewers, leaving small gaps. If using wooden skewers, soak them first for 30 minutes.
Grill on a preheated griddle or barbecue over high heat for 4-5 minutes per side until charred and cooked through. Rest for 3 minutes.
Serve in warm pitta with garlic sauce (toum), pickled turnips, and fresh salad.

image_placeholder: Charred shish taouk skewers on flatbread with pickled turnips, fresh tomatoes, and garlic sauce.
wine_pairing_notes: A chilled dry rosé or a light-bodied red like Gamay works well with charred, spiced chicken.
tips: Marinate for at least 2 hours, overnight is best.
Use chicken thighs rather than breast for juicier results.
Make garlic sauce (toum) by blending garlic, lemon juice, salt, and neutral oil until emulsified.

title: Kafta Meshwiyeh
description: Kafta meshwiyeh are fragrant Lebanese grilled minced meat kebabs, a staple of every barbecue and mezze spread. A blend of minced lamb and beef is seasoned with flat-leaf parsley, onion, and Lebanese seven-spice, then shaped around flat metal skewers and grilled over charcoal. The high onion and herb content keeps the meat moist. Served with hummus, tabbouleh, and warm pitta.

ingredients.name: onion
ingredients.notes: very finely grated, liquid squeezed out

substitutions.substitute: allspice, cinnamon, and black pepper mix

steps.instruction: Combine all ingredients and knead vigorously for 3-4 minutes until smooth and slightly sticky.
Refrigerate for at least 30 minutes to firm up.
With wet hands, mould about 80g of mixture around flat skewers in a long sausage shape, or form flat oval patties.
Grill on a preheated barbecue or griddle over high heat for 3-4 minutes per side until charred and cooked through.

image_placeholder: Charred kafta kebabs on flat metal skewers on a white platter with grilled tomatoes and pitta bread.
wine_pairing_notes: A spicy Syrah from the Bekaa Valley pairs beautifully with the spiced grilled meat.
tips: Squeeze out excess onion moisture — too much liquid makes the mixture too wet.
Flat metal skewers prevent kafta from spinning when turning.
Do not overmix — knead just enough to hold together.

title: Mujaddara
title_en: Lentil and Rice with Caramelised Onions
description: Mujaddara is one of the oldest dishes in Lebanese cuisine — a humble yet deeply satisfying combination of green or brown lentils and rice, crowned with a generous mound of deeply caramelised onions. The dish epitomises the beauty of peasant cooking: simple ingredients transformed through patience. The slow caramelisation of onions takes up to 40 minutes to achieve the dark, sweet, jammy result that defines this dish.

steps.instruction: Heat olive oil in a large pan over medium heat. Add all sliced onions and cook, stirring occasionally, for 30-40 minutes until deeply caramelised and dark golden. Remove two-thirds and set aside for topping.
Add lentils, cumin, salt, and pepper to the remaining onions. Pour in water, bring to a boil, reduce heat, cover and simmer for 15 minutes.
Drain the rice and add to the lentils. Cover and cook on low for 15-18 minutes until the rice is cooked and liquid absorbed. Do not stir.
Leave the lid on for 10 minutes off the heat. Fluff with a fork, transfer to a platter, and top with reserved caramelised onions. Serve with yoghurt.

image_placeholder: A generous mound of mujaddara topped with dark caramelised onions on an earthenware plate with yoghurt and green salad alongside.
tips: Patience with the onions is everything — 30-40 minutes for true dark-brown caramelisation.
Use green or brown lentils — they hold their shape during cooking.
Leftovers reheat well with a splash of water.

title: Fatayer bi Sabanekh
description: Fatayer bi sabanekh are irresistible Lebanese triangular spinach pies — a staple at bakeries and family gatherings. Soft olive-oil enriched dough is filled with fresh spinach, onion, sumac, lemon juice, and pine nuts, then pinched into their distinctive triangular shape and baked until golden. They freeze beautifully and make excellent packed lunches.

substitutions.substitute: lemon zest plus extra lemon juice

steps.instruction: Make the dough: combine flour, yeast, sugar, salt. Add oil and water. Knead 8-10 minutes until smooth. Cover and rise 1 hour.
Massage chopped spinach with salt to wilt. Squeeze dry in a tea towel.
Mix drained spinach with onion, sumac, lemon juice, olive oil, and pine nuts.
Preheat oven to 200°C. Divide dough into 20 balls. Roll each into a 10cm circle.
Place a tablespoon of filling in each circle. Pinch three equidistant corners together to form a triangle.
Brush with olive oil and bake 15-18 minutes until golden.

image_placeholder: A platter of golden triangular spinach fatayer, with one cut open to reveal the bright green spinach and pine nut filling.
wine_pairing_notes: A dry Vermentino or crisp Grüner Veltliner pairs well with the tangy spinach filling.
tips: Squeeze as much moisture from the spinach as possible.
Pinch edges firmly — fatayer open easily during baking.
Freeze unbaked on a tray, then bag. Bake from frozen at 190°C for 20-22 minutes.

title: Manakish bi Za'atar
description: Manakish bi za'atar is Lebanon's answer to pizza — a beloved breakfast and street food staple. Soft dough rounds are spread with a za'atar and olive oil paste before being baked at high heat until golden and fragrant. Za'atar — a blend of dried thyme, sumac, sesame seeds, and salt — is the soul of this dish. Traditionally baked in a wood-fired oven and eaten folded around fresh tomatoes, mint, and labneh.

substitutions.substitute: dried thyme mixed with sumac and sesame seeds

steps.instruction: Combine flour, yeast, sugar, and salt. Add olive oil and warm water. Knead 8 minutes until smooth. Cover and rise 1 hour.
Mix the za'atar with the olive oil for topping to form a thick paste.
Preheat oven to 250°C with a baking stone or inverted tray inside.
Divide dough into 6 balls. Roll each into a 20cm round, about 3-4mm thick.
Spread 2 generous tablespoons of za'atar paste over each round, pressing gently with your fingers.
Transfer to the hot baking stone and bake 6-8 minutes until edges are golden. Serve immediately, folded around tomatoes, mint, and labneh.

image_placeholder: A golden manakish bi za'atar on a wooden board with sliced tomatoes, fresh mint, and a bowl of labneh.
tips: The hotter the oven the better — a baking stone at 250°C mimics traditional bakery ovens.
Use a good quality Lebanese za'atar blend with plenty of sumac.
Best eaten immediately while still warm and pliable.

title: Kebbeh Nayyeh
description: Kebbeh nayyeh is Lebanon's revered raw meat dish — the Middle Eastern equivalent of steak tartare, but arguably more refined. Ultra-fresh, lean lamb is pounded or processed with soaked fine bulgur, onion, and spices until silky smooth, then served raw with olive oil, fresh mint, and thin bread. This dish demands the freshest, most trustworthy meat available. Due to the raw meat, this dish carries inherent food safety risks and should only be prepared with meat from a trusted butcher.

ingredients.name: very fresh lean lamb leg
ingredients.notes: trimmed of all fat, minced very finely twice

substitutions.notes: must be from a trusted butcher

ingredients.name: onion
ingredients.notes: very finely grated, liquid squeezed out

ingredients.name: ice water
ingredients.notes: to keep meat cold during processing

steps.instruction: Place the finely minced lamb, drained bulgur, grated onion, seven-spice, cumin, and salt in a food processor.
Process in pulses, adding ice water, until very smooth and paste-like. Keep everything cold.
Spread on a flat plate, create decorative lines with a fork, and drizzle generously with olive oil.
Serve immediately with fresh mint, spring onions, and thin pitta bread. Eat within 30 minutes.

image_placeholder: A plate of smooth kebbeh nayyeh with decorative fork lines, drizzled with olive oil, surrounded by fresh mint and thin pitta bread.
wine_pairing_notes: A robust Lebanese red from the Bekaa Valley or Arak is the classic pairing.
tips: CRITICAL: Use only the freshest meat from a trusted butcher, bought on the day of preparation.
Keep everything ice cold throughout preparation.
Traditional preparation uses a stone mortar (jorn); a food processor with ice water achieves similar results.

title: Knafeh
description: Knafeh is one of the Levant's most beloved desserts — a stunning combination of crispy golden shredded filo pastry (kataifi) filled with stretchy melted cheese, drenched in fragrant orange blossom sugar syrup. When done right, knafeh has a beautiful contrast between the buttery, crunchy exterior and the molten, gooey cheese interior. Tripoli in northern Lebanon is particularly famous for its knafeh. The cheese traditionally used is akkawi, desalinated by soaking to remove excess salt.

ingredients.name: akkawi cheese
ingredients.notes: soaked in water 4-6 hours, changing water 3 times

substitutions.notes: not the same but closest available substitute

steps.instruction: Make syrup: combine sugar, water, and lemon juice. Boil, then simmer 8-10 minutes. Remove from heat, add orange blossom and rose water. Cool completely.
Loosen kataifi pastry, shredding into smaller strands. Toss with melted butter until every strand is coated.
Drain the desalinated cheese and tear into small pieces.
Press half the buttered kataifi into a 28-30cm round tin. Spread cheese evenly. Cover with remaining kataifi, pressing down.
Bake at 180°C for 30-35 minutes until deep golden. Grill for 2-3 minutes at the end if needed.
Immediately invert onto a plate. Pour cold syrup over hot knafeh. Garnish with pistachios. Serve immediately.

image_placeholder: A golden knafeh on a round plate with stretchy cheese visible where a slice has been removed, garnished with crushed pistachios.
wine_pairing_notes: A late-harvest Muscat or a glass of mint tea.
tips: Syrup MUST be cold when poured over hot knafeh — hot on hot = soggy.
Desalinate akkawi cheese by soaking 4-6 hours, changing water several times.
Serve within 10 minutes of baking while cheese is still molten.

title: İskender Kebab
description: İskender kebab is one of Turkey's most celebrated dishes, originating from Bursa in the 19th century. Thinly sliced döner kebab meat is arranged over cubes of pitta bread, drenched in a rich, buttery tomato sauce, and served with a generous dollop of creamy yoghurt. The finishing touch — sizzling melted butter poured at the table — is dramatic and essential. The interplay of crispy bread, savoury meat, tangy tomato sauce, cool yoghurt, and hot butter makes this dish extraordinary.

substitutions.substitute: sweet paprika plus a pinch of chilli flakes

steps.instruction: Mix the minced beef with grated onion (liquid squeezed out), yoghurt, cumin, Aleppo pepper, oregano, salt, and black pepper. Knead 3-4 minutes. Shape into a flat loaf 3cm thick. Wrap and refrigerate 2 hours, then freeze 1 hour.
Slice the firm meat into very thin slices (3mm). Griddle in batches over high heat for 1-2 minutes per side until browned.
Make tomato sauce: melt 15g butter, add tomato paste, cook 1 minute. Add passata, pinch of salt, simmer 5-8 minutes.
Toast pitta cubes at 180°C for 5 minutes. Arrange on warm plates.
Lay meat over pitta, spoon tomato sauce over. Add yoghurt on the side and roasted chillies.
Melt 60g finishing butter until sizzling and foaming. Pour immediately over the meat. Serve at once.

image_placeholder: İskender kebab on a warm plate with thinly sliced döner meat over pitta cubes, glistening with sizzling butter and red tomato sauce, yoghurt on the side.
wine_pairing_notes: A medium-bodied Turkish red such as an Öküzgözü-Boğazkere blend.
tips: The finishing butter must be sizzling hot — pour at the very last moment.
Freezing the meat mixture for 1 hour before slicing makes thin cutting easier.
For more authenticity, use a mix of thinly sliced steak and minced meat.

title: Lahmacun
description: Lahmacun is Turkey's beloved thin, crispy flatbread topped with spiced lamb and vegetable mixture. The base is rolled paper-thin and baked at the highest possible oven temperature until crisp, while the topping of finely minced lamb, onion, tomato, pepper paste, and herbs stays moist. Traditionally rolled up with fresh parsley, lemon juice, and pickled onions, and eaten by hand.

substitutions.substitute: tomato paste mixed with 0.5 tsp Aleppo pepper

steps.instruction: Make dough: combine flour, yeast, salt, sugar. Add oil and water. Knead 6-8 minutes. Cover and rise 45 minutes.
Make topping: combine lamb, onion, tomato, pepper, pastes, parsley, spices, and salt. Mix until smooth and spreadable.
Preheat oven to maximum (250-270°C) with baking sheets inside.
Divide dough into 8 balls. Roll each paper-thin, about 1-2mm thick and 25cm diameter.
Spread a thin film of meat mixture over each round, going to the edges.
Bake on preheated sheets for 5-7 minutes until edges are crisp and lightly charred.
Serve immediately with fresh parsley and a generous squeeze of lemon. Roll up and eat by hand.

image_placeholder: Thin, crispy lahmacun with spiced lamb topping on a wooden board, with fresh parsley and lemon wedges.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Turkish lager or Şalgam (turnip juice) is traditional.
tips: Roll the dough as thin as possible — you should almost see through it.
Spread topping in a very thin layer; too much makes it soggy.
Highest oven temperature possible. A pizza stone helps.

title: Pide with Cheese and Egg
title_en: Turkish Pide with Cheese and Egg
description: Pide is Turkey's boat-shaped flatbread, often called Turkish pizza. The Trabzon-style cheese and egg version features a yeasted dough shaped into an elongated oval with raised edges, filled with a mixture of Turkish white cheese and mozzarella, with an egg cracked into the centre during the final minutes of baking. The result is a golden, bubbling bread with molten cheese and a just-set egg — a staple of Turkish pide shops and a popular weekend brunch dish.

steps.instruction: Make the dough: combine flour, yeast, salt, sugar. Add oil and warm water. Knead 8 minutes until smooth. Cover and rise 1 hour.
Preheat oven to 230°C with a baking stone or inverted tray inside.
Divide dough in half. Roll each piece into an oval about 30x15cm. Transfer to baking parchment. Fold and pinch the edges up to create a raised border about 2cm high, shaping it into a boat.
Mix the crumbled white cheese and grated mozzarella. Divide between the two pide, spreading inside the raised borders.
Brush the edges with beaten egg yolk and sprinkle with nigella seeds. Bake for 10 minutes until the edges begin to turn golden.
Remove from oven, make a well in the centre of each pide's cheese, and crack an egg into it. Return to oven for 4-5 minutes until the egg white is set but yolk is still runny.
Brush edges with melted butter immediately upon removing from the oven. Serve hot, cutting into slices.

image_placeholder: A golden boat-shaped Turkish pide with melted cheese and a runny egg in the centre, sprinkled with nigella seeds, on a wooden board.
wine_pairing_notes: A Turkish Narince white wine or a cold Efes beer.
tips: Shape the border high enough to contain the cheese and egg — about 2cm.
Crack the egg in for just the final 4-5 minutes so the yolk stays runny.
Brush with melted butter immediately after baking for a glossy, rich finish.

title: İmam Bayıldı
description: İmam bayıldı — meaning 'the imam fainted' — is one of Turkey's most celebrated dishes. Aubergines are halved and scored, then stuffed with a fragrant filling of slow-cooked onions, tomatoes, garlic, and parsley, before being braised in olive oil until the aubergine is meltingly soft. Legend has it that the imam fainted either from the deliciousness of the dish or from the amount of expensive olive oil used. This is a classic example of Turkish zeytinyağlı cooking — dishes cooked in and served with olive oil at room temperature.

steps.instruction: Peel the aubergines in alternating strips (zebra pattern). Make a deep lengthwise slit in each, being careful not to cut through. Salt the insides and leave for 30 minutes to draw out bitterness. Rinse and pat dry.
Heat half the olive oil in a wide pan. Fry the aubergines on all sides until golden, about 8-10 minutes. Remove and set aside.
In the same pan, add remaining olive oil. Cook the onions over medium-low heat for 15 minutes until very soft and starting to caramelise. Add garlic and cook 2 more minutes.
Add the tomatoes, parsley, sugar, salt, and pepper. Cook for 5 minutes until the tomatoes break down slightly.

Arrange the aubergines slit-side up in a deep pan or baking dish. Gently open the slits and stuff generously with the onion-tomato mixture. Spoon any remaining filling around the aubergines. Add water.

Cover and simmer on very low heat for 45-50 minutes until the aubergines are completely soft and have absorbed the flavours. Alternatively, bake covered at 180°C for the same time. Allow to cool to room temperature before serving — this dish is traditionally served at room temperature.

image_placeholder: İmam bayıldı — stuffed aubergines with caramelised onion and tomato filling — served at room temperature on a decorative Turkish plate, garnished with fresh parsley.
wine_pairing_notes: A dry Turkish rosé or a crisp Narince white wine. Served at room temperature, it also pairs well with raki.
tips: The zebra-stripe peeling helps the aubergine absorb flavours while holding its shape.
Do not skimp on the olive oil — the generous amount is essential to the dish's character.
Always serve at room temperature — the flavours develop and meld as it cools.

title: Menemen
title_en: Turkish Scrambled Eggs with Tomatoes and Peppers
description: Menemen is Turkey's answer to scrambled eggs — a simple but deeply flavourful breakfast dish of eggs gently stirred into a sauce of tomatoes, green peppers, and sometimes onions, flavoured with Aleppo pepper. Unlike shakshuka where eggs are poached in sauce, menemen features softly scrambled, creamy eggs folded through the vegetables. The key is not to overcook the eggs — they should remain soft and custardy. Served directly from the pan with plenty of crusty bread for scooping.

substitutions.substitute: sweet paprika plus pinch of chilli

steps.instruction: Heat the oil or butter in a wide frying pan over medium heat. If using onion, cook for 3 minutes until softened. Add the diced peppers and cook for 3-4 minutes.
Add the tomatoes, salt, and Aleppo pepper. Cook for 8-10 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the tomatoes break down into a thick sauce and the liquid reduces.

Reduce heat to low. Crack the eggs directly into the pan and gently stir them through the tomato-pepper mixture with a wooden spoon. Cook for 2-3 minutes, folding gently, until the eggs are just set but still creamy and soft. Do not overcook.
Remove from heat immediately (the eggs will continue to cook from residual heat). Season with black pepper. Serve straight from the pan with plenty of crusty bread.

image_placeholder: Menemen in a copper pan with soft scrambled eggs folded through a red tomato and green pepper sauce, served with crusty bread.
tips: The key is not overcooking the eggs — remove from heat while they still look slightly underdone.
Turkish sivri biber peppers give the most authentic flavour; Italian banana peppers are a good substitute.
Some regions add white cheese (beyaz peynir) on top — crumbled feta works well.

title: Mantı
description: Mantı are tiny Turkish dumplings traditionally from Kayseri in central Anatolia. Small squares of thin dough are filled with a seasoned lamb or beef mixture, sealed into small pouches, par-baked until golden, then boiled until tender. They are served with a trio of sauces: garlicky yoghurt, spiced red pepper oil with dried mint, and optionally a drizzle of sumac. The key to authentic mantı is making them very small — traditionally, forty should fit on a single spoon, though home versions are larger.

ingredients.name: Turkish pepper paste or tomato paste

steps.instruction: Make the dough: sift flour and salt, make a well, add beaten egg, water, and oil. Mix to form a firm dough. Knead 5-8 minutes until smooth and elastic. Wrap and rest in the fridge for 30 minutes.
Prepare the filling: mix the minced meat with grated onion (squeeze out excess liquid), salt, and pepper.

Preheat oven to 180°C. Roll dough very thinly (about 1mm) and cut into 3cm squares. Place a tiny amount of filling (chickpea-sized) in the centre of each. Pinch opposite corners together to seal into small pouches.
Place mantı on a greased baking tray. Par-bake for 8-10 minutes until lightly golden.
Bring a large pot of salted water to a boil. Gently add the par-baked mantı and simmer for 8-10 minutes until they float and the dough is tender. Drain and toss with a drizzle of oil.
Mix yoghurt with garlic and a pinch of salt. In a small pan, heat the olive oil, add pepper paste, Aleppo pepper, dried mint, and sumac. Cook 1 minute until fragrant.
Arrange mantı on a warm plate, spoon garlic yoghurt over generously, then drizzle the spiced oil. Serve immediately.

image_placeholder: Tiny Turkish mantı dumplings on a warm plate, covered in garlic yoghurt and drizzled with red spiced pepper oil, garnished with dried mint.
wine_pairing_notes: A Turkish Kalecik Karası red or a light Pinot Noir.
tips: The smaller the mantı, the more authentic — aim for 2-3cm squares.
Par-baking before boiling gives mantı a slightly firmer texture and prevents them from falling apart.
Mantı can be frozen after par-baking — boil directly from frozen, adding 2-3 extra minutes.

title: Mercimek Çorbası
description: Mercimek çorbası is Turkey's most beloved soup — a velvety, warming red lentil soup that is served at virtually every restaurant and home across the country. Red lentils are simmered with onion, carrot, potato, and tomato paste until completely soft, then blended until silky smooth. The soup is finished with a generous squeeze of lemon juice and a drizzle of Aleppo pepper-infused butter. Simple, nourishing, and deeply satisfying.

steps.instruction: Melt the butter in a large pot over medium heat. Add the onion and cook for 3 minutes until softened. Add the carrot and potato, cook 2 more minutes.
Stir in the tomato paste and cumin, cooking for 1 minute until fragrant. Add the rinsed lentils, water, salt, and pepper. Bring to a boil.
Reduce heat to medium-low, cover, and simmer for 25-30 minutes until the lentils and vegetables are completely soft.
Blend the soup until very smooth using a stick blender or food processor. Adjust consistency with water if needed. Taste and adjust seasoning.
For the topping, melt the remaining butter in a small pan and stir in the Aleppo pepper for 30 seconds. Serve the soup with a squeeze of lemon juice, a drizzle of the pepper butter, and crusty bread.

image_placeholder: A bowl of velvety orange-red Turkish lentil soup drizzled with red pepper butter, served with lemon wedges and crusty bread.
tips: The potato adds body and creaminess — do not skip it.
Lemon juice is essential — squeeze it in just before eating for the brightest flavour.
The pepper butter topping is traditional and transforms the soup from good to exceptional.

title: Gözleme
description: Gözleme are thin, handmade Turkish flatbreads filled with various ingredients and cooked on a convex griddle called a saç. The dough is rolled paper-thin and folded around fillings such as spinach and cheese, minced lamb, or potato. Cooked on a hot dry griddle until blistered and golden, gözleme are a beloved Turkish street food, often made by village women at markets. This spinach and cheese version is the most popular.

steps.instruction: Make the dough: combine flour and salt. Add water and oil, mix to form a soft dough. Knead 5 minutes until smooth. Divide into 4 balls, coat with a little oil, cover and rest 30 minutes.
Wilt the spinach in a pan for 2 minutes. Drain and squeeze dry. Mix with crumbled cheese, onion, Aleppo pepper, and a pinch of salt.
On a lightly floured surface, roll each dough ball as thin as possible — almost translucent. Spread a quarter of the filling over one half of the dough.
Fold the dough over the filling, then fold in the edges to create a sealed rectangle or half-moon shape.
Cook on a dry, very hot frying pan or griddle for 2-3 minutes per side, brushing with melted butter while cooking, until golden and blistered on both sides.
Cut into pieces and serve hot with a squeeze of lemon.

image_placeholder: Golden, blistered gözleme on a wooden board, cut to reveal the spinach and white cheese filling inside.
wine_pairing_notes: A glass of ayran (salted yoghurt drink) is the traditional accompaniment.
tips: Roll the dough as thin as possible — it should be almost see-through.
Cook on a very hot dry griddle for the best blisters and char.
Gözleme are best eaten immediately while hot and crispy.

title: Çılbır
title_en: Turkish Poached Eggs with Garlic Yoghurt
description: Çılbır is one of the world's oldest egg dishes, dating back to the 15th century Ottoman court. Perfectly poached eggs are nestled on a bed of thick, garlicky yoghurt, then finished with a sizzling drizzle of Aleppo pepper-infused butter or olive oil. The combination of warm, runny egg yolk mixing with cool, tangy yoghurt and spicy, fragrant oil is revelatory. A simple dish of extraordinary elegance, it has recently gained international popularity as a sophisticated brunch option.

ingredients.name: butter or extra virgin olive oil

substitutions.substitute: sweet paprika plus pinch of chilli flakes

steps.instruction: Prepare the yoghurt: mix the room-temperature yoghurt with the minced garlic and a pinch of salt. Divide between 2 serving bowls or plates, spreading it to create a bed for the eggs.

Poach the eggs: bring a saucepan of water to a gentle simmer, add vinegar. Create a gentle whirlpool. Crack eggs one at a time into a small sieve (to drain thin whites), then slide into the water. Poach for 2-3 minutes until whites are set but yolks are still runny. Remove with a slotted spoon and drain on kitchen paper.
While the eggs poach, heat the butter or olive oil in a small pan. Add the Aleppo pepper flakes and swirl for 30 seconds until fragrant and the oil turns red. Remove from heat.
Place 2 poached eggs on each bed of garlicky yoghurt. Immediately drizzle the hot pepper butter over the eggs and yoghurt. Garnish with fresh dill or mint. Serve at once with crusty bread.

image_placeholder: Two perfectly poached eggs on a bed of white garlic yoghurt, drizzled with red Aleppo pepper butter, garnished with fresh dill, in a rustic bowl.
tips: Bring the yoghurt to room temperature before serving — cold yoghurt against hot eggs creates an unpleasant contrast.
Use the freshest eggs possible — they hold their shape much better when poaching.
The pepper butter should be sizzling hot when drizzled over — the sound and aroma are part of the experience.

title: Sigara Böreği
description: Sigara böreği are crispy, cigar-shaped pastries made from yufka or filo dough, filled with a mixture of white cheese, parsley, and sometimes dill. They are a cornerstone of Turkish breakfast spreads and mezze tables. The thin pastry is rolled tightly around the filling and fried until golden and crunchy, creating an irresistible contrast between the shattering exterior and the soft, tangy cheese inside. Quick to prepare and even quicker to disappear.

steps.instruction: Mix the crumbled cheese with chopped parsley, dill if using, and black pepper.
Cut each filo sheet into strips about 10cm wide and 30cm long. Place a tablespoon of filling at one end of each strip.
Roll up tightly like a cigar, tucking in the sides as you go. Seal the end with a little beaten egg. Repeat with remaining strips.
Heat the oil to 170°C in a deep pan. Fry the böreks in batches for 2-3 minutes, turning once, until golden and crispy all over. Drain on kitchen paper.
Serve hot, with a glass of Turkish tea.

image_placeholder: Crispy golden sigara böreği arranged on a plate with a cup of Turkish tea and fresh herbs.
wine_pairing_notes: Turkish tea is the traditional and best accompaniment. Otherwise, a crisp white wine.
tips: Roll them as tightly as possible — loose böreks puff up and burst when frying.
Fry at 170°C — too hot and the outside burns before the cheese melts; too low and they absorb oil.
Can be assembled ahead and frozen — fry directly from frozen, adding 1-2 minutes.

title: Künefe
description: Künefe is a beloved Turkish dessert from the Hatay province, made from shredded kadayıf pastry layered around stretchy, unsalted cheese, then soaked in sugar syrup. Unlike knafeh which is baked, traditional Hatay künefe is cooked in a special round copper pan on the stovetop, creating a deeply caramelised, crunchy base. It is served immediately with a scattering of ground pistachios and clotted cream (kaymak). The key is the cheese — fresh, unsalted, stretchy dil peyniri or a similar cheese that melts into long, elastic strands.

substitutions.notes: closest available substitute for stretchy texture

steps.instruction: Make syrup: combine sugar, water, and lemon juice. Boil then simmer 8 minutes. Cool completely.
Shred the kadayıf into shorter strands. Toss with melted butter until every strand is coated.
Press half the buttered kadayıf into a round, oven-safe copper or cast-iron pan (about 22cm). Spread the cheese evenly on top. Cover with remaining kadayıf, pressing down firmly.
Cook on medium heat on the stovetop for 8-10 minutes until the base is deep golden and caramelised. Alternatively, bake at 180°C for 25 minutes.
Invert onto a plate. Pour cold syrup over the hot künefe. Garnish with ground pistachios. Serve immediately.

image_placeholder: A round künefe on a plate with golden caramelised top, stretchy cheese visible at the edges, garnished with bright green ground pistachios.
wine_pairing_notes: A glass of Turkish tea or a dessert wine.
tips: Must be served immediately — künefe waits for no one. The cheese becomes rubbery as it cools.
Fresh, unsalted stretchy cheese is essential — aged or salted cheese will not work.
Cold syrup on hot pastry is the rule — never warm on warm.

title: Sütlaç
description: Sütlaç is Turkey's beloved baked rice pudding — a comfort dessert found in every muhallebici (milk pudding shop) and restaurant across the country. Unlike many other rice puddings, sütlaç is first cooked on the stovetop until thick and creamy, then baked until a beautiful dark-brown caramelised skin forms on top. This skin is the hallmark of a good sütlaç. Served chilled in the individual earthenware bowls it was baked in, it is creamy, subtly sweet, and deeply comforting.

steps.instruction: Cook the rice in the water over medium heat for 15 minutes until the rice is very soft and has absorbed all the water, stirring occasionally.
Add the milk and sugar. Bring to a gentle simmer, stirring frequently, and cook for 20 minutes until the mixture has thickened.
Stir in the cornflour mixture, vanilla, and rose water if using. Cook for another 3-4 minutes, stirring constantly, until thick enough to coat the back of a spoon.
Pour into individual oven-safe dishes or ramekins. Place under a hot grill for 5-8 minutes until the top develops a dark caramelised skin. Watch carefully to prevent burning.
Allow to cool to room temperature, then refrigerate for at least 2 hours until set and chilled. Serve cold with a dusting of cinnamon.

image_placeholder: Individual earthenware bowls of sütlaç with a dark caramelised top, served chilled with a dusting of cinnamon.
wine_pairing_notes: Turkish tea or a chilled glass of sweet Muscat.
tips: The caramelised top under the grill is what makes sütlaç special — do not skip this step.
Stir frequently while cooking to prevent the milk from scorching on the bottom.
Use short-grain rice (like Arborio) for the creamiest result — long-grain rice stays too separate.

title: Djaj M'qualli
title_en: Chicken Tagine with Preserved Lemons and Olives
description: This is Morocco's most iconic tagine — chicken braised low and slow with saffron, ginger, and onions until falling off the bone, then finished with briny preserved lemons and cracked green olives. The sauce is rich, golden, and deeply fragrant from the saffron and smen (aged butter). Traditionally cooked in a conical clay tagine, the dish produces a luscious sauce that demands plenty of bread for soaking. The preserved lemons add a unique, intensely citrusy and salty dimension found nowhere else in world cuisine.

substitutions.notes: colour only, not the same flavour

ingredients.name: salt
ingredients.notes: adjust as preserved lemons are salty

steps.instruction: In a tagine or heavy-bottomed pot, heat the olive oil and butter over medium heat. Add the grated onion, garlic, ginger, saffron with its water, turmeric, cinnamon, and salt. Cook for 5 minutes until fragrant.
Add the chicken pieces, turning to coat in the spice mixture. Add the water. Bring to a simmer, cover, and cook on low heat for 45-50 minutes, turning the chicken halfway, until very tender.
Remove the chicken and set aside. Increase heat and reduce the sauce for 10 minutes until thickened and golden.
Return the chicken to the pot. Add the preserved lemon rinds and olives. Simmer uncovered for 10 more minutes.
Garnish with fresh coriander. Serve directly from the tagine with crusty Moroccan bread or couscous.

image_placeholder: A golden chicken tagine with preserved lemon rinds and green olives in a traditional clay tagine pot, garnished with coriander.
wine_pairing_notes: A Moroccan Gris rosé from Meknès or a dry Gewürztraminer complements the saffron and preserved lemon beautifully.
tips: Use genuine preserved lemons — they provide a unique flavour impossible to replicate with fresh lemons.
The sauce should be reduced until thick and golden; a watery sauce is a common mistake.
Smen (aged Moroccan butter) adds an authentic depth, but regular butter works well.

title: Tagine d'Agneau aux Pruneaux
description: This luxurious Marrakchi tagine combines tender braised lamb shoulder with sweet, plump prunes, toasted almonds, and warm spices including cinnamon, ginger, and saffron. The interplay of savoury meat with sweet fruit is a hallmark of Moroccan cuisine, reflecting centuries of Arab-Andalusian culinary influence. The prunes are added towards the end to retain their shape while imparting their honeyed sweetness to the sauce. Finished with a drizzle of honey and a scattering of sesame seeds.

steps.instruction: Heat olive oil in a tagine or heavy pot. Brown the lamb in batches over high heat. Remove and set aside.
Reduce heat to medium. Add onions and cook for 8 minutes until softened. Add garlic, ginger, cinnamon, ras el hanout, and saffron. Cook 2 minutes until fragrant.
Return lamb to the pot. Add water, salt, and 1 tbsp honey. Bring to a simmer, cover, and cook on low heat for 1.5 hours until the lamb is very tender.
Add the prunes and remaining honey. Cook uncovered for 20 minutes until the sauce thickens and the prunes are plump and glossy.
Garnish with toasted almonds and sesame seeds. Serve with couscous or crusty bread.

image_placeholder: A rich lamb tagine with glossy prunes and toasted almonds in a traditional tagine pot, sprinkled with sesame seeds.
wine_pairing_notes: A rich, spice-noted Moroccan red from the Meknès region or a Châteauneuf-du-Pape.
tips: Brown the lamb well for depth of flavour — do not crowd the pan.
Add prunes only in the last 20 minutes so they hold their shape.
The sauce should be thick and glossy, not watery — reduce uncovered if needed.

title: Couscous aux Sept Légumes
description: Couscous with seven vegetables is Morocco's quintessential Friday lunch dish — a tradition that brings families together weekly across the country. A fragrant broth of lamb or chicken is laden with seven seasonal vegetables including courgettes, carrots, turnips, pumpkin, cabbage, tomatoes, and chickpeas. The steamed couscous is mounded on a large communal platter, the vegetables artfully arranged on top, and the broth ladled around. The number seven is considered auspicious in Moroccan culture.

steps.instruction: In a large couscoussier or stockpot, heat oil. Brown the meat pieces. Add onion, ginger, turmeric, saffron, ras el hanout, coriander bunch, salt, and water. Bring to a boil, reduce to a simmer, cover, and cook 45 minutes.
Add the harder vegetables — carrots, turnips, and pumpkin. Continue simmering for 20 minutes.
Add the softer vegetables — courgettes, cabbage, tomatoes, and chickpeas. Simmer for another 15-20 minutes until all vegetables are tender.

Meanwhile, prepare the couscous: place in a bowl, add a pinch of salt and drizzle of olive oil. Pour over an equal volume of boiling broth from the stew. Cover and let stand 10 minutes. Fluff with a fork, add butter, and fluff again.

Mound the couscous on a large serving platter. Arrange the vegetables artfully on top, then the meat. Ladle some broth around the base. Serve extra broth in a bowl alongside, with harissa on the side.

image_placeholder: A grand platter of steamed couscous mounded with colourful vegetables — orange pumpkin, green courgettes, white turnips — and braised meat, with broth ladled around the edges.
wine_pairing_notes: A Moroccan red from the Guerrouane or Meknès region, or a spicy Grenache-based blend.
tips: Add vegetables in stages based on cooking time — harder vegetables first, softer ones later.
The broth should be flavourful enough to drink on its own — taste and adjust seasoning.
Traditionally served on a communal platter with everyone eating from their section towards the centre.

title: Harira
description: Harira is Morocco's most famous soup — a rich, fragrant tomato-based soup loaded with lentils, chickpeas, and sometimes lamb or beef, thickened with a tedouira (flour and water slurry) and brightened with fresh herbs. It is the traditional soup served to break the fast during Ramadan, though it is enjoyed year-round. The soup is deeply nourishing and complex in flavour, with a silky, slightly thick consistency. Traditionally served with dates and chebakia (honey cookies) during Ramadan.

steps.instruction: Heat oil in a large pot. If using meat, brown it for 5 minutes. Add onion and celery, cook 5 minutes until softened.
Add all spices and cook 1 minute. Add tomatoes, tomato paste, lentils, chickpeas, half the herbs, salt, and water. Bring to a boil.
Reduce to a simmer, cover, and cook for 45-50 minutes until lentils are very soft.

Mix the flour with water to make the tedouira. Stir gradually into the soup while simmering. Add the broken vermicelli and cook 5 more minutes until the soup is slightly thickened and the pasta is tender.
Stir in the remaining fresh herbs. Serve with a squeeze of lemon juice and dates on the side.

image_placeholder: A bowl of rich, russet-coloured harira soup garnished with fresh coriander and a lemon wedge, served with dates.
tips: The tedouira (flour-water thickener) gives harira its characteristic silky body.
Fresh lemon juice squeezed in at serving is essential — it brightens all the flavours.
Harira improves overnight — make a large batch and reheat the next day.

title: Bastilla (Pastilla)
description: Bastilla is Morocco's most spectacular dish — an ornate pie of shredded, spiced chicken, lemony egg custard, and sweetened toasted almonds, all wrapped in layers of crispy warqa pastry and dusted with icing sugar and cinnamon. This sweet-savoury masterpiece from Fez was originally reserved for royalty and weddings. The combination of textures and flavours — crispy, creamy, sweet, savoury, spiced — in a single bite is unforgettable. Traditionally made with pigeon, the chicken version is now standard for home cooking.

steps.instruction: In a large pan, cook onions in 30g butter until soft. Add garlic, ginger, cinnamon, turmeric, saffron, salt, and chicken. Pour in stock. Cover and simmer 25 minutes until chicken is cooked. Remove chicken and shred.
Reduce the cooking liquid by half over high heat. Lower heat, pour in beaten eggs, stirring gently to create a loose, custardy mixture with the herbs. Set aside.
Toast almonds in a dry pan until golden. Roughly chop and mix with 40g icing sugar, remaining cinnamon, and orange blossom water.

Preheat oven to 180°C. Brush a 28cm round baking dish with melted butter. Layer 6 filo sheets, brushing each with butter, letting edges overhang. Spread the almond mixture, then the shredded chicken, then the egg mixture.
Fold overhanging filo over the filling. Layer remaining 6 sheets on top, tucking edges under. Brush generously with butter.
Bake for 25-30 minutes until deep golden. Invert onto a plate. Dust generously with remaining icing sugar and create a decorative cinnamon lattice on top.

image_placeholder: A golden bastilla dusted with icing sugar and cinnamon lattice, with one slice removed revealing layers of chicken, egg, and almond filling.
wine_pairing_notes: A Moroccan Gris rosé or an off-dry Riesling balances the sweet-savoury combination.
tips: The sweet-savoury combination is intentional and essential — do not omit the sugar and cinnamon on top.
Reduce the cooking liquid well before adding eggs — too much liquid makes a soggy pie.
Can be assembled a day ahead and baked just before serving.

title: Zaalouk
description: Zaalouk is a beloved Moroccan cooked salad of aubergines and tomatoes, smoky and richly spiced with cumin, paprika, and garlic. The aubergines are traditionally charred or boiled until soft, then mashed and cooked down with a tomato sauce until thick and jammy. Served warm or at room temperature with plenty of bread, zaalouk is a cornerstone of the Moroccan salad course that begins every meal.

steps.instruction: Peel and dice the aubergines. Boil in salted water for 15 minutes until very soft. Drain and mash roughly with a fork.
In a wide pan, heat olive oil. Add garlic, cumin, and paprika. Cook 1 minute.
Add the tomatoes and cook for 10 minutes until they break down into a thick sauce.
Add the mashed aubergine. Cook, stirring occasionally, for 10-15 minutes until the mixture is thick, jammy, and the flavours have melded.
Stir in lemon juice, coriander, and cayenne if using. Serve warm or at room temperature with crusty bread.

image_placeholder: A bowl of thick, jammy zaalouk with visible aubergine and tomato pieces, garnished with fresh coriander and served with Moroccan bread.
wine_pairing_notes: A dry Moroccan rosé or a light, chilled Grenache.
tips: Cook the zaalouk until thick and jammy — it should not be watery.
For extra smokiness, char the aubergines over a flame before mashing.
Zaalouk tastes even better the next day as flavours develop.

title: Rfissa
description: Rfissa is a traditional Moroccan celebratory dish, particularly associated with the birth of a child. Tender, spiced chicken is cooked with lentils in a fragrant fenugreek-scented broth, then served over torn pieces of msemmen (Moroccan flaky flatbread). The combination of the soft, sauce-soaked bread, tender chicken, and earthy lentils in a warming, saffron-laced broth is deeply comforting. Fenugreek, believed to aid lactation, gives the dish its distinctive slightly bitter, maple-like aroma.

ingredients.name: fenugreek seeds
ingredients.notes: soaked in hot water for 1 hour, drained

steps.instruction: In a large pot, heat oil. Add onion, saffron, ginger, turmeric, pepper, drained fenugreek, and salt. Cook 3 minutes.
Add chicken, turning to coat. Add water, bring to a boil, reduce heat, cover and simmer 40 minutes.
Add the lentils and continue cooking for 25 minutes until lentils and chicken are tender.
Tear the msemmen into pieces and arrange on a serving platter. Place the chicken on top, pour the lentils and broth over. Garnish with coriander.

image_placeholder: Rfissa served on a large platter with tender chicken pieces over torn flatbread, lentils, and a golden fenugreek-scented broth.
tips: Soak the fenugreek seeds in hot water for at least 1 hour — this mellows the bitterness.
The bread should soak up the broth and become soft — this is the intended texture.
Rfissa is traditionally made for new mothers, as fenugreek is believed to aid lactation.

title: Msemmen
description: Msemmen are Morocco's beloved square, flaky flatbreads — a staple at every breakfast table and tea time. The dough is made from a simple mixture of flour, semolina, and water, then stretched paper-thin, brushed with oil and butter, folded into square layers, and cooked on a griddle. The folding technique creates dozens of flaky, buttery layers reminiscent of puff pastry. They can be eaten plain with honey and butter, or stuffed with a savoury filling.

steps.instruction: Mix flour, semolina, salt, and sugar. Gradually add warm water, kneading for 8-10 minutes until very smooth and slightly sticky.
Divide into 8 balls. Oil your hands and the work surface. Let rest 15 minutes.

Mix softened butter with oil. Take one ball and stretch it on an oiled surface as thin as possible — almost translucent. Smear with butter-oil mixture. Fold the sides in to form a square. Repeat the stretching and folding once more for extra layers.
Cook on a dry griddle or frying pan over medium heat for 2-3 minutes per side until golden and flaky. Press gently with a spatula while cooking.

image_placeholder: A stack of golden, flaky msemmen on a traditional Moroccan plate with a pot of honey and a glass of mint tea.
tips: The dough must be very smooth and slightly sticky for the best stretching.
Oil your hands and work surface generously when stretching — the dough should be almost transparent.
Cook on medium heat — too hot and the outside burns before the layers separate and cook through.

title: Briouats au Poulet
description: Briouats are Morocco's elegant little filo pastry parcels, filled with a variety of savoury or sweet fillings. This chicken version features shredded chicken, onions, and herbs spiced with ras el hanout, wrapped in triangular or cigar-shaped warqa pastry and fried until golden and crispy. They are a popular starter at special occasions and a standard feature of the Moroccan appetiser course. Light, crispy, and deeply flavourful, they disappear quickly from any table.

steps.instruction: Poach the chicken thighs in simmered water for 15 minutes until cooked. Shred finely.
Heat olive oil. Sauté onion 5 minutes until soft. Add spices, cook 1 minute. Add shredded chicken and herbs. Pour in beaten eggs and stir until eggs form a loose scramble with the chicken. Cool.
Cut filo sheets into strips about 8cm wide. Place a tablespoon of filling at one end. Fold corner over to form a triangle and continue folding flag-style to the end. Seal with a dab of water.
Heat frying oil to 170°C. Fry briouats in batches for 2-3 minutes, turning once, until golden all over. Drain on kitchen paper.
Serve hot, optionally with a squeeze of lemon.

image_placeholder: Golden, crispy briouats arranged on a plate with a small bowl of dipping sauce.
wine_pairing_notes: A Moroccan Gris rosé or a crisp Sauvignon Blanc.
tips: Keep filo covered with a damp towel while working to prevent drying.
Let the filling cool completely before wrapping — hot filling makes filo soggy.
Can be assembled and frozen — fry from frozen, adding 1-2 extra minutes.

title: Shakshuka à la Marocaine
description: Moroccan shakshuka is a vibrant dish of eggs poached in a spiced tomato and pepper sauce, enhanced with cumin, paprika, and preserved lemon — distinguishing it from the Tunisian and Israeli versions. The sauce is cooked until thick and concentrated before the eggs are gently cracked in and covered to steam. Served directly from the pan with crusty Moroccan bread for scooping, it is a beloved breakfast, brunch, or light supper throughout Morocco.

steps.instruction: Heat olive oil in a wide ovenproof frying pan. Cook onion and pepper for 8 minutes until soft. Add garlic and spices, cook 1 minute.
Add tomatoes, preserved lemon if using, and salt. Simmer for 15 minutes until thick and concentrated.
Make 4 wells in the sauce. Crack an egg into each well. Cover with a lid and cook on low heat for 5-7 minutes until whites are set but yolks are still runny.
Garnish with fresh coriander and serve immediately from the pan with crusty bread.

image_placeholder: A cast-iron pan of Moroccan shakshuka with four eggs nestled in a thick, red tomato and pepper sauce, garnished with fresh coriander.
tips: The sauce must be thick before adding eggs — watery sauce = undercooked whites.
Cover the pan to steam the eggs rather than stirring them in.
Preserved lemon is the Moroccan twist that makes this version special.

title: Kefta Tagine with Eggs
description: Kefta tagine is a beloved Moroccan comfort dish of spiced lamb or beef meatballs simmered in a rich, cumin-scented tomato sauce with eggs poached on top. Found in every neighbourhood restaurant across Morocco, it is simple to prepare yet deeply satisfying. The meatballs are flavoured with cumin, paprika, and coriander, while the sauce is enriched with tomatoes and herbs. The eggs, cracked on top at the end, create a beautiful contrast as their yolks break into the spicy sauce.

steps.instruction: Make meatballs: mix the mince with grated onion, half the cumin, half the paprika, half the coriander, salt, and pepper. Roll into walnut-sized balls.
Heat oil in a tagine or oven-safe pan. Cook sauce onion for 5 minutes. Add garlic, remaining cumin and paprika, cook 1 minute. Add tomatoes and simmer 10 minutes.
Nestle the meatballs into the sauce. Cover and simmer for 20 minutes until the meatballs are cooked through and the sauce is thick.
Make wells between the meatballs and crack an egg into each. Cover and cook 5-7 minutes until egg whites are set.
Garnish with remaining coriander. Serve from the pan with crusty bread.

image_placeholder: A tagine of spiced meatballs in tomato sauce with four poached eggs on top, garnished with coriander, served with bread.
wine_pairing_notes: A medium-bodied Moroccan red or a spicy Tempranillo.
tips: Keep the meatballs small — walnut-sized — for the best texture and even cooking.
The sauce should be quite thick before adding the eggs.
Break the runny yolks into the sauce while eating — this is the best part.

title: Chebakia
description: Chebakia are Morocco's iconic flower-shaped fried pastries, traditionally prepared for Ramadan. The dough is flavoured with a stunning blend of anise, cinnamon, saffron, orange blossom water, and toasted sesame seeds, then shaped into intricate flower patterns, deep-fried until golden, and immediately dipped in warm honey. The result is a crispy, fragrant cookie that shatters into flaky layers of honeyed sweetness. Making chebakia is a communal activity — families often gather to shape hundreds in the days before Ramadan.

steps.instruction: Mix flour, ground toasted sesame, ground almonds, cinnamon, and anise. Add melted butter, olive oil, beaten egg, orange blossom water, vinegar, saffron with its water, and enough warm water to form a smooth, pliable dough. Knead 10 minutes. Rest 30 minutes.

Roll the dough about 3mm thick. Cut into 10cm strips. Using a pastry wheel, make parallel cuts in each strip. Shape each strip into a flower by pulling and folding the cut edges through each other. This takes practice.
Heat the frying oil to 165°C. Fry the chebakia in batches, 2-3 at a time, for 3-4 minutes, turning once, until golden brown. Do not let them darken too much.
Meanwhile, warm the honey in a wide pan over low heat. Immediately dip each hot chebakia into the warm honey, turning to coat. Let excess honey drip off and transfer to a rack.
Sprinkle with toasted sesame seeds while still sticky. Allow to cool and set before serving.

image_placeholder: A plate of golden, flower-shaped chebakia glistening with honey and studded with sesame seeds, arranged on a traditional Moroccan tray.
wine_pairing_notes: Moroccan mint tea is the only appropriate accompaniment.
tips: The shaping technique takes practice — watch videos or have a Moroccan grandmother show you.
Fry at a moderate temperature (165°C) — too hot and they brown outside but stay raw inside.
Dip in warm (not hot) honey immediately after frying — the heat helps absorption.

title: Falafel
description: Falafel are the Middle East's most iconic street food — crispy, golden fritters made from soaked raw chickpeas (never cooked) that are ground with fresh herbs, onion, garlic, and spices, then deep-fried until the interior turns a beautiful vivid green. Egyptian falafel (ta'amiya) traditionally use broad beans, but the chickpea version from the Levant has conquered the world. The key to perfect falafel is using dried chickpeas soaked overnight — never tinned — and frying at the right temperature for a crunchy exterior and fluffy, herb-flecked interior.

steps.instruction: Drain the soaked chickpeas very well. Add to a food processor with the parsley, coriander, onion, garlic, cumin, ground coriander, cayenne, and salt. Pulse until finely ground but NOT a smooth paste — some texture should remain.
Transfer to a bowl. Stir in the bicarbonate of soda. Refrigerate for 30 minutes to firm up. If too wet, add a tablespoon of flour.
Shape into balls or patties (about 35g each). Dip the tops in sesame seeds if using.
Heat oil to 180°C. Fry the falafel in batches, without crowding, for 3-4 minutes until deep golden brown and crispy. Drain on kitchen paper.
Serve in pitta bread with tahini sauce, pickled turnips, fresh vegetables, and hot sauce.

image_placeholder: A pile of golden, crispy falafel on a plate with tahini sauce, pickled vegetables, and fresh herbs in a pitta bread pocket.
wine_pairing_notes: A crisp, dry white wine or a cold lager. Arak is also traditional.
tips: NEVER use cooked or tinned chickpeas — they will not hold together and the texture will be mushy.
The mixture should be moist but holdable — if it falls apart, add a tablespoon of flour.
Fry at exactly 180°C — too cool and they absorb oil; too hot and the outside burns before the inside cooks.

title: Shawarma Djaj
description: Chicken shawarma is the Middle East's most popular street food — marinated chicken stacked on a vertical rotisserie and slowly roasted, then shaved into thin, juicy strips. At home, the same flavour is achieved by marinating chicken thighs in a blend of yoghurt, lemon, cumin, turmeric, paprika, cinnamon, and cardamom, then roasting or grilling at high heat. The chicken is typically served in a pitta or laffa wrap with garlic sauce (toum), pickled turnips, tahini, and fresh vegetables.

steps.instruction: Mix all marinade ingredients. Add chicken and coat thoroughly. Refrigerate for at least 4 hours, ideally overnight.
Preheat oven to 220°C or prepare a very hot grill. Remove chicken from marinade and place skin-side up on a wire rack over a baking tray.
Roast for 25-30 minutes until the skin is deeply charred and the chicken is cooked through (75°C internal). Alternatively, grill over high heat for 6-7 minutes per side.
Rest for 5 minutes, then slice into thin strips. Serve in warm pitta or laffa with garlic sauce, tahini, pickled turnips, and salad.

image_placeholder: Sliced chicken shawarma in a warm pitta wrap with garlic sauce, pickled turnips, and fresh vegetables.
wine_pairing_notes: A dry, chilled rosé or a cold Lebanese beer.
tips: Overnight marination makes a significant difference — plan ahead.
For the most authentic flavour at home, roast at the highest temperature possible with skin on for crispy edges.
The combination of toum (garlic sauce) and tahini is the classic duo for shawarma.

title: Maqluba
title_en: Upside-Down Rice with Chicken and Aubergine
description: Maqluba — meaning 'upside down' — is the showstopping dish of Palestinian cuisine. Layers of golden fried aubergine, tender chicken, and spiced rice are cooked together in a pot, then dramatically flipped onto a platter to reveal the beautiful layered cake of food. The moment of the flip is always accompanied by cheers and nervous anticipation. The dish is fragrant with cinnamon, allspice, and turmeric, and served with a simple yoghurt salad.

steps.instruction: Salt the aubergine slices and let stand 20 minutes. Pat dry. Fry in batches in oil until golden on both sides. Drain on kitchen paper.
In a separate pot, brown the chicken pieces on all sides. Add onion, garlic, spices, and stock. Simmer 25 minutes until chicken is cooked. Remove chicken and strain the stock.

In a heavy-bottomed pot, arrange the fried aubergine slices in a single layer on the bottom. Place the chicken on top. Cover with the drained, soaked rice. Pour the strained stock over — it should just cover the rice.
Bring to a boil, then reduce to the lowest heat. Cover tightly and cook for 20 minutes until the rice is done and liquid is absorbed.

Let rest covered for 10 minutes. Place a large serving platter on top and, in one confident movement, flip the pot upside down. Lift the pot away to reveal the layered dish. Garnish with toasted nuts.

image_placeholder: A maqluba flipped onto a large platter showing the golden aubergine layer on top with rice and chicken beneath, garnished with toasted pine nuts.
wine_pairing_notes: A medium-bodied red from the Levant or a rich Grenache.
tips: The flip is the moment of truth — use a platter larger than the pot and flip with confidence.
Fry the aubergine until truly golden — it needs to hold its shape at the base.
Let it rest 10 minutes before flipping — this helps everything set.

title: Fatteh
description: Fatteh is a magnificent Levantine dish of layered crispy pitta, warm chickpeas, and garlicky yoghurt, finished with toasted pine nuts and a drizzle of paprika-infused butter. This architectural dish from Damascus combines textures and temperatures — crunchy, creamy, warm, and cool — in every bite. It is a breakfast staple in Syria and Lebanon, particularly during Eid celebrations. Each component is simple, but the combination is far greater than the sum of its parts.

ingredients.name: pitta bread
ingredients.notes: torn into pieces and toasted or fried

steps.instruction: Toast or fry the pitta pieces until golden and crispy. Arrange in the bottom of a deep serving dish.
Warm the chickpeas with a little of their liquid and a pinch of cumin and salt. Spoon over the crispy pitta.
Mix the yoghurt with crushed garlic, tahini, lemon juice, and salt until smooth. Pour over the warm chickpeas.
Melt the butter, add paprika, and swirl for 30 seconds. Drizzle over the yoghurt layer along with the toasted pine nuts.
Serve immediately — the contrast of temperatures and textures is the point.

image_placeholder: A layered fatteh in a deep bowl showing crispy pitta, chickpeas, and white yoghurt, drizzled with red paprika butter and golden pine nuts.
tips: Assemble and serve immediately — the pitta should still be crunchy against the yoghurt.
Room-temperature yoghurt is important — cold yoghurt against warm chickpeas is jarring.
Fatteh is traditionally a breakfast or brunch dish, not a dinner one.

title: Musakhan
description: Musakhan is Palestine's national dish — roasted chicken rubbed with sumac and allspice, served on taboon bread piled with deeply caramelised onions swimming in olive oil and more sumac. The sheer quantity of onions (slow-cooked until dark and sweet) and the generous use of sumac create a dish that is hauntingly tangy, sweet, and savoury. Traditionally baked on taboon bread in a wood-fired oven, the bread soaks up all the juices to become a rich, flavourful base.

steps.instruction: Rub chicken with 2 tbsp sumac, allspice, cinnamon, salt, and a drizzle of oil. Marinate at least 1 hour.
Heat remaining olive oil in a large pan. Cook all the sliced onions over medium-low heat for 30-40 minutes until deeply caramelised and dark golden. Stir in remaining 2 tbsp sumac. Season with salt.
Preheat oven to 200°C. Place the taboon bread or pitta on a large baking tray. Pile the caramelised onions on top. Nestle the marinated chicken pieces into the onions.
Roast for 35-40 minutes until the chicken is golden and cooked through. Garnish with toasted pine nuts.
Serve on the bread, scooping up the sumac-soaked onions and chicken together.

image_placeholder: Musakhan — roasted sumac chicken on a bed of deeply caramelised onions on taboon bread, garnished with toasted pine nuts.
wine_pairing_notes: A Palestinian or Jordanian red wine, or a Syrah-based blend.
tips: Do not skimp on the sumac — 4 tablespoons is correct and essential.
The onions must be deeply caramelised over 30-40 minutes — this is non-negotiable.
The bread soaking up the juices is the whole point — do not place it on a rack.

title: Shorbat Freekeh
description: Freekeh soup is a deeply nourishing Syrian soup made with roasted green wheat (freekeh), which has a distinctive smoky, nutty flavour. The cracked freekeh is simmered in a rich chicken broth until tender, creating a warming, earthy soup that is a staple of Syrian home cooking. Often garnished with toasted pine nuts and served during Ramadan or at celebrations, freekeh soup is simple to make but profoundly satisfying.

steps.instruction: Heat oil in a large pot. Add onion and cook 3 minutes. Add chicken, water, cinnamon, allspice, and salt. Bring to a boil, skim foam, and simmer 30 minutes.
Remove chicken, shred the meat, and discard bones and onion. Return shredded meat to the broth.
Add the rinsed freekeh. Simmer for 20-25 minutes until freekeh is tender.
Serve in bowls, garnished with toasted pine nuts and lemon wedges.

image_placeholder: A bowl of earthy freekeh soup with shredded chicken, topped with toasted pine nuts and a lemon wedge.
tips: Use cracked freekeh for soup — whole freekeh takes much longer to cook.
The smoky flavour of freekeh is what makes this soup special — do not substitute with plain wheat.
A squeeze of lemon just before eating brightens all the flavours.

title: Muhammara
description: Muhammara is a stunning, smoky-sweet red pepper and walnut dip from Aleppo, Syria. Roasted red peppers are blended with toasted walnuts, breadcrumbs, pomegranate molasses, Aleppo pepper, and cumin to create a dip of extraordinary depth and complexity. The colour is a deep, vibrant red, and the flavour balances sweet, smoky, nutty, and tangy notes. It is a staple of the Levantine mezze table and can also serve as a sauce for grilled meats.

ingredients.name: roasted red peppers
ingredients.notes: about 400g, from a jar or freshly roasted

substitutions.substitute: balsamic reduction with a squeeze of lemon

steps.instruction: Add the roasted peppers, toasted walnuts, breadcrumbs, pomegranate molasses, Aleppo pepper, cumin, garlic, lemon juice, and salt to a food processor.
Pulse until you have a coarse paste — not completely smooth. Some texture from the walnuts should remain.
With the processor running, drizzle in the olive oil. Taste and adjust seasoning.
Spread on a plate, drizzle with more olive oil, and scatter with a few walnut pieces. Serve with pitta or as a sauce for grilled meat.

image_placeholder: A vibrant red muhammara dip on a plate, drizzled with olive oil and scattered with walnut pieces, served with pitta bread.
wine_pairing_notes: A dry rosé or a light, fruity red wine like a Carignan.
tips: Toast the walnuts lightly for the best flavour — raw walnuts taste flat.
The quality of the pomegranate molasses matters hugely — look for a thick, dark variety.
Do not over-process — the chunky texture is part of muhammara's appeal.

title: Maftoul Falastini
description: Maftoul is Palestine's hand-rolled couscous — larger and more rustic than Moroccan couscous, with a distinctive chewy texture. The pearls are traditionally hand-rolled from cracked wheat and flour, then steamed over a spiced chickpea and chicken stew. This labour of love is a centrepiece of Palestinian celebrations and family gatherings. The flavour is deeply earthy and satisfying, with the steamed maftoul absorbing the aromatic broth.

steps.instruction: In a large pot, heat oil and brown the chicken. Add onion, spices, salt, and water. Bring to a boil, skim, and simmer 40 minutes.
Add chickpeas and cook 10 more minutes. Remove the chicken and keep warm.
Prepare the maftoul according to package directions — typically steaming over the broth for 15-20 minutes, or cooking in salted water.
Fluff the maftoul with a little olive oil. Mound on a platter, arrange chicken and chickpeas on top, and ladle broth around. Serve with yoghurt.

image_placeholder: A platter of Palestinian maftoul with chicken and chickpeas, with broth ladled around the edges.
wine_pairing_notes: A medium-bodied red from the Levant.
tips: If you cannot find maftoul, moghrabieh or large-grain couscous are acceptable substitutes.
The broth should be well-seasoned — it is what flavours the maftoul.
Maftoul is traditionally steamed, not boiled — steaming keeps the grains separate and chewy.

title: Kousa Mahshi
description: Kousa mahshi are tender courgettes hollowed out and stuffed with a fragrant mixture of rice, minced lamb, tomato, and warming spices, then simmered in a tomatoey broth until the courgettes are meltingly soft and the rice filling is perfectly cooked. A cornerstone of Levantine home cooking, this dish requires patience and a steady hand with a corer, but rewards with deeply comforting, layered flavours. Served with yoghurt on the side.

steps.instruction: Hollow out the courgettes using a corer, leaving about 5mm wall thickness. Be careful not to pierce the bottom. Reserve the scooped flesh for another use.
Mix the mince, rice, 1 tbsp tomato paste, allspice, cinnamon, half the salt, and a splash of water. Stuff the courgettes about three-quarters full (the rice will expand).
In a pot, combine tinned tomatoes, remaining tomato paste, water, lemon juice, dried mint, and remaining salt. Arrange the stuffed courgettes snugly in the pot, standing upright.
Bring to a gentle boil, then reduce to a low simmer. Cover and cook for 45-50 minutes until the courgettes are very tender and the rice is cooked through.
Serve the courgettes with the tomato broth spooned over, and yoghurt on the side.

image_placeholder: Stuffed courgettes arranged in a pot with a rich tomato broth, garnished with dried mint.
wine_pairing_notes: A light red like a Cinsault or a dry rosé.
tips: Only fill the courgettes three-quarters — the rice expands as it cooks and will split the courgette if overfilled.
A proper courgette corer (manara) makes life much easier — available at Middle Eastern grocery shops.
Choose short, wide courgettes specifically for stuffing — the long thin ones are too narrow.

title: Sambousek
description: Sambousek are beloved half-moon shaped pastries found across the Levant, typically filled with spiced minced lamb, onions, and pine nuts. The pastry is thin and crispy when fried, with a decorative crimped edge. These are a staple of the mezze table and a popular street food snack. The filling is deeply savoury with warm spices like cinnamon and allspice, complemented by the rich crunch of toasted pine nuts.

steps.instruction: Make the dough: mix flour and salt. Add oil and water, knead 5 minutes until smooth. Wrap and rest 30 minutes.
Cook the filling: sauté onion in a little oil until soft. Add lamb, brown and break up. Add spices and pine nuts. Season and cool.
Roll dough thin. Cut into 10cm circles. Place a tablespoon of filling on one half. Fold over and crimp the edge decoratively with a fork.
Fry at 170°C in batches for 3-4 minutes until golden. Drain on kitchen paper. Serve hot with lemon wedges.

image_placeholder: Golden, crispy sambousek pastries with decorative crimped edges on a plate with lemon wedges.
