Remove cinnamon stick and cardamom pods. Blend the sauce with a stick blender or in a jug blender until silky smooth. If using cashew paste, stir it in now. Return to the pan.
Add the seared chicken to the sauce along with any resting juices. Simmer gently for 12-15 minutes until the chicken is cooked through and tender.

Stir in double cream, sugar, remaining garam masala, and kasuri methi (crush between your palms before adding). Simmer for 3 minutes. Taste and adjust salt. Garnish with fresh coriander and serve with basmati rice or naan.

image_placeholder: A bowl of creamy butter chicken in rich orange-red sauce, garnished with a swirl of cream and fresh coriander leaves, served alongside fluffy basmati rice on a dark slate plate.
wine_pairing_notes: A chilled mango lassi or an Indian pale lager such as Kingfisher complements the rich, creamy sauce beautifully.
tips: Marinating overnight makes a significant difference — the yoghurt tenderises the chicken while spices penetrate deeply.
Kasuri methi is the secret ingredient that gives butter chicken its distinctive restaurant flavour. Crush it between your palms to release the oils before adding.
For a richer sauce, add an extra tablespoon of butter just before serving — this is the restaurant trick.

title: Dal Makhani
description: Dal makhani is the crown jewel of Punjabi cuisine — a luxuriously creamy preparation of whole black lentils (urad dal) and kidney beans, slow-cooked for hours with butter, cream, and a fragrant tomato-onion base. Originating from the dhabas (roadside eateries) of Punjab, the dish gains its characteristic velvety texture from the extended cooking time, which breaks down the lentils into a thick, luscious gravy. The addition of kasuri methi at the end provides the distinctive finishing note.

steps.instruction: Drain the soaked lentils and kidney beans. Place in a pressure cooker with 1 litre of water and a pinch of salt. Cook on high pressure for 25-30 minutes (or 10 whistles on a stovetop pressure cooker). The lentils and beans should be completely soft and mashable.
Heat ghee and butter in a heavy-bottomed pan over medium heat. Add grated onion and cook for 7-8 minutes, stirring frequently, until deep golden.

Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 1 minute until fragrant. Add tomato purée, Kashmiri chilli powder, garam masala, and turmeric. Cook for 5-6 minutes, stirring often, until the oil separates from the masala.

Add the cooked lentils and beans along with their cooking liquid. Stir well and bring to a simmer. Cook on low heat for 45-60 minutes, stirring every 10 minutes, mashing some lentils against the side of the pan. Add 250 ml hot water gradually if the dal becomes too thick.

Stir in cream, sugar, and salt. Add kasuri methi, crushing it between your palms. Simmer for 5 more minutes. The dal should be thick and creamy with some whole lentils remaining. Serve topped with a knob of butter and alongside naan or steamed rice.

image_placeholder: A copper bowl of dark, creamy dal makhani topped with a swirl of cream and a pat of melting butter, with torn naan bread alongside on a hammered brass thali.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold salted lassi or an amber ale pairs wonderfully with the rich, buttery lentils.
tips: The secret to restaurant-quality dal makhani is patience — the longer you simmer, the creamier and more flavourful it becomes. Some restaurants simmer for 8-12 hours.
If you don't have a pressure cooker, boil the lentils in a covered pot for 1.5-2 hours until completely tender before proceeding.
Make a large batch — dal makhani tastes even better the next day as the flavours deepen overnight.

title: Aloo Gobi
description: Aloo gobi is one of the most beloved vegetarian dishes across Northern India, a dry-style curry of potatoes and cauliflower cooked with cumin, turmeric, and ginger. Despite its humble ingredients, this dish delivers deeply satisfying flavours through careful spice layering and the caramelisation of vegetables. It is a staple in Punjabi households, often served with hot rotis or parathas for a complete vegetarian meal.

substitutions.notes: add at the end for tartness

steps.instruction: Heat oil in a large heavy-based frying pan or wok over medium-high heat. Add cumin seeds and let them splutter for 10 seconds.
Add chopped onion and cook for 5 minutes until softened and lightly golden. Add ginger, garlic, and green chilli and cook for 1 minute.
Add turmeric, ground coriander, and Kashmiri chilli powder. Stir for 30 seconds, then add the potato cubes. Toss to coat in spices and cook for 5 minutes, stirring occasionally.

Add cauliflower florets and salt. Stir well, then reduce heat to medium-low. Cover and cook for 20-25 minutes, stirring every 5 minutes, until both potatoes and cauliflower are tender but not mushy. If sticking, add a splash of water.

Uncover, increase heat slightly, and cook for 3-4 minutes to get some golden colour on the vegetables. Sprinkle with amchur powder and garam masala, toss well. Garnish with fresh coriander and serve hot.

image_placeholder: Golden-hued aloo gobi with tender potato and cauliflower pieces sprinkled with fresh coriander, served on a traditional Indian steel plate with roti on the side.
wine_pairing_notes: A refreshing sweet lassi or a wheat beer like Hoegaarden pairs well with the earthy spices.
tips: Don't stir too often — allowing the vegetables to sit undisturbed for a few minutes helps develop a lovely golden crust.
Adding a splash of water and covering creates steam that cooks the vegetables through without deep-frying.
Amchur (dried mango powder) provides a tangy brightness that elevates the dish. If unavailable, a squeeze of lemon at the end works similarly.

title: Paneer Tikka
description: Paneer tikka is a classic North Indian appetiser featuring cubes of paneer marinated in a vibrant spiced yoghurt mixture, then grilled or charred alongside peppers and onions. The marinade of hung yoghurt, Kashmiri chilli, and chaat masala gives the paneer a smoky, tangy crust while keeping the interior soft and creamy. Originally cooked in a tandoor oven, it can be brilliantly replicated under a domestic grill or on a barbecue. It is one of India's most popular vegetarian starter dishes.

substitutions.notes: press for 30 minutes first; makes it vegan

ingredients.name: thick natural yoghurt
ingredients.notes: hung in muslin for 30 min to thicken

steps.instruction: Combine hung yoghurt, Kashmiri chilli powder, garam masala, chaat masala, cumin, coriander, ginger-garlic paste, lemon juice, oil, and salt in a large bowl to form a smooth marinade.
Add paneer cubes, pepper squares, and onion pieces to the marinade. Gently fold to coat evenly without breaking the paneer. Cover and refrigerate for at least 1 hour, up to 4 hours.
Preheat the grill to high (240°C) or prepare a barbecue. Thread marinated paneer, peppers, onions, and cherry tomatoes alternately onto metal skewers or pre-soaked wooden skewers.
Grill for 10-12 minutes, turning every 3 minutes, until the paneer has charred spots and the vegetables are tender. Brush with any remaining marinade while grilling.
Remove from skewers, arrange on a platter. Sprinkle with extra chaat masala and serve immediately with green chutney and lemon wedges.

image_placeholder: Charred paneer tikka skewers with colourful peppers and onions on a dark slate board, with a bowl of green chutney and lemon wedges on the side.
wine_pairing_notes: A chilled Indian lager or a glass of off-dry Gewürztraminer complements the smoky, spiced paneer.
tips: Hanging yoghurt in a muslin cloth removes excess moisture, creating a thicker marinade that clings better to the paneer.
If using a regular oven, place skewers on a wire rack over a baking tray at 230°C and cook for 15-18 minutes, turning once.

For restaurant-style smoky flavour, place a small piece of burning charcoal in a metal bowl inside the marinating container, pour a little ghee on the charcoal, and cover tightly for 5 minutes (dhungar technique).

title: Naan
description: Naan is the quintessential Indian flatbread, originating from the tandoor ovens of Mughal-era India. Made with a yeasted dough enriched with yoghurt and sometimes egg, naan is characterised by its pillowy soft interior and slightly charred, blistered exterior. While traditionally baked against the scorching walls of a clay tandoor, excellent results can be achieved using a screaming hot cast-iron pan. Naan is the perfect accompaniment to butter chicken, dal makhani, or any rich curry.

substitutions.notes: melt butter with 2 minced garlic cloves for garlic naan

steps.instruction: Combine flour, yeast, sugar, and salt in a large bowl. Make a well in the centre and add yoghurt, warm water, and oil. Mix with a wooden spoon, then knead on a lightly floured surface for 8-10 minutes until smooth and elastic.
Place dough in an oiled bowl, cover with a damp tea towel, and leave in a warm place for 1-1.5 hours until doubled in size.
Knock back the dough and divide into 6 equal pieces. Roll each piece into a teardrop or oval shape about 5 mm thick. Sprinkle with nigella seeds if using, pressing them gently into the surface.

Heat a cast-iron pan or heavy frying pan over the highest possible heat until smoking. Place one naan in the dry pan and cook for 1-2 minutes until large bubbles form on the surface and the underside has charred spots.

Flip and cook the other side for 1 minute. Optionally, hold the naan with tongs directly over a gas flame for a few seconds for extra char. Brush immediately with melted butter and keep warm wrapped in a clean tea towel while cooking the remaining naan.

image_placeholder: Freshly baked naan bread with golden blistered spots and nigella seeds, stacked on a wooden board with a small bowl of melted garlic butter.
tips: The pan must be ripping hot — this mimics the intense heat of a tandoor and creates the characteristic blistered surface.
Don't roll the naan too thin or it will be crispy rather than soft and pillowy.
Naan can be made ahead and reheated in a hot pan for 30 seconds per side, or wrapped in foil and warmed in a 180°C oven for 5 minutes.

title: Masala Dosa
title_en: Crispy Lentil Crêpe with Spiced Potato Filling
description: Masala dosa is South India's most iconic breakfast dish — a golden, lacy fermented crêpe made from a batter of rice and urad dal, filled with a spiced potato mixture. Originating from the Udupi region of Karnataka, dosa has become beloved across India and the world. The fermentation process gives the batter a subtle tanginess and helps create the characteristic crispy texture. Served with coconut chutney and sambar, it is a complete, nutritionally balanced meal.

substitutions.notes: traditional choice, available at Indian grocers

steps.instruction: Drain the soaked rice and urad dal separately. Grind the urad dal with fenugreek seeds and 60 ml water in a blender until light and fluffy (about 5 minutes). Transfer to a large bowl. Grind the rice with 80 ml water until smooth but slightly grainy. Combine both batters, add salt, and mix well. Cover and leave to ferment in a warm place for 8-12 hours until doubled in volume and slightly sour-smelling.

For the potato filling: heat 2 tbsp oil in a pan over medium heat. Add mustard seeds — when they pop, add urad dal for tempering and curry leaves. Cook for 30 seconds. Add chopped onion and cook 5 minutes until softened. Add ginger, green chillies, and turmeric, cook 1 minute.
Add the mashed potatoes, salt to taste, and 2-3 tbsp water. Mix well and cook for 3-4 minutes. Fold in fresh coriander. Set aside and keep warm.

Heat a large non-stick pan or cast-iron tava over medium-high heat. Lightly oil the surface. Pour a ladleful of batter (about 80 ml) into the centre and quickly spread in a circular motion using the back of the ladle, working from the centre outwards, to create a thin crêpe.

Drizzle a little oil around the edges and cook for 2-3 minutes until the underside is golden and crispy. Place 2-3 tablespoons of potato filling along the centre. Fold the dosa over the filling or roll it up. Serve immediately with coconut chutney and sambar.

image_placeholder: A golden, crispy masala dosa served on a banana leaf with a mound of spiced potato filling visible, accompanied by small steel bowls of coconut chutney and sambar.
wine_pairing_notes: Traditionally served with filter coffee or a warm cup of South Indian chai.
tips: Fermentation time depends on room temperature — in winter, place the batter near a warm oven or in a switched-off oven with just the light on. The batter should double and have a slightly sour aroma.
Spread the batter quickly and confidently in one circular motion — hesitation leads to thick, uneven dosas.
Leftover batter keeps in the fridge for 2-3 days and actually makes better dosas as fermentation continues.

title: Sambar
title_en: South Indian Lentil and Vegetable Stew
description: Sambar is the soul of South Indian cuisine — a tangy, aromatic lentil stew enriched with tamarind and a fragrant spice blend. It is made with toor dal (split pigeon peas) simmered with seasonal vegetables like drumstick, pumpkin, or aubergine, and finished with a crackling tempering of mustard seeds, curry leaves, and dried chillies. Sambar is indispensable alongside dosa, idli, or plain rice, and varies from household to household across Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, and Kerala.

substitutions.substitute: 2 tbsp lemon juice + 1 tsp brown sugar

ingredients.name: drumstick
ingredients.notes: cut into 5 cm pieces; or use okra

steps.instruction: Rinse toor dal and cook with 600 ml water and turmeric in a pressure cooker for 15 minutes on high pressure (or boil in a pot for 30-40 minutes) until completely soft. Mash with a whisk until smooth.
In a separate pan, bring 400 ml water to a boil. Add the vegetables (drumstick, aubergine, carrot, shallots) and cook for 10 minutes until just tender. Add chopped tomato and cook 3 more minutes.

Add tamarind paste, sambar powder, salt, and jaggery to the vegetables. Stir and simmer for 5 minutes. Add the mashed dal and stir to combine. The sambar should have a soupy consistency — add hot water if too thick. Simmer for 10 minutes.
Prepare the tempering: heat oil in a small pan over high heat. Add mustard seeds — when they splutter, add dried red chillies, curry leaves, and asafoetida. Fry for 15 seconds until fragrant.
Pour the tempering directly into the sambar. Stir, garnish with fresh coriander, and serve hot with rice, dosa, or idli.

image_placeholder: A traditional South Indian brass bowl filled with golden sambar studded with vegetables, with a tempering of mustard seeds and curry leaves glistening on top, served alongside a stack of fluffy idlis.
wine_pairing_notes: Best enjoyed with a South Indian filter coffee or buttermilk (chaas).
tips: The key to good sambar is the balance of tamarind tang, jaggery sweetness, and spice heat — taste and adjust these three elements.
Sambar powder varies by brand; for best results, try MTR or Aachi brands, or toast and grind your own from coriander seeds, dried chillies, fenugreek, and chana dal.
Sambar thickens as it sits. Add water when reheating to bring it back to the right consistency.

title: Thengai Chutney
description: Coconut chutney is the essential condiment of South Indian cuisine — a cool, creamy blend of fresh coconut, green chillies, and roasted chana dal, finished with a sizzling tempering. It provides a perfect counterpoint to crispy dosas, idlis, and vadas, offering a mellow coconut sweetness balanced by sharp chilli heat and tangy notes. Every South Indian household has its own variation, but this Tamil Nadu-style version is the most widely known.

ingredients.name: fresh coconut
ingredients.notes: grated; or use frozen grated coconut

substitutions.ratio: 100 g soaked in 60 ml warm water for 15 min

steps.instruction: Combine grated coconut, roasted chana dal, green chillies, ginger, salt, and cold water in a blender. Blend until smooth but still slightly textured. Taste and adjust salt and chilli.
Transfer to a serving bowl. Heat oil in a small pan over high heat. Add mustard seeds — when they pop, add urad dal and dried red chilli. Fry for 20 seconds until the dal turns golden.
Add curry leaves (stand back, they splutter) and immediately pour the tempering over the chutney. Stir gently and serve at room temperature alongside dosa, idli, or vada.

image_placeholder: A small brass bowl of white coconut chutney flecked with curry leaves and mustard seeds from the tempering, served on a banana leaf alongside golden dosas.
tips: Fresh coconut makes all the difference. If unavailable, frozen grated coconut from Asian supermarkets is the next best option.
The chutney thickens as it sits — add a splash of cold water before serving if needed.
Add a small piece of raw mango or a teaspoon of tamarind paste for extra tanginess.

title: Rasam
description: Rasam is a beloved South Indian soup-like preparation prized for its restorative and digestive properties. This thin, tangy broth is made from tamarind, tomatoes, black pepper, and cumin, often enriched with a small amount of toor dal. Known as 'pepper water' in its earliest form, rasam is the ancestor of the Anglo-Indian mulligatawny soup. It is sipped on its own, poured over steamed rice, or served as a second course in a traditional South Indian meal.

steps.instruction: Dissolve tamarind paste in 200 ml warm water. In a pot, combine tamarind water, chopped tomatoes, crushed pepper and cumin, garlic, turmeric, rasam powder, salt, and jaggery. Add remaining 400 ml water.
Bring to a boil over medium heat, then reduce to a simmer. Cook for 15 minutes until the tomatoes break down completely and the raw smell of tamarind disappears. If using cooked dal, stir it in now.
Prepare the tempering: heat ghee in a small pan, add mustard seeds. When they pop, add dried red chilli and curry leaves. Fry for 15 seconds and pour into the rasam.
Stir in fresh coriander. Do not boil vigorously after tempering — just bring to a gentle froth and serve immediately. Rasam is best very hot. Serve in small bowls or over steamed rice.

image_placeholder: A brass tumbler filled with golden-red rasam, with visible curry leaves and mustard seeds floating on the surface, served alongside a bowl of steamed rice.
tips: Rasam should be thin and soupy — it is not meant to be thick like sambar. Do not reduce it too much.
Rasam is known as an excellent remedy for colds — the pepper and garlic have natural decongestant properties.
Never boil rasam vigorously after the tempering, as this dissipates the volatile aromatic compounds.

title: Appam with Vegetable Stew
title_en: Fermented Rice Hoppers with Coconut Vegetable Stew
description: Appam (or hoppers) are bowl-shaped fermented rice crêpes with a soft, spongy centre and lacy, crispy edges — one of Kerala's most distinctive breakfast dishes. The fermented batter of rice and coconut creates a subtle sweetness and lightness. Paired with a fragrant coconut milk vegetable stew spiced with whole cardamom, cloves, and curry leaves, it is an elegant and satisfying meal. The stew's gentle, aromatic sauce contrasts beautifully with the tangy appam.

steps.instruction: Drain the soaked rice. Blend with cooked rice, grated coconut, sugar, salt, and 150 ml water until very smooth. Mix in yeast. Cover and ferment in a warm place for 6-8 hours until bubbly and slightly risen. The batter should be slightly thinner than dosa batter — add water if needed to achieve a pouring consistency.

For the stew: heat coconut oil in a pan over medium heat. Add cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, and peppercorns. Fry for 30 seconds. Add sliced onion and cook 5 minutes until softened. Add ginger, green chillies, and curry leaves, cook 1 minute.
Add potatoes, carrots, and 200 ml water. Cover and cook for 10 minutes. Add green beans and peas, cook 5 more minutes until all vegetables are just tender.
Pour in coconut milk and salt. Bring to a gentle simmer (do not boil vigorously or the coconut milk may split). Cook for 5 minutes until the stew is creamy. Adjust seasoning.

For the appam: heat an appam pan (appachatti) or small non-stick wok over medium heat. Add a little coconut oil. Pour 60 ml batter into the centre, then quickly swirl the pan so the batter coats the sides thinly while pooling in the centre. Cover and cook for 2-3 minutes until edges are lacy and crisp and the centre is soft and spongy. Gently remove with a spatula. Repeat with remaining batter.

image_placeholder: A delicate bowl-shaped appam with lacy, crispy edges and a soft white centre on a banana leaf, alongside a bowl of creamy coconut vegetable stew with visible cardamom pods and curry leaves.
wine_pairing_notes: Traditionally served with Kerala-style black tea or filter coffee.
tips: An appam pan (appachatti) gives the best results with its rounded base. A small non-stick wok is the best substitute.
The batter should be pourable but not watery — thinner than pancake batter. Adjust with water as needed.
The stew must never boil vigorously once coconut milk is added — gentle simmering keeps it smooth and creamy.

title: Macher Jhol
description: Macher jhol is the quintessential Bengali fish curry — a light, soupy preparation that showcases fresh fish in a turmeric-and-cumin-spiced tomato gravy. Unlike the rich, cream-based curries of North India, Bengali fish curries celebrate simplicity and the natural flavour of the fish. Rohu or catla are traditional choices, but any firm white fish works beautifully. The dish is a staple of the Bengali daily meal, served over steamed rice as the centrepiece of lunch.

ingredients.name: firm white fish fillets
ingredients.notes: rohu, catla, or cod, cut into large pieces

substitutions.notes: less authentic but works; add pinch of mustard powder for flavour

steps.instruction: Rub fish pieces with 0.5 tsp turmeric and a pinch of salt. Heat 2 tbsp mustard oil in a pan over medium-high heat until just smoking. Fry fish pieces for 1-2 minutes per side until golden. Remove and set aside — the fish does not need to be cooked through.
In the same pan, add remaining mustard oil. Add nigella seeds, cumin seeds, bay leaf, and dried red chillies. Fry for 30 seconds until spluttering.
Add potato quarters and fry for 3-4 minutes until lightly golden on the edges. Add chopped tomato, remaining turmeric, ground cumin, chilli powder, and salt. Cook for 3 minutes until tomatoes soften.
Add 400 ml water and bring to a boil. Reduce heat, cover, and simmer for 12-15 minutes until potatoes are nearly cooked through.

Gently slide the fried fish pieces into the gravy. Add green chillies. Cover and simmer for 5-7 minutes until the fish is cooked through and flakes easily. The gravy should be thin and soupy. Garnish with fresh coriander and serve with steamed rice.

image_placeholder: A traditional Bengali thali plate with a bowl of light golden macher jhol containing pieces of fish and potato, served with a mound of steamed white rice.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Kingfisher lager or a glass of dry Riesling pairs well with the light, turmeric-spiced curry.
tips: Mustard oil is essential for authentic Bengali flavour — heat it until it just starts to smoke to remove the raw pungency, then reduce heat before cooking.
Handle the fish gently in the curry to avoid it breaking apart. Use a deep, wide pan rather than stirring.
The gravy should be thin and brothy — this is not meant to be a thick curry. Bengalis call this 'jhol' meaning broth.

title: Mishti Doi
description: Mishti doi is Bengal's most cherished dessert — a caramelised sweet yoghurt with a distinctive amber colour and subtle smoky-sweet flavour. The magic lies in the caramelised sugar (or jaggery) that is whisked into warm milk before culturing with yoghurt. Traditionally set in small earthenware pots (which absorb excess moisture and add an earthy aroma), this dessert is served at celebrations and festivals across Bengal. Its silky, set custard texture and unique caramel-tangy flavour make it unlike any other yoghurt dessert.

substitutions.notes: traditional Bengali choice; gives a deeper, more complex flavour

ingredients.name: natural yoghurt
ingredients.notes: as culture starter, at room temperature

steps.instruction: Pour milk into a heavy-bottomed pan and bring to a boil over medium heat, stirring occasionally. Reduce heat and simmer for 20-25 minutes, stirring frequently, until the milk reduces by about a third and thickens slightly.

Meanwhile, in a separate small pan, make the caramel: heat the sugar over medium heat without stirring until it melts and turns a deep amber colour (about 5-6 minutes). Watch carefully to prevent burning.

Carefully pour the caramelised sugar into the hot reduced milk — it will bubble vigorously. Stir continuously until the caramel is fully dissolved. Remove from heat and allow the mixture to cool until just warm to the touch (about 40-45°C).
Whisk the yoghurt starter until smooth, then gently fold into the warm caramelised milk. Pour into small earthenware pots, ramekins, or a single serving bowl. Cover with cling film.
Place in a warm spot (such as an oven with just the light on) for 6-8 hours or overnight until firmly set. Refrigerate for at least 2 hours before serving chilled.

image_placeholder: Small earthenware pots of amber-coloured mishti doi, one shown with a spoonful revealing the smooth, set custard-like interior, on a patterned Bengali textile.
tips: The milk must cool to lukewarm (40-45°C) before adding the yoghurt culture — too hot will kill the bacteria, too cold and it won't set.
Earthenware pots are traditional and absorb excess moisture, giving a thicker set. Small ramekins are the best Western alternative.
Setting time depends on ambient temperature — in a warm kitchen it may set in 6 hours, in winter you may need 10-12 hours. An oven with just the light on provides ideal warmth.

title: Shorshe Ilish
description: Shorshe ilish is the most revered dish in Bengali cuisine — hilsa fish steamed in a pungent mustard paste sauce. Ilish (hilsa) is considered the king of fish in Bengal, and this preparation highlights its rich, oily flesh against a sharp, fiery mustard gravy. The fish is marinated in turmeric and salt, then steamed or gently cooked in a gravy of ground mustard seeds, green chillies, and mustard oil. It is a dish of remarkable complexity from minimal ingredients, and is central to Bengali celebrations and the monsoon season when hilsa is at its finest.

substitutions.notes: oily fish works best as a substitute

steps.instruction: Rub fish steaks with 0.5 tsp turmeric and salt. Set aside for 15 minutes. Drain the soaked mustard seeds and grind to a smooth paste in a blender with 2 green chillies and 3-4 tbsp water. The paste should be fine and pungent.
Heat mustard oil in a pan until smoking, then reduce heat to medium. Briefly fry the fish pieces for 30 seconds per side — just to seal, not cook through. Remove gently.
In the same pan, add the mustard paste, remaining turmeric, salt, and 100 ml water. Stir and bring to a gentle simmer. Cook for 3-4 minutes.

Gently place the fish steaks into the mustard sauce. Add slit green chillies. Spoon sauce over the fish, cover, and cook on low heat for 8-10 minutes until the fish is cooked through and the sauce has thickened slightly. Drizzle with a teaspoon of raw mustard oil and serve with steamed rice.

image_placeholder: Glistening pieces of hilsa fish in a bright yellow mustard sauce with slit green chillies, served in a traditional Bengali kansa (bell-metal) bowl alongside steamed rice.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold wheat beer or mango lassi complements the pungent mustard and rich fish.
tips: Hilsa can be hard to find outside South Asia — mackerel is the best substitute due to its similar oily texture.
Soaking the mustard seeds before grinding removes some bitterness and creates a smoother paste.
A final drizzle of raw (uncooked) mustard oil over the finished dish adds an authentic punch of flavour.

title: Pani Puri
description: Pani puri is India's most addictive street snack — tiny, hollow crispy puffs (puris) filled with spiced potato and chickpeas, then flooded with a tangy, mint-coriander spiced water (pani) and eaten in one glorious bite. The explosion of flavours — sweet, sour, spicy, tangy — all at once makes it an unforgettable experience. Found at street stalls across India, particularly in Mumbai and Delhi, pani puri is a communal eating experience where the vendor fills and hands you one puri at a time.

substitutions.notes: available at Indian grocers; much easier

steps.instruction: Make the puris (skip if using ready-made): combine semolina, plain flour, and salt. Add water gradually (about 60 ml) and knead to a stiff dough. Rest 15 minutes. Roll very thin (1-2 mm) and cut into 4 cm rounds. Deep fry at 180°C until puffed and golden. Drain on kitchen paper.

Make the pani (spiced water): blend mint, coriander, green chillies, ginger, and 100 ml water until very smooth. Strain through a fine sieve into a jug. Add remaining cold water, tamarind paste, cumin powder, chaat masala, black salt, lemon juice, and regular salt to taste. Stir well. Chill in the fridge for at least 30 minutes — the pani should be ice cold.
Prepare the filling: mix diced potatoes, drained chickpeas, finely diced red onion, a pinch of chaat masala, and salt.

To serve: gently crack a small hole in the top of each puri with your thumb. Fill with a teaspoon of the potato-chickpea mixture, then fill with chilled pani. Eat immediately in one bite. The experience should be a burst of tangy, spicy, cool, and crunchy all at once.

image_placeholder: A plate of crispy golden pani puri shells alongside a glass jug of vibrant green spiced water, with a small bowl of potato-chickpea filling, on a colourful Indian street food cart.
tips: Ready-made puri shells from Indian supermarkets are perfectly acceptable and save considerable time.
The pani must be ice cold for the full experience — make it well in advance and refrigerate.
Eat each pani puri in one bite immediately after filling — if you wait, the puri becomes soggy.

title: Vada Pav
description: Vada pav is Mumbai's beloved street food — a spiced potato fritter (vada) sandwiched in a soft pav (bread roll), slathered with garlic chutney and served with a fried green chilli. Often called India's answer to the burger, it was created in the 1960s near Dadar station to provide an affordable, filling meal for commuters. The crispy, golden vada with its fluffy, spiced potato interior, fiery garlic chutney, and pillowy pav creates a symphony of textures and flavours that is pure comfort food.

ingredients.name: dry garlic chutney
ingredients.notes: dry-roasted garlic, coconut, and chilli powder blended

steps.instruction: Heat 1 tbsp oil in a small pan. Add mustard seeds — when they pop, add curry leaves, ginger, garlic, and green chillies. Fry for 1 minute. Add turmeric and pour this tempering over the mashed potatoes. Add salt and coriander, mix well. Shape into 6 round patties.
Make the batter: whisk gram flour, rice flour, bicarbonate of soda, a pinch of turmeric, salt, and 120-140 ml water until smooth with no lumps. The batter should coat the back of a spoon.

Heat oil to 180°C in a deep pan or kadai. Dip each potato patty into the batter, coating completely, and carefully lower into the hot oil. Fry 3-4 at a time for 3-4 minutes until deep golden and crispy. Drain on kitchen paper.

Slice each pav horizontally without cutting all the way through. Spread dry garlic chutney generously on both cut sides. Place a hot vada inside and press gently. Serve immediately with a fried green chilli on the side.

image_placeholder: A classic Mumbai vada pav with a golden crispy vada in a soft white pav roll, alongside a fried green chilli and a small heap of dry garlic chutney, on newspaper as per street tradition.
tips: The batter must be smooth and the right consistency — too thin and it won't coat properly, too thick and the vada will be doughy.
Fry the green chilli in the hot oil for a few seconds — it becomes milder and slightly smoky, perfect as a side.
Dry garlic chutney (sukha chutney) can be made by dry-roasting garlic, desiccated coconut, and Kashmiri chilli powder, then grinding to a powder. It keeps in an airtight container for weeks.

title: Pav Bhaji
title_en: Spiced Vegetable Mash with Buttered Rolls
description: Pav bhaji is one of Mumbai's most iconic street foods — a thick, buttery mash of mixed vegetables cooked with a special spice blend (pav bhaji masala), served with soft, butter-toasted pav rolls. Created in the 1850s as a quick meal for textile mill workers, it has evolved into one of India's most popular fast foods. The bhaji is a gorgeous orange-red colour from the spice mix and butter, and the generous quantities of butter used are absolutely essential to the authentic experience.

substitutions.substitute: garam masala + 1 tsp paprika + 1 tsp cumin

steps.instruction: Boil cauliflower and peas in salted water for 8-10 minutes until soft. Drain and mash roughly.
Heat 3 tbsp butter in a large, heavy-based pan (a flat tava is traditional). Add chopped onions and cook for 5-6 minutes until softened. Add ginger, garlic, and green chillies. Cook 2 minutes.
Add chopped tomatoes and cook for 5 minutes until they break down. Add pav bhaji masala and Kashmiri chilli powder. Cook 2 minutes, stirring.

Add mashed potatoes, mashed cauliflower-peas mixture, and chopped capsicum. Add 200 ml water and mash everything together vigorously using a potato masher directly in the pan. The mixture should be a thick, well-combined mash — not smooth, but without large chunks.
Add 2 tbsp butter, salt, and lemon juice. Cook on medium heat for 10 minutes, stirring and mashing continuously. Add splashes of water if it becomes too dry. The bhaji should be thick and scoopable.
For the pav: slice each roll in half. Heat remaining butter on a flat pan and toast the pav cut-side down until golden and crispy, about 2 minutes.
Serve the bhaji topped with a knob of butter, chopped coriander, and diced red onion, with the butter-toasted pav on the side and lemon wedges.

image_placeholder: A sizzling plate of orange-red pav bhaji with a pat of melting butter on top, garnished with chopped onion and coriander, with butter-toasted pav rolls arranged around the plate.
wine_pairing_notes: A chilled Indian lager or a sweet mango lassi.
tips: Don't skimp on the butter — it's what makes pav bhaji indulgent. Mumbai street vendors use an astonishing amount.
Mash the bhaji directly on the tava (flat griddle) using a potato masher — this is the traditional technique and helps caramelise the mixture.
Pav bhaji masala is key — brands like Everest or MDH are widely available at Indian grocers and make a huge difference versus improvised spice blends.

title: Papdi Chaat
description: Papdi chaat is Delhi's quintessential snack — a lively assembly of crispy flour crackers (papdi) topped with boiled potatoes, chickpeas, tangy tamarind chutney, cool yoghurt, and a flurry of chaat masala. Every bite is an explosion of contrasts: crunchy and soft, sweet and sour, cool and spicy. Chaat embodies the genius of Indian street food, where simple components are layered to create something far greater than the sum of their parts. It is the ultimate example of the six-taste philosophy in Indian cuisine.

ingredients.name: natural yoghurt
ingredients.notes: whisked smooth, slightly sweetened with 1 tsp sugar

steps.instruction: Arrange papdi crackers on a large serving plate or individual plates in a single layer.
Top each papdi with a small spoonful of cubed potato and chickpeas.
Drizzle whisked yoghurt generously over the papdis. Follow with tamarind chutney and green chutney.
Sprinkle with chaat masala, roasted cumin powder, and Kashmiri chilli powder. Top with sev, chopped coriander, and pomegranate seeds. Serve immediately before the papdi lose their crunch.

image_placeholder: A vibrant plate of papdi chaat drizzled with white yoghurt, dark tamarind chutney, and green mint chutney, scattered with sev noodles and pomegranate seeds.
tips: Assemble just before serving — papdi should be crunchy, not soggy. This is a last-minute dish.
Store-bought papdi, chutneys, and sev from Indian supermarkets make this an effortless no-cook dish.
Balance is everything: sweet (tamarind), sour (yoghurt), spicy (chilli), tangy (chaat masala), crunchy (papdi, sev), and soft (potato). Taste and adjust.

title: Gulab Jamun
description: Gulab jamun is India's most beloved dessert — soft, spongy milk-solid dumplings soaked in a fragrant rose and cardamom sugar syrup. The name comes from 'gulab' (rose) and 'jamun' (a berry they resemble). Made from khoya (reduced milk solids) or milk powder, the dumplings are deep-fried to a dark golden brown, then immediately plunged into warm sugar syrup where they absorb the fragrant liquid and become irresistibly soft and syrupy. No Indian celebration is complete without them.

substitutions.notes: traditional and superior result; available at Indian grocers

steps.instruction: Make the sugar syrup first: combine sugar and water in a saucepan. Heat over medium, stirring until sugar dissolves. Add crushed cardamom pods and simmer for 5-7 minutes until slightly thickened (one-string consistency). Remove from heat, stir in rose water and saffron. Keep warm.

Combine milk powder, plain flour, and bicarbonate of soda in a bowl. Add melted ghee and mix. Gradually add warm milk, a tablespoon at a time, and knead gently into a soft, smooth dough. Do not overwork — the dough should hold together without cracks when rolled. Rest for 5 minutes.

Divide into 20-22 small portions. Roll each between your palms into smooth balls without any cracks (cracks will cause them to break while frying). They should be slightly smaller than desired — they will swell in the syrup.

Heat oil to 130-140°C (lower than typical frying temperature — this is crucial). Gently slide in 5-6 balls at a time. Fry on low-medium heat, turning frequently, for 6-8 minutes until uniformly deep golden brown. They must cook slowly to ensure the centre cooks through.

Immediately transfer hot gulab jamun into the warm sugar syrup. Let them soak for at least 1 hour — ideally 3-4 hours — turning occasionally. They will swell and absorb the syrup. Serve warm or at room temperature, garnished with chopped pistachios.

image_placeholder: Golden gulab jamun soaked in syrup, served in a decorative bowl garnished with slivered pistachios and a few saffron strands, with syrup pooling around the base.
tips: The frying temperature is critical — too hot and the outside browns before the inside cooks, resulting in a hard centre. Keep the oil at 130-140°C and fry slowly.
The dough must have no cracks when rolled — any cracks will cause the balls to break apart during frying. If the dough is too dry, add a few drops more milk; if too wet, add a pinch more milk powder.
Gulab jamun taste best when soaked for several hours or overnight — the syrup penetrates completely, making them melt-in-the-mouth soft.

title: Kheer
description: Kheer is India's oldest and most celebratory dessert — a slow-cooked rice pudding enriched with full-fat milk, sugar, cardamom, saffron, and nuts. Unlike Western rice pudding, kheer achieves its luxurious texture through patient reduction of milk, which concentrates the flavour and creates a naturally thick, creamy consistency. It is served at temples, festivals, weddings, and daily meals alike, embodying the concept of comfort through simplicity. This Rajasthani version uses saffron and a generous amount of dried fruits.

steps.instruction: Pour the milk into a heavy-bottomed pan and bring to a boil over medium heat, stirring frequently to prevent a skin forming or the bottom catching. Once boiling, reduce to a simmer.

Drain the soaked rice and add to the simmering milk. Cook on low heat for 35-40 minutes, stirring every few minutes, until the rice is very soft and the milk has reduced to about two-thirds of its original volume.
Add sugar, crushed cardamom, saffron milk, and half the nuts. Stir well and cook for another 5 minutes. The kheer should be creamy and thickened — it will continue to thicken as it cools.
Remove from heat. Stir in rose water if using. Pour into a serving bowl or individual dishes. Garnish with remaining slivered nuts and raisins. Serve warm, at room temperature, or chilled.

image_placeholder: A ceramic bowl of creamy, pale gold kheer garnished with saffron strands, slivered almonds, and pistachios, with a silver spoon resting on the side.
tips: Stir frequently while the milk reduces to prevent it catching on the bottom — a heavy-bottomed pan is essential.
Kheer thickens considerably as it cools, so take it off the heat when it's slightly thinner than your desired consistency.
For the best flavour, use whole milk and reduce it patiently rather than using condensed milk shortcuts.

title: Jalebi
description: Jalebi is one of India's most recognisable sweets — bright orange, pretzel-shaped spirals of fermented batter, deep-fried until crispy and immediately dunked in saffron-cardamom sugar syrup. The result is a sweet with a shattering crispy exterior and a syrupy, slightly chewy interior. Originating from the Middle Eastern zalabia, jalebi has been a staple of Indian celebrations and street food for centuries. Served piping hot for breakfast with rabri (thickened milk) or as a festive sweet, its intoxicating sweetness and satisfying crunch are legendary.

substitutions.notes: skip fermentation; less tangy but still works

steps.instruction: Combine plain flour, cornflour, yoghurt, saffron, and warm water in a bowl. Whisk until smooth with no lumps — the batter should be like thick double cream. Cover and leave in a warm place for 8-12 hours to ferment. The batter will become bubbly and slightly sour.
Make the sugar syrup: dissolve sugar in 200 ml water over medium heat. Add cardamom powder and lemon juice. Simmer for 5-7 minutes until slightly thick and sticky. Stir in rose water. Keep warm.
After fermentation, stir the batter — it should be bubbly. Pour into a squeeze bottle or a piping bag with a small round nozzle (5 mm). Heat ghee or oil in a wide pan to 170°C.
Squeeze the batter directly into the hot oil in concentric circle or pretzel shapes (about 5-6 cm diameter). Fry 3-4 at a time for 2-3 minutes, flipping once, until crispy and deep golden-orange.
Lift out with a slotted spoon and immediately dunk into the warm sugar syrup for 30-60 seconds. Remove and let excess syrup drip off. Serve immediately while still crispy.

image_placeholder: A stack of bright orange, crispy jalebi spirals glistening with sugar syrup on a decorative plate, with saffron strands visible on the surface.
tips: Fermenting the batter is crucial for authentic jalebi — it creates the characteristic tanginess and helps the batter stay crispy after soaking.
Maintain the oil at 170°C — too hot and the jalebi brown before cooking through, too cool and they absorb excess oil.
Jalebi must be eaten immediately — they lose their crispiness within 30 minutes. This is why street vendors make them to order.

title: Gaeng Khiao Wan Gai
description: Green curry is one of Thailand's most beloved dishes — a fragrant, spicy coconut curry made with fresh green curry paste, chicken, Thai aubergines, and sweet basil. The 'green' comes from fresh green chillies in the paste, and the dish balances creamy coconut milk with the bright heat of the curry. In Thai cuisine, green curry is considered the spiciest of the three main curry colours. It exemplifies the Thai philosophy of balancing sweet, salty, sour, and spicy in every bite.

substitutions.notes: for vegetarian version; press and cube

ingredients.name: coconut milk
ingredients.notes: full-fat, do not shake the tin

ingredients.name: green curry paste
ingredients.notes: Thai brand such as Mae Ploy or Maesri

steps.instruction: Scoop the thick cream from the top of the coconut milk tin (about 80 ml). Heat this cream in a wok or large pan over medium-high heat with the oil. When it starts to split and become oily (2-3 minutes), add the green curry paste and fry for 1-2 minutes until very fragrant.
Add the chicken strips and stir-fry in the paste for 3-4 minutes until sealed on the outside and well coated.

Pour in the remaining coconut milk. Add Thai aubergines, bamboo shoots, and green beans. Tear in the kaffir lime leaves. Bring to a simmer and cook for 10-12 minutes until the chicken is cooked through and vegetables are tender.
Season with fish sauce and palm sugar. Stir to dissolve the sugar. Taste and adjust — the curry should be creamy, aromatic, slightly sweet, and spicy. Stir in most of the basil leaves.
Ladle into bowls and garnish with remaining basil leaves and sliced red chilli. Serve with steamed jasmine rice.

image_placeholder: A bowl of vibrant green curry with chicken, Thai aubergines, and basil leaves floating in creamy coconut sauce, served alongside a bowl of steamed jasmine rice.
wine_pairing_notes: An off-dry Riesling or a cold Singha beer complements the aromatic heat beautifully.
tips: Splitting the coconut cream (frying until the oils separate) before adding the paste is the authentic Thai technique — it deepens the flavour immensely.
Kaffir lime leaves are available frozen at Asian supermarkets and keep for months in the freezer.
For extra heat, slice a few bird's eye chillies into the curry during cooking.

title: Gaeng Phet
description: Red curry is a cornerstone of Thai cuisine — a rich, aromatic curry featuring a paste of dried red chillies, lemongrass, galangal, and shrimp paste, simmered in coconut milk. Slightly milder than green curry, it has a deeper, more rounded flavour. This version pairs chicken with pumpkin and Thai basil, a classic combination that balances the curry's heat with natural sweetness.

substitutions.notes: add in the last 5 minutes of cooking

steps.instruction: Heat oil in a wok over medium-high heat. Add the thick cream from the top of the coconut tin and cook until it splits. Add red curry paste and fry for 1-2 minutes until fragrant and the oil turns red.
Add chicken pieces and stir-fry for 3 minutes until coated in paste and sealed. Add remaining coconut milk, pumpkin, and kaffir lime leaves. Bring to a simmer.
Cook for 15 minutes until pumpkin is tender and chicken is cooked through. Add sliced red pepper in the last 3 minutes.
Season with fish sauce and palm sugar. Stir in basil leaves. Serve with steamed jasmine rice.

image_placeholder: A bowl of deep red Thai curry with chunks of golden pumpkin and chicken, garnished with Thai basil and sliced red chilli, with jasmine rice on the side.
wine_pairing_notes: A fruity Pinot Gris or a cold Chang beer pairs well with the rich, spiced coconut sauce.
tips: Pumpkin adds natural sweetness that beautifully balances the curry paste's heat — don't skip it.
Add the red pepper at the end to keep it slightly crunchy for textural contrast.
Like most Thai curries, this tastes even better the next day as the flavours meld.

title: Gaeng Massaman
description: Massaman curry is Thailand's most internationally celebrated curry — a rich, mildly spiced coconut curry influenced by Malay and Indian Muslim cuisine. Unlike other Thai curries, it features warm spices like cinnamon, star anise, and cardamom alongside the classic Thai aromatics. Slow-cooked with tender beef, potatoes, and peanuts, it is a deeply comforting dish with complex layers of flavour. Often ranked among the world's best dishes, massaman represents the beautiful cultural interchange of Southern Thai border cuisine.

substitutions.substitute: 1 tbsp lime juice + 1 tsp brown sugar

steps.instruction: Heat oil in a large heavy-bottomed pot or Dutch oven. Add the thick cream from one tin of coconut milk and cook until it splits. Add massaman paste and fry for 2 minutes until very fragrant. Add cinnamon, star anise, cardamom, and bay leaves.
Add beef cubes and stir to coat in paste. Cook for 3-4 minutes until sealed on all sides.
Pour in remaining coconut milk. Bring to a simmer, then cover and cook on low heat for 45-50 minutes until the beef is nearly tender.
Add potato chunks and onion quarters. Continue to simmer, covered, for 20 minutes until potatoes are cooked through and beef is very tender.
Stir in peanuts, tamarind paste, fish sauce, and palm sugar. Simmer uncovered for 5 minutes. The sauce should be thick and rich. Serve with steamed jasmine rice.

image_placeholder: A rich, dark brown massaman curry in a ceramic bowl with tender beef chunks, whole potatoes, and peanuts visible in the thick coconut sauce, with star anise and cinnamon as garnish.
wine_pairing_notes: A bold Shiraz or a cold Singha beer pairs well with the warm, aromatic spices.
tips: Low and slow is the key — the beef needs time to become meltingly tender in the coconut milk.
Tamarind is essential for the characteristic tang that balances the richness. Don't substitute with lime juice if you can avoid it.
Massaman curry improves dramatically when made a day ahead and reheated.

title: Pad Thai
description: Pad Thai is Thailand's national noodle dish — stir-fried rice noodles with a tangy-sweet tamarind sauce, prawns or chicken, egg, bean sprouts, and crushed peanuts. Created in the 1930s as part of a national campaign to promote Thai identity, it has become the world's most recognised Thai dish. The key is the wok-charred flavour (wok hei) and the balance of sweet (palm sugar), sour (tamarind), salty (fish sauce), and umami (dried prawns). A squeeze of lime and a scatter of peanuts complete the experience.

ingredients.name: flat rice noodles (pad thai noodles)
ingredients.notes: dried, soaked in room-temperature water for 30-60 minutes until pliable

substitutions.notes: add before noodles and cook through first

substitutions.substitute: 2 tbsp lime juice + 1 tbsp brown sugar

steps.instruction: Make the pad thai sauce: whisk together tamarind paste, fish sauce, and palm sugar in a small bowl until the sugar dissolves. Set aside. Drain the soaked noodles.
Heat 2 tbsp oil in a wok over the highest heat possible. Add tofu cubes and fry until golden on all sides, about 3 minutes. Add garlic, shallot, and dried shrimp. Stir-fry for 30 seconds.

Add prawns and stir-fry for 1-2 minutes until just pink. Push everything to one side of the wok. Add remaining oil and pour in the beaten eggs. Scramble lightly for 30 seconds until just set, then mix with the other ingredients.
Add drained noodles and the pad thai sauce. Toss vigorously with tongs for 2-3 minutes until the noodles are coated, softened, and slightly charred in places. Add preserved radish if using.
Add half the bean sprouts and garlic chives. Toss for 30 seconds. Transfer to plates and top with remaining bean sprouts, crushed peanuts, and lime wedges. Serve immediately.

image_placeholder: A plate of pad thai with glossy, wok-charred noodles, pink prawns, and golden tofu, topped with crushed peanuts, bean sprouts, and lime wedges on a dark plate.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Singha beer or an off-dry Riesling pairs well with the sweet-sour-salty sauce.
tips: Cook pad thai in small batches (2 servings at a time) — overcrowding the wok causes steaming instead of stir-frying.
Soak the noodles in room-temperature (not hot) water to prevent them becoming mushy during stir-frying.
The highest possible heat is essential for wok hei — that slightly smoky, charred flavour that defines great pad thai.

title: Tom Yum Goong
description: Tom yum goong is Thailand's most famous soup — a fiery, sour, and aromatic broth brimming with prawns, mushrooms, and the holy trinity of Thai aromatics: lemongrass, galangal, and kaffir lime leaves. The name translates to 'boiled-mixed' (tom yum) 'prawns' (goong). The clear version (tom yum nam sai) showcases the pure, electrifying broth, while the creamy version adds a splash of evaporated milk or roasted chilli paste for richness. It is a masterclass in Thai flavour balancing.

ingredients.name: lemongrass stalks
ingredients.notes: bruised and cut into 3 cm pieces

ingredients.name: straw mushrooms
ingredients.notes: tinned, drained and halved; or oyster mushrooms

ingredients.name: nam prik pao (Thai roasted chilli paste)

steps.instruction: Bring stock to a boil in a pot. Add lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, and chillies. Simmer for 5 minutes to infuse the aromatics.
Add mushrooms and cherry tomatoes. Cook for 3 minutes. Add nam prik pao if using (for the creamy version).
Add prawns and cook for 2-3 minutes until they turn pink and curl. Do not overcook.

Remove from heat. Add fish sauce and lime juice — these must be added off the heat to preserve their bright flavour. Taste and adjust: it should be simultaneously hot, sour, salty, and aromatic. Garnish with fresh coriander and serve immediately.

image_placeholder: A steaming bowl of tom yum goong with pink prawns, mushrooms, and cherry tomatoes in a clear aromatic broth, garnished with coriander and bird's eye chillies.
wine_pairing_notes: A crisp Sauvignon Blanc or a cold Thai beer like Chang pairs perfectly with the hot, sour broth.
tips: The lemongrass, galangal, and kaffir lime leaves are for flavouring only — they are not meant to be eaten. Leave them in for presentation but warn diners.
Add lime juice and fish sauce off the heat to preserve their fresh, bright flavours.
If using shell-on prawns, the shells add tremendous flavour to the broth — peel at the table.

title: Som Tam
description: Som tam is Thailand's most popular salad — a vibrant, punchy preparation of shredded green papaya pounded in a mortar with lime, chilli, fish sauce, palm sugar, peanuts, and dried shrimp. Originating from the Isan region of Northeastern Thailand, it has become ubiquitous across the country. The dish is traditionally prepared to order in a large clay mortar (khrok), with the vendor adjusting the heat, sourness, and sweetness to each customer's preference.

ingredients.name: green (unripe) papaya
ingredients.notes: peeled and shredded into thin strips

substitutions.notes: good alternatives when papaya is unavailable

steps.instruction: Pound garlic and chillies in a large mortar and pestle until roughly broken up. Add green beans and pound lightly — you want them bruised, not mashed.
Add palm sugar, fish sauce, and lime juice. Pound briefly to dissolve the sugar.
Add shredded papaya a handful at a time, pounding and tossing with a spoon to bruise the papaya and combine with the dressing. Do not over-pound — the papaya should retain its crunch.
Add cherry tomatoes, dried shrimp, and peanuts. Pound very lightly and toss to combine. Taste and adjust: it should be simultaneously sweet, sour, salty, and hot. Serve immediately.

image_placeholder: A mound of shredded green papaya salad with halved cherry tomatoes, peanuts, and dried shrimp, served on a plate lined with cabbage leaves alongside sticky rice.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Singha beer or a sparkling wine cuts through the heat beautifully.
tips: A mortar and pestle is essential for authentic som tam — a food processor won't give the right texture. The bruising action releases juices differently from chopping.
Shred the papaya using a julienne peeler or by scoring the flesh with a knife and then shaving it off — the strips should be thin and uniform.
Adjust chilli to taste but remember: authentic Isan-style som tam is eye-wateringly hot.

title: Pad Kra Pao Moo
title_en: Holy Basil Stir-Fry with Minced Pork
description: Pad kra pao is Thailand's true everyday dish — a fiery stir-fry of minced pork with holy basil, garlic, and chillies, served over rice with a crispy fried egg. While pad thai may be more famous internationally, pad kra pao is what Thais actually eat for lunch. The dish is defined by holy basil (kra pao), whose peppery, clove-like flavour is distinctly different from sweet basil. It comes together in minutes and is the ultimate Thai comfort food.

steps.instruction: Pound garlic and chillies together roughly in a mortar and pestle — they should be broken up but not a paste.
Heat oil in a wok over the highest heat possible. Add the garlic-chilli mixture and stir-fry for 15 seconds until fragrant.
Add minced pork and stir-fry vigorously, breaking up any clumps, for 3-4 minutes until cooked through and slightly browned.
Add oyster sauce, fish sauce, light soy sauce, dark soy sauce, and sugar. Stir-fry for 1 minute to combine.
Remove wok from heat and add holy basil leaves. Toss quickly — the residual heat will wilt them perfectly. They should still be vibrant green.
Fry eggs in a separate pan with plenty of oil until the edges are crispy and lacy, but the yolk remains runny. Serve the stir-fry over jasmine rice with a fried egg on top.

image_placeholder: A plate of pad kra pao with dark, glossy minced pork and bright green basil leaves over steamed rice, topped with a crispy fried egg with a runny yolk.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Singha or Leo beer is the classic accompaniment.
tips: Holy basil (kra pao) is different from sweet basil — it has a spicy, peppery flavour. Available at Thai and Southeast Asian grocers, often frozen.
High heat is essential. The wok should be smoking before you add the garlic and chilli.
The fried egg must have crispy, lacy edges — achieved by frying in plenty of very hot oil.

title: Khao Pad
description: Khao pad is Thai fried rice — a deceptively simple dish that showcases the Thai genius for balanced flavours. Unlike Chinese fried rice, khao pad is lighter, more aromatic, and always served with fresh cucumber, lime, and fish sauce on the side. The key is day-old jasmine rice, high heat, and restraint with seasonings. It is a staple street food and home-cooked meal, endlessly adaptable with prawns, chicken, or crab.

ingredients.name: cooked jasmine rice
ingredients.notes: day-old, cold, from about 200 g uncooked

steps.instruction: Heat oil in a wok over high heat. Add garlic and stir-fry for 10 seconds. Add diced chicken and stir-fry for 3-4 minutes until cooked through.
Push chicken to one side, crack eggs into the wok and scramble roughly for 30 seconds. Mix with the chicken.
Add cold rice, breaking up any clumps. Stir-fry vigorously for 2-3 minutes, tossing frequently, until the rice is hot and slightly charred in places.
Add onion, tomato wedges, fish sauce, soy sauce, sugar, and white pepper. Toss for 1-2 minutes. Add spring onions and toss once more.
Serve on plates with sliced cucumber and lime wedges on the side. Offer extra fish sauce and dried chilli flakes at the table.

image_placeholder: A plate of golden Thai fried rice garnished with spring onions and a tomato wedge, served with sliced cucumber and a lime wedge on the side.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Thai beer or a glass of Grüner Veltliner.
tips: Day-old rice is essential — freshly cooked rice is too moist and will become mushy. Spread rice on a tray and refrigerate uncovered for several hours.
Thai fried rice is lighter and more delicate than Chinese versions — don't overload with sauces.
Fish sauce and lime at the table is the authentic Thai way to finish and personalise the dish.

title: Larb Moo
description: Larb is the national dish of Laos and the signature salad of Thailand's Isan region — a vibrant warm salad of minced pork dressed with lime juice, fish sauce, toasted rice powder, and fresh herbs. The toasted rice powder (khao khua) is the defining ingredient, adding a nutty crunch and slightly smoky flavour. Larb is bright, refreshing, and explosively flavoured — meant to be eaten with sticky rice and raw vegetables.

steps.instruction: Toast the glutinous rice in a dry frying pan over medium heat for 5-6 minutes, shaking frequently, until golden brown. Grind to a coarse powder in a mortar and pestle or spice grinder. This is khao khua (toasted rice powder).

Dry-fry the minced pork in a pan over medium-high heat with 2 tbsp water for 5-6 minutes, breaking up clumps, until cooked through. Do not add oil — the pork releases enough fat. Remove from heat and drain any excess liquid.
While still warm, add lime juice, fish sauce, sugar, and dried chilli flakes to the pork. Toss well.

Add sliced shallots, spring onions, mint, coriander, and toasted rice powder. Toss everything together. Taste and adjust — it should be sour, salty, and spicy with a nutty crunch. Serve immediately with sticky rice and raw cabbage leaves.

image_placeholder: A mound of larb moo on a plate surrounded by fresh mint leaves, sliced shallots, and raw cabbage leaves, with a basket of sticky rice alongside.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Beerlao or Singha beer is the traditional pairing.
tips: Toasted rice powder (khao khua) is what makes larb unique — don't skip it. It adds a nutty, slightly smoky crunch.
Dress the pork while still warm — it absorbs the lime and fish sauce better.
Larb should be assertively sour and salty — be generous with the lime juice and fish sauce.

title: Khao Niao Mamuang
description: Mango sticky rice is Thailand's most beloved dessert — a simple but sublime combination of sweet glutinous rice, ripe mango, and a silky coconut cream sauce. The warm sticky rice is soaked in sweetened coconut milk until plump and fragrant, then served with sliced ripe mango and a drizzle of salted coconut cream. The contrast of sweet, salty, and creamy against the fresh, slightly tart mango is sheer perfection. It appears at every Thai restaurant and street stall during mango season.

ingredients.name: glutinous (sticky) rice
ingredients.notes: soaked in water for at least 4 hours, preferably overnight

ingredients.name: ripe mangoes
ingredients.notes: Nam Dok Mai or Alphonso varieties preferred

steps.instruction: Drain the soaked sticky rice. Steam in a bamboo steamer or fine-mesh sieve over simmering water (rice should not touch the water) for 20-25 minutes until translucent and tender. Flip halfway through.
While the rice steams, warm 200 ml coconut milk with 60 g sugar, salt, and pandan leaf in a small pan until the sugar dissolves. Do not boil.

Transfer the hot steamed rice to a bowl. Pour the warm sweetened coconut milk over the rice, stir gently, and cover. Let it absorb for 15-20 minutes, stirring once or twice. The rice should be glossy and sweet.
Make the topping sauce: warm remaining 100 ml coconut milk with 20 g sugar and a pinch of salt. This should be slightly thicker and saltier than the soaking liquid.
Peel and slice the mangoes. Mound the sticky rice on plates, arrange mango slices alongside, drizzle with the coconut topping sauce, and sprinkle with toasted sesame seeds.

image_placeholder: A plate of glossy Thai sticky rice with sliced golden mango, drizzled with coconut cream sauce and sesame seeds, on a banana leaf-lined plate.
tips: The rice must be steamed, not boiled — boiling makes it mushy. A bamboo steamer lined with muslin is ideal.
Soak the rice for at least 4 hours, ideally overnight — this is crucial for the right chewy texture.
The mango must be perfectly ripe — sweet, fragrant, and yielding to gentle pressure.

title: Tom Kha Gai
description: Tom kha gai is Thailand's elegant coconut soup — a rich, creamy broth infused with galangal, lemongrass, and kaffir lime leaves, with tender chicken and mushrooms. Less fiery than tom yum but equally complex, it showcases the Thai mastery of balancing coconut richness with aromatic herbs and lime acidity. The name means 'boiled galangal chicken', reflecting the importance of galangal (not ginger) to its distinctive flavour profile.

ingredients.name: lemongrass stalks
ingredients.notes: bruised and cut into 3 cm pieces

steps.instruction: Combine chicken stock and coconut milk in a pot. Add galangal, lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, and chillies. Bring to a gentle simmer over medium heat and cook for 5 minutes to infuse.
Add sliced chicken and mushrooms. Simmer gently for 8-10 minutes until the chicken is cooked through. Do not boil vigorously or the coconut milk will split.
Remove from heat. Stir in fish sauce, lime juice, and palm sugar. Taste and adjust — it should be rich, sour, and subtly sweet. Garnish with coriander leaves and serve.

image_placeholder: A bowl of creamy white tom kha gai soup with sliced chicken, mushrooms, and floating kaffir lime leaves and lemongrass pieces, garnished with fresh coriander.
wine_pairing_notes: An unoaked Chardonnay or a cold Singha beer pairs beautifully with the creamy, herbal soup.
tips: Galangal gives tom kha its distinctive citrusy, piney flavour — ginger is not the same but is an acceptable substitute.
Never boil coconut milk soups vigorously — gentle simmering prevents the coconut milk from splitting.
Add the lime juice off the heat to preserve its bright, fresh acidity.

title: Satay Gai
description: Thai satay is a beloved street food — marinated chicken skewers grilled over charcoal and served with a rich peanut dipping sauce and a tangy cucumber relish. While satay originated in Indonesia, the Thai version has developed its own distinct identity with its coconut milk-based marinade, turmeric-yellow colour, and accompanying ajad (cucumber relish). Satay vendors are found at every Thai night market, the aroma of charcoal-grilled meat drawing crowds from afar.

steps.instruction: Combine coconut milk, turmeric, cumin, coriander, fish sauce, and palm sugar for the marinade. Add chicken strips and marinate for at least 2 hours, ideally overnight. Thread onto pre-soaked bamboo skewers.

Make the peanut sauce: heat coconut milk in a small pan, add red curry paste and fry for 1 minute. Add peanut butter, tamarind paste, palm sugar, and fish sauce. Stir until smooth. Simmer for 5 minutes, adding water if too thick. The sauce should be pourable but rich.
Make the ajad relish: dissolve sugar in rice vinegar over gentle heat. Cool, then mix with sliced cucumber and shallot.
Grill skewers on a barbecue or under a hot grill for 3-4 minutes per side, basting with remaining marinade, until charred and cooked through. Serve with peanut sauce and cucumber relish.

image_placeholder: Golden turmeric-stained chicken satay skewers on a plate with char marks, served with a bowl of rich peanut sauce and a small dish of cucumber relish.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Singha beer or an off-dry Riesling pairs beautifully with the rich peanut sauce.
tips: Marinate overnight for the most flavourful satay — the coconut milk and spices tenderise the chicken while infusing deep flavour.
Soak bamboo skewers for at least 30 minutes before grilling to prevent them burning.
The peanut sauce should be warm when served — it thickens as it cools, so thin with water as needed.

title: Pad See Ew
title_en: Stir-Fried Wide Rice Noodles with Soy Sauce
description: Pad see ew (literally 'fried with soy sauce') is Thailand's other great noodle dish — wide, flat rice noodles stir-fried with dark soy sauce, Chinese broccoli, egg, and chicken or pork. The dish is all about the wok hei — that smoky, slightly charred flavour that comes from blisteringly high heat. Simpler than pad thai, it relies on the caramelisation of dark soy sauce on the noodles for its distinctive colour and sweet-savoury depth.

ingredients.name: fresh wide rice noodles (sen yai)
ingredients.notes: or dried wide rice noodles soaked

ingredients.name: Chinese broccoli (gai lan)
ingredients.notes: cut into 5 cm pieces, stems and leaves separated

steps.instruction: If using fresh noodles, gently separate them with your hands. If using dried, soak in warm water for 30 minutes until pliable, then drain.
Heat oil in a wok over the highest possible heat until smoking. Add garlic and stir-fry for 10 seconds. Add chicken and stir-fry for 2-3 minutes until mostly cooked.
Push chicken to one side. Crack eggs into the wok and let them set for 20 seconds, then break up roughly.
Add noodles and Chinese broccoli stems. Add dark soy sauce, light soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sugar. Toss vigorously for 2-3 minutes, pressing noodles against the wok to get charred spots.
Add broccoli leaves and white pepper. Toss for 30 seconds until the leaves just wilt. Serve immediately.

image_placeholder: A plate of dark, glossy pad see ew noodles with charred edges, chicken pieces, and bright green Chinese broccoli, with white pepper visible on top.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Thai beer or a fruity Pinot Noir.
tips: The wok must be smoking hot — this is the single most important factor for great pad see ew.
Cook in small batches (2 servings max) to maintain high enough heat for proper wok hei.
Press the noodles flat against the wok and let them sit for 30 seconds without stirring to develop those desirable charred spots.

title: Khao Soi
title_en: Northern Thai Coconut Curry Noodle Soup
description: Khao soi is Chiang Mai's signature dish — a rich coconut curry noodle soup of Burmese origin that has become the star of Northern Thai cuisine. It features egg noodles in a creamy, mildly spiced curry broth, topped with crispy fried noodles, pickled mustard greens, shallots, and a squeeze of lime. The interplay of the creamy soup, chewy noodles, and crispy topping, plus the sharp pickled and raw condiments, creates one of the most layered eating experiences in Thai cuisine.

steps.instruction: Fry 80 g of egg noodles in hot oil (180°C) until golden and crispy, about 2 minutes. Drain on kitchen paper and set aside for topping.
In a pot, heat 2 tbsp of the coconut cream (thick part) until it splits. Add curry paste, curry powder, and turmeric. Fry for 2 minutes until fragrant.
Add chicken and cook for 3 minutes. Pour in remaining coconut milk and chicken stock. Bring to a simmer and cook for 15-18 minutes until chicken is tender.
Season with fish sauce and palm sugar. Taste and adjust. Cook the remaining egg noodles in boiling water for 2-3 minutes, drain, and divide between bowls.
Ladle the curry broth and chicken over the noodles. Top with crispy fried noodles, sliced shallots, pickled mustard greens, and coriander. Serve with lime wedges.

image_placeholder: A bowl of rich golden khao soi with egg noodles in creamy coconut curry broth, topped with crispy fried noodles, sliced shallots, and lime, with pickled greens on the side.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Chang beer or a fruity Gewürztraminer.
tips: The crispy noodle topping is essential — it provides the textural contrast that makes khao soi special.
Pickled mustard greens (phak dong) are available at Asian supermarkets. Their sour crunch is key to the dish.
If you can find khao soi paste (available at Thai grocers), it gives the most authentic result — substitute for the curry paste and curry powder.

title: Tod Mun Pla
description: Tod mun pla are Thailand's beloved fish cakes — bouncy, fragrant patties of pounded white fish mixed with red curry paste, kaffir lime leaves, and snake beans, then deep-fried to a golden crust. Their distinctive bouncy texture comes from vigorously pounding or processing the fish paste, which develops the proteins. Served with a sweet-sour cucumber relish (ajad), they are ubiquitous at Thai markets, restaurants, and as street snacks. Light, flavourful, and satisfying.

ingredients.name: firm white fish fillets
ingredients.notes: cod, haddock, or pollock; roughly chopped

ingredients.name: kaffir lime leaves
ingredients.notes: very finely shredded, central vein removed

ingredients.name: green beans or snake beans
ingredients.notes: very finely sliced into thin rounds

steps.instruction: Make the cucumber relish: dissolve sugar in rice vinegar over gentle heat. Cool completely. Mix with diced cucumber, sliced shallot, sliced chilli, and crushed peanuts. Set aside.
Place fish, curry paste, fish sauce, egg, and palm sugar in a food processor. Pulse until you have a smooth, sticky paste — about 1 minute. Do not over-process to mush; it should be bouncy.
Transfer to a bowl. Add shredded kaffir lime leaves and sliced green beans. Mix thoroughly by hand, slapping the mixture against the bowl a few times to develop the bouncy texture.

With wet hands, shape the mixture into small flat patties (about 5 cm diameter, 1 cm thick). Heat oil to 170°C. Fry fish cakes in batches for 3-4 minutes, turning once, until deep golden. Drain on kitchen paper.
Serve hot with the cucumber relish on the side.

image_placeholder: Golden Thai fish cakes arranged on a plate with a small bowl of cucumber relish studded with peanuts and chilli, on a banana leaf.
wine_pairing_notes: A crisp Sauvignon Blanc or a cold Thai beer complements the spiced fish cakes.
tips: Slapping the fish paste against the bowl helps develop the proteins, giving the fish cakes their characteristic bouncy texture.
The kaffir lime leaves must be very finely shredded — use a sharp knife and stack them before cutting into hair-thin strips.
Fish cakes can be shaped and refrigerated for up to 24 hours before frying — they actually hold together better when chilled.

title: Tacos al Pastor
description: Tacos al pastor are Mexico City's most iconic street taco — thinly sliced pork marinated in a vibrant adobo of dried chillies, achiote paste, and pineapple, traditionally cooked on a vertical spit (trompo) inspired by Lebanese shawarma. The meat is shaved off in thin, crispy-edged slices and served on small corn tortillas with pineapple, coriander, and onion. This oven-adapted version captures the essential flavours and caramelised edges of the original.

substitutions.substitute: 2 tbsp mild chilli powder + 1 tsp paprika
1 tbsp paprika + 1 tsp cumin + 1 tsp oregano

steps.instruction: Soak guajillo and ancho chillies in boiling water for 15 minutes until soft. Drain, reserving 100 ml soaking liquid. Blend chillies with achiote paste, 60 ml pineapple juice, vinegar, garlic, cumin, oregano, salt, and reserved soaking liquid until smooth.
Coat pork slices thoroughly in the adobo marinade. Layer in a dish with pineapple slices between layers. Cover and refrigerate for at least 2 hours, ideally overnight.

Preheat oven to 200°C. Arrange marinated pork on a wire rack over a baking tray (or thread onto a skewer for a trompo effect). Place pineapple slices on top. Roast for 25-30 minutes until the edges are charred and caramelised.
Remove pork and chop into small pieces. Dice the roasted pineapple. Warm corn tortillas in a dry pan for 30 seconds per side.
Assemble tacos: fill each tortilla with pork, diced pineapple, onion, coriander, and a squeeze of lime. Serve with salsa verde.

image_placeholder: A plate of tacos al pastor on small corn tortillas with charred pork, diced pineapple, coriander, and onion, with lime wedges and salsa verde on the side.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Mexican lager like Modelo Especial or a tangy margarita are the classic pairings.
tips: Achiote paste (annatto seed paste) is the key ingredient for authentic al pastor colour and flavour — available at Latin American grocers.
For the most authentic result, finish the roasted pork under the grill for 2-3 minutes for extra charring.
Double-stack the tortillas (two per taco) as they do in Mexico — the extra layer catches the juices.

title: Mole Poblano
description: Mole poblano is the crown jewel of Mexican cuisine — a complex, deeply layered sauce of dried chillies, chocolate, nuts, seeds, spices, and dried fruit, simmered for hours into a dark, velvety masterpiece. Originating from Puebla, this dish is reserved for celebrations and special occasions. The sauce contains over 20 ingredients, each contributing to its remarkable depth — not predominantly chocolate-flavoured as many assume, but rather an intricate balance where no single ingredient dominates.

steps.instruction: Toast chillies in a dry pan for 30 seconds per side until fragrant and slightly darkened (do not burn). Soak in boiling water for 15 minutes until soft. Reserve 200 ml soaking liquid.

Dry-roast tomatoes, onion quarters, and garlic on a comal or under a grill until charred. Peel garlic. In the same dry pan, toast sesame seeds, almonds, peanuts, and raisins separately until golden. Toast cinnamon, cloves, peppercorns, and anise seeds until fragrant. Toast the stale tortilla until dark brown.

Blend the drained chillies with the charred vegetables and 200 ml reserved soaking liquid until smooth. In a separate batch, blend the toasted nuts, seeds, raisins, spices, tortilla, and oregano with 200 ml stock until smooth.

Heat lard in a large heavy pot over medium heat. Fry the chilli purée for 5 minutes, stirring constantly. Add the nut-spice purée and fry for another 5 minutes. Add remaining stock, chocolate, and piloncillo. Stir until dissolved. Simmer on low heat for 30 minutes, stirring occasionally, until thick and rich.

Meanwhile, poach chicken pieces in salted water for 25 minutes until cooked through. Add the chicken to the mole sauce and simmer for 10 minutes to absorb flavours. Serve garnished with sesame seeds alongside rice and warm tortillas.

image_placeholder: Dark, glossy mole poblano sauce draped over chicken pieces on a decorative plate, sprinkled with sesame seeds, with a stack of warm tortillas alongside.
wine_pairing_notes: A robust Zinfandel or a cold Mexican dark lager like Negra Modelo pairs beautifully with the complex sauce.
tips: This is a labour of love — plan for 3-4 hours total. The reward is one of the world's great sauces.
Mole freezes brilliantly — make a double batch and freeze in portions for future use.
Toast each ingredient separately and carefully — burnt chillies or seeds will make the mole bitter.

title: Pozole Rojo
description: Pozole is one of Mexico's most ancient and celebratory soups — a rich, deep-red broth of dried chillies simmered with pork and hominy (large-kernel corn). This pre-Hispanic dish was reserved for ritual occasions and has remained a centrepiece of Mexican fiestas. The generous toppings of shredded cabbage, radish, oregano, and lime added at the table are integral to the experience, providing fresh crunch against the rich, meaty broth.

substitutions.notes: different texture but acceptable in stew

steps.instruction: Place pork, halved onion, garlic, and salt in a large pot with 2 litres of water. Bring to a boil, skim any foam, reduce to a simmer, and cook for 1.5 hours until the pork is very tender.
Toast dried chillies in a dry pan for 30 seconds per side. Soak in boiling water for 15 minutes. Blend with 200 ml soaking liquid, cumin, and oregano until smooth. Strain through a sieve.
Remove pork from broth and shred with two forks. Strain broth. Return broth to pot and add the chilli purée and hominy. Simmer for 20 minutes.
Return shredded pork to the pot. Simmer 10 more minutes. Taste and adjust salt and oregano.
Serve in deep bowls with shredded cabbage, sliced radishes, dried oregano, lime wedges, and tostadas on the side. Each person garnishes their own bowl.

image_placeholder: A deep bowl of rich red pozole broth with visible hominy and shredded pork, topped with shredded cabbage, sliced radishes, and oregano, with lime wedges on the side.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Mexican lager or a mezcal on the side is the traditional choice.
tips: Pozole is a celebration dish — it feeds a crowd and tastes even better the next day.
The toppings are essential to the experience. Set up a 'pozole bar' with all the garnishes for guests to help themselves.
Tinned hominy works perfectly. If using dried hominy (maíz pozolero), soak overnight and cook for 2-3 hours before adding to the broth.

title: Tamales de Puerco
description: Tamales are one of the oldest foods in the Americas — bundles of seasoned masa (corn dough) filled with savoury or sweet fillings, wrapped in corn husks and steamed until firm.
