These pork tamales are filled with a red chilli-braised pork filling. Making tamales is traditionally a communal activity (tamalada), where family and friends gather to assemble dozens at a time. The masa should be light and fluffy — the test is whether a small ball floats in water.

ingredients.name: dried corn husks
ingredients.notes: soaked in hot water for 1 hour

steps.instruction: Beat lard with an electric mixer for 5 minutes until fluffy and light. Gradually add masa harina, warm stock, baking powder, and salt. Beat for another 5 minutes until the dough is light and a small ball floats in a glass of water.
For the filling: blend soaked guajillo chillies with garlic, cumin, and 100 ml water. Fry the sauce for 5 minutes, then mix with shredded pork. Season with salt.
Spread 2-3 tbsp of masa dough onto the smooth side of each soaked corn husk, leaving a border at the top and bottom. Place 1-2 tbsp of pork filling in the centre.
Fold the husk sides over the filling, then fold the bottom up. Secure with a thin strip of husk if needed. Stand tamales upright in a steamer with the open end facing up.
Steam for 60-75 minutes until the masa pulls away cleanly from the husk. Let rest 10 minutes before serving.

image_placeholder: Freshly steamed tamales partially unwrapped from their corn husks revealing the golden masa and red pork filling, arranged on a colourful Mexican ceramic plate.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Bohemia or Victoria Mexican lager, or a paloma cocktail.
tips: The float test is real — if a small ball of masa floats in water, it's light enough. If it sinks, beat longer or add more lard.
Tamales freeze beautifully — make a large batch and reheat by steaming for 15 minutes from frozen.
Lard gives the most authentic flavour and texture, but vegetable shortening is an acceptable substitute.

title: Enchiladas Verdes
description: Enchiladas verdes are corn tortillas filled with shredded chicken, rolled, and bathed in a vibrant salsa verde made from tomatillos and green chillies. Topped with crema and queso fresco, they are one of Mexico's most satisfying comfort dishes. The tomatillo-based sauce provides a bright, tangy counterpoint to the rich filling, and the dish comes together quickly once the sauce is made.

steps.instruction: Simmer tomatillos, chillies, garlic, and onion in water for 10 minutes until softened. Drain, then blend with coriander, stock, and salt until smooth.
Heat 1 tbsp oil in a pan and fry the salsa verde for 5 minutes until slightly darkened and thickened. Set aside.
Briefly fry each tortilla in remaining oil for 10 seconds per side to soften. Dip in the salsa verde, fill with shredded chicken, and roll up. Place seam-side down in a baking dish.
Pour remaining salsa verde over the enchiladas. Drizzle with crema and scatter with crumbled queso fresco.
Bake at 180°C for 15 minutes until heated through and bubbly. Serve immediately with rice and beans.

image_placeholder: A baking dish of enchiladas verdes smothered in green tomatillo salsa, drizzled with white crema and crumbled queso fresco, with fresh coriander on top.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Dos Equis lager or a crisp Albariño.
tips: Briefly frying the tortillas before filling prevents them from falling apart in the sauce.
The salsa verde must be fried in oil to develop its full flavour — this step is called 'sazonar' and is crucial.
Use leftover rotisserie chicken for a weeknight shortcut.

title: Chilaquiles Rojos
description: Chilaquiles are Mexico's ultimate breakfast comfort food — crispy tortilla chips simmered briefly in a rich salsa until they soften slightly while retaining some crunch. Topped with crema, queso fresco, onion, and a fried egg, they are the perfect balance of crispy, creamy, tangy, and spicy. This is Mexican morning sustenance at its finest — and a brilliant way to use day-old tortillas.

ingredients.name: corn tortillas
ingredients.notes: cut into triangles, or use thick tortilla chips

steps.instruction: Fry tortilla triangles in batches in hot oil (180°C) until golden and crispy, about 2 minutes. Drain on kitchen paper. Alternatively, use thick store-bought tortilla chips.
Toast chillies, then soak in boiling water for 10 minutes. Simmer tomatoes, garlic, and onion in water for 8 minutes. Blend everything with stock and salt until smooth. Strain.

Heat 1 tbsp oil in a large pan, fry the salsa for 5 minutes until thickened. Add the fried tortilla chips and toss gently in the salsa for 1-2 minutes — they should soften slightly but retain some crunch.
Fry eggs sunny-side up in a separate pan. Serve chilaquiles immediately topped with fried eggs, crema, queso fresco, sliced red onion, and coriander.

image_placeholder: A plate of red chilaquiles with crispy-soft tortilla chips in rich red salsa, topped with a sunny-side-up egg, crumbled queso fresco, sliced red onion, and fresh coriander.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold michelada (beer cocktail with lime and chilli) or fresh orange juice.
tips: The timing of adding chips to salsa is crucial — too long and they become mushy, too brief and they don't absorb enough sauce.
Day-old tortillas fry crispier than fresh ones because they've lost moisture.
Serve immediately — chilaquiles wait for no one.

title: Elote
description: Elote is Mexico's iconic street snack — grilled corn on the cob slathered with mayonnaise, cotija cheese, chilli powder, and lime. Sold from carts on virtually every Mexican street corner, the combination of charred sweetness, tangy lime, creamy mayo, salty cheese, and spicy chilli is irresistible. It is the ultimate demonstration of how a few bold condiments can transform a simple ingredient.

steps.instruction: Grill corn on a barbecue or a ridged grill pan over high heat for 10-12 minutes, turning regularly, until charred in spots all over.
Immediately brush each ear generously with mayonnaise while still hot.
Roll in crumbled cotija cheese, pressing gently so it adheres. Sprinkle with chilli powder, squeeze lime over, and scatter with coriander. Serve immediately.

image_placeholder: A charred corn cob smothered in white mayonnaise and crumbled cotija cheese, dusted with red chilli powder, with a lime wedge on the side, held on a stick.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Modelo Especial or a margarita.
tips: Get proper char on the corn — the smoky sweetness is what makes elote special.
Tajín seasoning (available at Mexican grocers) is the authentic finishing spice — a blend of chilli, lime, and salt.
For esquites (off-the-cob version), cut the grilled kernels off and serve in a cup with the same toppings plus a squeeze of lime.

title: Guacamole
description: Guacamole is Mexico's gift to the world — a simple, vibrant mash of ripe avocados with lime, salt, coriander, onion, and chilli. Dating back to the Aztecs who called it 'ahuacamolli' (avocado sauce), it remains best when kept simple, allowing the buttery avocado to shine. The key is perfectly ripe avocados and a rough, chunky texture — never smooth. It transforms any meal and is indispensable at any Mexican table.

ingredients.name: jalapeño or serrano chilli
ingredients.notes: finely diced, seeds removed for less heat

steps.instruction: Halve the avocados, remove the stones, and scoop the flesh into a bowl. Add lime juice and salt immediately.
Mash with a fork to a rough, chunky consistency — do not make it smooth. Fold in diced onion, coriander, chilli, and tomato if using.
Taste and adjust lime juice, salt, and chilli. Serve immediately with tortilla chips or as a condiment.

image_placeholder: A stone molcajete filled with chunky guacamole, scattered with coriander leaves and diced red onion, surrounded by warm tortilla chips.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Mexican lager, a margarita, or a crisp Sauvignon Blanc.
tips: Avocados must be perfectly ripe — they should yield to gentle pressure. If too firm, they'll be flavourless and starchy.
Adding lime juice immediately prevents browning and provides essential acidity.
Never use a blender or food processor — guacamole should be chunky, mashed by hand.

title: Ceviche de Camarón
description: Mexican prawn ceviche is a vibrant coastal dish from Sinaloa — fresh prawns marinated in lime juice, mixed with tomato, cucumber, red onion, avocado, and coriander, then drizzled with hot sauce. Unlike Peruvian ceviche, the Mexican version is more of a cold seafood cocktail, served with tostadas or crackers. It is refreshing, zesty, and perfect for warm weather.

steps.instruction: Place halved prawns in a glass bowl. Pour lime juice over, ensuring prawns are fully submerged. Cover and refrigerate for 25-30 minutes until prawns are opaque and pink throughout.
Drain about half the lime juice (reserve it — this is 'leche de tigre'). Add diced tomatoes, cucumber, red onion, jalapeño, coriander, and salt. Toss gently.
Just before serving, fold in diced avocado and a dash of hot sauce. Serve in glasses or bowls with tostadas for scooping. Drizzle with a little reserved leche de tigre.

image_placeholder: A glass filled with vibrant Mexican prawn ceviche showing pink prawns, diced avocado, tomato, and cucumber, with tostadas arranged alongside.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Pacifico beer or a michelada is the ideal pairing.
tips: Use the freshest prawns possible — they must smell of the sea, never fishy.
Don't marinate for more than 40 minutes or the texture becomes rubbery.
Add avocado just before serving to prevent it browning.

title: Chiles Rellenos
description: Chiles rellenos are roasted poblano peppers stuffed with melted cheese (or picadillo), dipped in a fluffy egg batter, and fried until golden. They are one of Mexico's most iconic dishes, dating back to the colonial era. The smoky-sweet poblano pepper provides the perfect vessel for the filling, and the light, cloud-like egg coating makes them irresistible. Served with a simple tomato sauce, they are pure Mexican comfort food.

substitutions.substitute: Anaheim peppers or large pointed peppers

steps.instruction: Char poblanos directly over a gas flame or under a grill, turning until blackened all over. Place in a bowl, cover with cling film for 10 minutes to steam. Peel off the charred skin, make a small slit, and carefully remove seeds without tearing.
Stuff each pepper with cheese sticks. Secure with a toothpick if needed. Dust lightly with flour.
Beat egg whites until stiff peaks form. Fold in yolks one at a time with a pinch of salt to make a light, fluffy batter.
Heat oil to 180°C. Dip each stuffed pepper in the egg batter, coating completely, and fry for 2-3 minutes per side until golden and puffed. Drain on kitchen paper.
For the sauce: blend tinned tomatoes with onion, garlic, and salt. Fry in 1 tbsp oil for 5 minutes. Serve the chiles rellenos on a pool of tomato sauce.

image_placeholder: Golden, puffy chiles rellenos on a pool of red tomato sauce, showing the melted cheese oozing from one that's been cut open, on a colourful Mexican plate.
wine_pairing_notes: A crisp Albariño or a cold Mexican lager like Bohemia.
tips: The egg batter must be freshly made and used immediately — it deflates quickly.
Charring and steaming the poblanos properly makes peeling easy. Don't wash them under water or you'll lose flavour.
Poblano heat varies — taste a small piece first. If very mild, add a strip of jalapeño inside with the cheese.

title: Sopa Azteca
description: Sopa azteca (tortilla soup) is a deeply flavoured tomato-chilli broth served with crispy tortilla strips, avocado, cheese, and crema. It is one of Mexico's most comforting soups, combining the smoky richness of dried pasilla chillies with a robust tomato base. The garnishes added at the table — crispy tortillas, creamy avocado, tangy cheese — transform each bowlful into a textural masterpiece.

steps.instruction: Fry tortilla strips in hot oil until golden and crispy, about 2 minutes. Drain on kitchen paper. In the same oil, fry the pasilla chillies for 10 seconds until puffed. Remove and set aside.
Char tomatoes, onion, and garlic under the grill or in a dry pan until blackened. Blend with a fried pasilla chilli and 200 ml stock until smooth.
Heat 1 tbsp oil in a pot, fry the tomato purée for 5 minutes until darkened. Add remaining stock, bring to a simmer, and cook for 15 minutes. Season with salt.
Ladle soup into bowls. Top with crispy tortilla strips, diced avocado, crumbled queso fresco, a drizzle of crema, and remaining fried pasilla chilli crumbled on top. Serve with lime wedges.

image_placeholder: A bowl of deep red tortilla soup topped with crispy tortilla strips, diced avocado, crumbled white cheese, and a drizzle of cream, with a lime wedge on the rim.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Negra Modelo or a crisp rosé.
tips: Charring the tomatoes develops a deep, smoky sweetness that is the backbone of this soup.
Keep tortilla strips separate until serving so they stay crispy.
The soup base can be made ahead and refrigerated — add the garnishes just before serving.

title: Cochinita Pibil
description: Cochinita pibil is the signature dish of the Yucatán Peninsula — pork marinated in achiote, bitter orange juice, and spices, then wrapped in banana leaves and slow-roasted until falling apart. The dish dates back to Mayan cooking traditions where meat was buried in a pit (pib) with hot stones. The result is incredibly tender, bright orange pork with a distinctive citrusy, earthy flavour unlike any other Mexican preparation.

substitutions.substitute: 75 ml orange juice + 75 ml lime juice

ingredients.name: red onion
ingredients.notes: thinly sliced, pickled in lime juice

steps.instruction: Blend achiote paste, bitter orange juice, garlic, cumin, peppercorns, oregano, and salt into a smooth marinade. Score the pork deeply and rub the marinade all over and into the cuts. Marinate for at least 4 hours, ideally overnight.

Preheat oven to 150°C. Line a deep roasting tin with wilted banana leaves, leaving overhang. Place the marinated pork in the centre, pour any remaining marinade over, and fold the banana leaves to enclose completely.
Cover tightly with foil and roast for 3.5-4 hours until the pork is fall-apart tender. Remove from oven and shred the pork with two forks, mixing with the cooking juices.
Serve on warm tortillas with pickled red onions and a whole roasted habanero on the side (for the brave).

image_placeholder: Shredded bright orange cochinita pibil on corn tortillas with pink pickled onions, served on a banana leaf with a habanero chilli on the side.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Montejo beer or a mezcal paloma cocktail.
tips: Achiote paste (recado rojo) is essential and available at Mexican/Latin American grocers. It provides the distinctive colour and flavour.
The banana leaf wrapping adds a subtle herbaceous flavour and keeps the meat incredibly moist.
Cochinita pibil is perfect for batch cooking — it freezes well and makes amazing tacos, tortas, and quesadillas.

title: Churros con Chocolate
description: Churros are crispy, ridged sticks of fried dough coated in cinnamon sugar, served with thick hot chocolate for dipping. While popularised by Spain, churros have a long and beloved tradition in Mexico, where they are sold from street carts and churrerías. The outside is shattering crispy while the inside remains light and slightly doughy. Paired with a thick Mexican-style hot chocolate, they are pure indulgence.

steps.instruction: Bring water, butter, 2 tbsp sugar, and salt to a boil in a saucepan. Remove from heat and add flour all at once. Beat vigorously with a wooden spoon until the dough forms a smooth ball that pulls away from the sides.
Let cool for 2 minutes, then beat in eggs one at a time and vanilla until smooth and glossy. Transfer to a piping bag fitted with a large star nozzle.
Heat oil to 180°C. Pipe 10-12 cm lengths of dough directly into the hot oil, cutting with scissors. Fry in batches for 3-4 minutes, turning once, until deep golden and crispy.
Mix 80 g caster sugar with 1.5 tsp cinnamon on a plate. Roll hot churros in the cinnamon sugar immediately after draining.
For the chocolate sauce: heat cream and milk until simmering. Pour over chopped chocolate and stir until smooth and thick. Serve churros warm with the chocolate sauce for dipping.

image_placeholder: Golden, cinnamon-sugar-coated churros piled on parchment paper next to a small cup of thick dark chocolate sauce for dipping.
tips: The dough is essentially choux pastry — it must be smooth before adding eggs, and each egg must be fully incorporated before adding the next.
A star-shaped nozzle creates the classic ridged surface, which increases the crispy surface area.
Churros are best eaten immediately — they lose their crispiness within 20 minutes.

title: Horchata
description: Horchata is Mexico's beloved agua fresca — a refreshing, milky-white drink made from soaked rice, cinnamon, and sugar, blended and strained into a creamy beverage. Unlike its Spanish ancestor (made with tiger nuts), Mexican horchata uses rice and sometimes almonds. Served ice-cold, it is the perfect companion to spicy food, providing a cooling, sweet contrast. It is found at every taquería and street stall across Mexico.

ingredients.name: water
ingredients.notes: warm for soaking + cold for blending

steps.instruction: Soak rice and cinnamon sticks in 600 ml warm water for at least 4 hours, ideally overnight.
Remove cinnamon sticks. Blend the soaked rice with its water in a blender on high speed for 2-3 minutes until very smooth.
Strain through a fine muslin cloth or nut milk bag, squeezing to extract all the liquid. Discard the pulp.
Stir in remaining 600 ml cold water, sugar, and vanilla. Mix until sugar dissolves. Refrigerate until very cold. Serve over ice with a dusting of ground cinnamon.

image_placeholder: A tall glass of milky-white horchata filled with ice, dusted with ground cinnamon, with a cinnamon stick as garnish, on a colourful Mexican tile background.
tips: Longer soaking (overnight) produces a creamier, more flavourful horchata.
Strain thoroughly — any grit from the rice will make the drink unpleasant.
Some versions add blanched almonds or condensed milk for extra richness.

title: Carnitas
description: Carnitas ('little meats') are a Michoacán speciality — pork shoulder braised slowly in its own fat and juices until impossibly tender, then crisped under a hot grill for caramelised, golden edges. The meat is pulled into chunks that are simultaneously melt-in-the-mouth and crispy — an addictive contrast that makes carnitas one of Mexico's most beloved taco fillings. Traditionally cooked in large copper pots, this oven method produces excellent results.

steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 150°C. Season pork chunks with salt, pepper, cumin, and oregano. Heat lard in a Dutch oven over high heat and brown the pork on all sides in batches, about 8 minutes total.
Add onion, garlic, bay leaves, orange juice, lime juice, and stock. Bring to a simmer, cover tightly, and transfer to the oven. Braise for 3 hours until the meat is fall-apart tender.
Remove pork and shred with two forks into rough chunks. Strain and reserve the braising liquid.

Spread shredded pork on a baking tray, spoon over some braising liquid, and grill under high heat for 5-8 minutes until the edges are crispy and caramelised. Serve in warm corn tortillas with salsa, coriander, onion, and lime.

image_placeholder: Crispy-edged shredded carnitas piled on small corn tortillas with diced onion, coriander, and a squeeze of lime, arranged on a wooden board.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Mexican lager or a smoky mezcal cocktail.
tips: The grilling step at the end is essential — it creates those irresistible crispy edges that define great carnitas.
Don't discard the braising liquid — use it to moisten the meat before serving and as a base for sauces.
Carnitas are perfect for batch cooking and freeze beautifully.

title: Ceviche Clásico
description: Peruvian ceviche is the national dish — ultra-fresh white fish cured in lime juice (leche de tigre) with ají limo chillies, red onion, and coriander, served with sweet potato and toasted corn (cancha). Unlike Mexican ceviche, Peruvian ceviche is prepared and served immediately — the fish should barely be 'cooked' by the lime, retaining a silky, almost raw centre. The resulting leche de tigre (tiger's milk) is so prized it is often drunk as a shot.

ingredients.name: very fresh white fish fillets
ingredients.notes: sea bass, sole, or halibut; diced into 2 cm cubes

steps.instruction: Soak sliced red onion in ice-cold salted water for 10 minutes to reduce harshness. Drain and pat dry.

Place fish cubes in a glass bowl. Add garlic, ginger, ají limo, and salt. Pour lime juice over — the fish should be mostly covered. Let stand for 3-5 minutes only (traditional Peruvian style is barely cured).
Add drained red onion and coriander. Toss gently. The liquid (leche de tigre) should be slightly milky from the fish proteins.
Serve immediately in a shallow bowl with the leche de tigre, accompanied by sliced boiled sweet potato and cancha. Eat within 15 minutes.

image_placeholder: A shallow white bowl of Peruvian ceviche with translucent fish cubes, red onion slivers, and coriander in milky leche de tigre, with sweet potato slices and cancha on the side.
wine_pairing_notes: A Peruvian pisco sour or a crisp Sauvignon Blanc from Ica Valley.
tips: Freshness is non-negotiable — the fish must be sashimi-grade. If it smells fishy, don't make ceviche.
Cure for no more than 5 minutes for authentic Peruvian style. The fish should still be translucent in the centre.
The leche de tigre is considered a hangover cure in Peru — serve it as a shot alongside the ceviche.

title: Lomo Saltado
description: Lomo saltado is Peru's beloved chifa (Chinese-Peruvian) dish — tender strips of beef stir-fried with onions, tomatoes, and ají amarillo, flambéed with pisco, and served over chips alongside rice. Yes, chips and rice together. This fusion of Chinese wok technique with Peruvian ingredients is emblematic of Lima's multicultural cuisine, and is arguably the country's most popular everyday dish after ceviche.

substitutions.substitute: yellow chilli paste or 1 yellow pepper + pinch cayenne
substitutions.notes: less authentic but provides colour and mild heat

steps.instruction: Deep-fry the potato chips at 180°C until golden and crispy. Drain and keep warm. Season beef strips with salt, pepper, and cumin.
Heat oil in a wok or large pan over the highest heat. Sear beef strips in two batches for 1-2 minutes until browned but still pink inside. Remove and set aside.
In the same wok, stir-fry onion wedges for 2 minutes. Add garlic and ají amarillo paste, cook 30 seconds. Add tomato wedges and cook 1 minute — they should hold their shape.
Return beef to the wok. Add soy sauce and red wine vinegar. Toss vigorously for 30 seconds. Add fried chips and coriander, toss once more, and serve immediately alongside steamed rice.

image_placeholder: A plate of lomo saltado with seared beef strips, tomato and onion wedges, and golden chips, served beside a mound of steamed white rice.
wine_pairing_notes: A Cusqueña beer or a Malbec from Argentina (popular in Peru).
tips: The wok must be screaming hot for proper searing — if the beef stews instead of searing, the dish loses its character.
Tomatoes should be added at the very end and barely cooked — they should retain their shape and freshness.
Serving chips and rice together is non-negotiable in Peru — it's the best part.

title: Ají de Gallina
description: Ají de gallina is Peru's ultimate comfort dish — shredded chicken in a creamy, golden sauce thickened with bread and ground walnuts, flavoured with ají amarillo chillies, Parmesan, and evaporated milk. The result is rich, mildly spicy, and utterly addictive. This colonial-era dish has become a weekday favourite across Peru, served over rice with boiled eggs, olives, and boiled potatoes.

ingredients.name: white bread
ingredients.notes: crusts removed, soaked in 150 ml evaporated milk

substitutions.notes: lacks authentic flavour but provides colour

steps.instruction: Poach chicken breasts in salted water for 20 minutes until cooked through. Reserve 300 ml stock. Shred the chicken finely with two forks.
Blend soaked bread with walnuts, evaporated milk, and 100 ml chicken stock until smooth.
Heat oil in a large pan. Sauté onion for 5 minutes until softened. Add garlic and ají amarillo paste, cook 2 minutes. Add cumin.

Pour in the bread-walnut mixture and remaining stock. Stir well and simmer for 10 minutes, stirring frequently, until thickened. Add shredded chicken and Parmesan. Cook 5 more minutes. Season with salt.
Serve over steamed rice, garnished with halved boiled eggs and black olives.

image_placeholder: A plate of ají de gallina — golden, creamy shredded chicken sauce over white rice, garnished with halved boiled eggs and black olives.
wine_pairing_notes: A crisp Peruvian Torrontés or a Cusqueña lager.
tips: Ají amarillo paste is available at Latin American grocers and online — it's the soul of Peruvian cooking.
The bread-walnut mixture is the secret to the sauce's creamy thickness without needing a roux.
This dish reheats beautifully — make a big batch for weeknight meals.

title: Causa Limeña
description: Causa limeña is a cold terrine of seasoned mashed potatoes layered with avocado, chicken or tuna, and mayonnaise. The potatoes are mashed with ají amarillo and lime juice, creating a vibrant yellow, tangy base. This elegant dish is perfect for warm weather and celebrations — it looks stunning when unmoulded and sliced, revealing its colourful layers. It dates back to pre-Columbian times when potatoes were seasoned with chilli peppers.

steps.instruction: Boil potatoes until very tender, about 20 minutes. Drain well and rice or mash until completely smooth with no lumps. While still warm, mix in ají amarillo paste, lime juice, oil, and salt. The mixture should be smooth, pliable, and yellow.
Mix shredded chicken with mayonnaise, diced red onion, and a squeeze of lime. Season with salt.

Line a rectangular mould or loaf tin with cling film. Press half the potato mixture into the base in an even layer. Add a layer of sliced avocado, then the chicken mixture, then another layer of avocado. Top with remaining potato, pressing down firmly.
Refrigerate for at least 1 hour. Invert onto a serving plate, remove cling film. Garnish with olives and coriander. Slice into portions with a sharp knife.

image_placeholder: A sliced causa limeña showing distinct yellow potato layers sandwiching green avocado and white chicken filling, garnished with olives on a white plate.
wine_pairing_notes: A Peruvian pisco sour or a crisp Verdejo.
tips: The potatoes must be riced or mashed very smooth — any lumps will spoil the elegant texture.
Work with the potato mixture while still warm — it becomes difficult to shape when cold.
Causa can be made individual portions using ring moulds for an elegant starter.

title: Anticuchos
description: Anticuchos are Peru's most popular street food — tender, charcoal-grilled skewers of marinated beef heart with a deeply flavoured ají panca and cumin marinade. Dating back to pre-Columbian times and influenced by African and Spanish cooking, they are found at every street corner and festival in Lima. The beef heart, when properly marinated, is remarkably tender and flavourful — converting even sceptics with its charred, smoky deliciousness.

ingredients.name: beef heart
ingredients.notes: trimmed of fat and sinew, cut into 3 cm cubes

substitutions.notes: easier to find but less traditional

substitutions.substitute: 2 tbsp mild chilli paste + 1 tsp smoked paprika

steps.instruction: Combine ají panca paste, vinegar, garlic, cumin, oregano, salt, pepper, and oil. Marinate the beef heart cubes in this mixture for at least 4 hours, ideally overnight.
Thread marinated meat onto metal or soaked wooden skewers. Heat a barbecue or grill pan to high.
Grill skewers for 2-3 minutes per side, basting with remaining marinade, until charred outside but still slightly pink inside. Do not overcook or the heart becomes tough.
Serve immediately with boiled potatoes and corn, with a drizzle of any remaining marinade as sauce.

image_placeholder: Charred anticucho skewers with a dark, glossy ají panca glaze, served alongside a boiled potato and corn on the cob, on a wooden board.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Cusqueña dark lager or a Malbec.
tips: Beef heart is surprisingly affordable and incredibly flavourful. Ask your butcher to trim it.
The extended marinade is essential — the vinegar and spices tenderise the heart perfectly.
Don't overcook — beef heart should be medium at most, or it becomes chewy.

title: Papa a la Huancaína
description: Papa a la huancaína is one of Peru's most iconic starters — boiled potatoes smothered in a bright yellow, creamy cheese and ají amarillo sauce. Named after the city of Huancayo, this cold dish is found on virtually every Peruvian restaurant menu. The sauce is silky, mildly spicy, and addictively rich, made by blending queso fresco, ají amarillo, evaporated milk, and crackers.

steps.instruction: Boil potatoes until tender, about 20 minutes. Drain, cool, and slice into 1 cm rounds.
Blend queso fresco, ají amarillo paste, evaporated milk, crackers, oil, garlic, and salt until completely smooth and pourable. Add more milk if too thick.
Arrange lettuce leaves on a plate, layer potato slices on top. Pour the huancaína sauce generously over the potatoes. Garnish with halved eggs and olives.

image_placeholder: Sliced boiled potatoes on lettuce leaves covered in bright yellow huancaína sauce, garnished with halved eggs and black olives on a white plate.
wine_pairing_notes: A pisco sour or a crisp Sauvignon Blanc.
tips: The sauce should be pourable but not watery — adjust thickness with evaporated milk.
Ají amarillo paste gives the sauce its signature golden colour and gentle heat.
Serve cold or at room temperature — this is not a hot dish.

title: Arroz con Pollo
description: Peruvian arroz con pollo is distinct from other Latin American versions — the rice gets its vibrant green colour from a purée of coriander and beer, cooked with chicken, peas, and ají amarillo. It is a beloved family dish that appears on tables across Peru multiple times a week, each household having its own variation. The beer adds a subtle depth and helps create fluffy, flavourful rice.

steps.instruction: Blend coriander with beer until completely smooth and bright green. Set aside. Season chicken with salt, pepper, and cumin.
Heat oil in a large, heavy pot. Brown chicken pieces for 4 minutes per side until golden. Remove and set aside.
Sauté onion, garlic, and ají amarillo paste for 5 minutes. Add rice and stir for 1 minute to toast. Pour in coriander-beer mixture and chicken stock. Bring to a boil.

Nestle chicken pieces into the rice. Add peas, carrot, and red pepper. Cover tightly and cook on low heat for 25 minutes until rice is fluffy and chicken is cooked through. Let rest 5 minutes before serving.

image_placeholder: A large serving dish of vibrant green Peruvian arroz con pollo with golden chicken pieces nestled in green-flecked rice with peas and red pepper.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Cusqueña lager or a Peruvian Torrontés.
tips: The coriander must be blended until completely smooth for the vivid green colour.
Use a tight-fitting lid and resist lifting it during cooking — the steam is essential for fluffy rice.
This is a complete one-pot meal — no sides needed.

title: Tiradito
description: Tiradito is Peru's elegant cousin of ceviche — thinly sliced raw fish (like sashimi) dressed with a vibrant ají amarillo tiger's milk and drizzled with olive oil. Unlike ceviche, the fish is not cubed but sliced paper-thin, and there is no onion. This Nikkei (Japanese-Peruvian) fusion dish showcases the beautiful cultural interchange of Lima's cuisine, combining Japanese knife skills with Peruvian flavours.

steps.instruction: Using a very sharp knife, slice the fish into paper-thin slices (3-4 mm thick), slicing against the grain. Arrange in a single layer on a chilled plate.
Blend lime juice, ají amarillo paste, ginger, garlic, fish stock, and salt until smooth. This is the leche de tigre.
Just before serving, spoon the leche de tigre over the fish slices. Drizzle with olive oil. Scatter with coriander and sesame seeds. Serve immediately.

image_placeholder: Paper-thin slices of white fish fanned on a white plate, drizzled with bright yellow ají amarillo leche de tigre and olive oil, with micro coriander and sesame seeds.
wine_pairing_notes: A crisp Albariño or a pisco sour.
tips: The sharpest possible knife is essential — a dull knife will tear rather than slice the fish.
Keep the fish refrigerated until the moment of slicing and serve on a chilled plate.
The leche de tigre should be poured just before serving so the acid doesn't 'cook' the thin slices.

title: Suspiro a la Limeña
description: Suspiro a la limeña ('sigh of a woman from Lima') is Peru's most romantic dessert — a velvety manjar blanco (dulce de leche) base topped with a cloud of port wine meringue. The base is made by slowly reducing milk with sugar and egg yolks until thick and caramel-like. The poet José Gálvez named it 'suspiro' because it is as soft and sweet as a woman's sigh. Served in small portions, it is intensely sweet and utterly indulgent.

steps.instruction: Combine condensed milk and evaporated milk in a heavy saucepan. Cook over low heat, stirring frequently, for 30-40 minutes until thickened to a dulce de leche consistency and golden.

Temper the egg yolks: whisk yolks in a bowl, then gradually add small spoonfuls of the hot milk mixture while whisking continuously. Pour back into the saucepan and cook, stirring, for 5 minutes until thick. Stir in vanilla. Pour into serving glasses or ramekins. Cool completely.
Make the meringue: whisk egg whites until soft peaks form. Gradually add sugar while whisking to stiff, glossy peaks. Fold in port wine gently.
Spoon or pipe meringue on top of the cooled dulce de leche base. Dust with ground cinnamon and serve chilled.

image_placeholder: Small glass cups of suspiro a la limeña with a golden dulce de leche base topped with a swirl of white port meringue, dusted with cinnamon.
wine_pairing_notes: A glass of late-harvest Muscat or a small cup of espresso.
tips: Stir the milk mixture constantly to prevent scorching on the bottom.
The base must be completely cool before adding the meringue, or it will deflate.
This is intensely sweet — serve in small portions. Espresso cups or small glasses work perfectly.

title: Picarones
title_en: Peruvian Sweet Potato and Pumpkin Doughnuts
description: Picarones are Peru's beloved street dessert — ring-shaped doughnuts made from sweet potato and pumpkin dough, deep-fried to golden perfection and drizzled with warm chancaca (spiced molasses) syrup. They are the Peruvian answer to churros, with a distinctly Andean character from the sweet potato and squash. Dating back to colonial Lima, they are still sold by picaroneras (street vendors) in parks and plazas across the city.

steps.instruction: Boil sweet potato and pumpkin until very soft, about 15 minutes. Drain well, then mash until completely smooth. Cool to lukewarm.
Combine flour, yeast, sugar, salt, and anise seeds. Mix in the mashed vegetables to form a soft, slightly sticky dough. Knead for 5 minutes. Cover and leave to rise for 1 hour.
Make the chancaca syrup: combine panela, water, cinnamon, cloves, and orange peel. Simmer for 15-20 minutes until thick and syrupy. Strain.
Heat oil to 170°C. Take a handful of dough, shape into a ring (wrap around a wet finger), and carefully lower into the oil. Fry for 3-4 minutes, turning, until deep golden. Drain on kitchen paper.
Drizzle warm chancaca syrup over the picarones and serve immediately.

image_placeholder: Golden ring-shaped picarones stacked on a plate, drizzled with dark, glossy chancaca syrup, with a cinnamon stick as garnish.
tips: The dough should be soft and sticky — don't add too much flour or the picarones will be dense.
Wet your hands before shaping to prevent sticking.
The chancaca syrup can be made ahead and reheated before serving.

title: Jerk Chicken
description: Jerk chicken is Jamaica's most iconic dish — chicken marinated in an intensely flavoured paste of Scotch bonnet peppers, allspice (pimento), thyme, and scallions, then slow-cooked over pimento wood. The word 'jerk' refers to both the seasoning method and the cooking technique, originally developed by the Maroons (escaped African slaves) in the Blue Mountains. The result is smoky, fiery, aromatic, and deeply spiced — one of the Caribbean's greatest contributions to world cuisine.

steps.instruction: Blend all ingredients except the chicken into a smooth jerk paste using a food processor or blender. It should be thick and vibrant.
Rub the jerk paste thoroughly into the scored chicken, getting it deep into the cuts. Cover and marinate in the fridge for at least 4 hours, ideally overnight.
Preheat a barbecue for indirect cooking (or oven to 190°C). If using a barbecue, set up for two-zone cooking. Place chicken on the cooler side, cover, and cook for 35-40 minutes.
Move chicken to direct heat (or under a hot grill) for 5-8 minutes to char the skin. The chicken should be cooked through (75°C internal) with a dark, caramelised exterior.
Rest for 5 minutes. Serve with rice and peas, fried plantain, and coleslaw.

image_placeholder: Charred jerk chicken with a dark, glossy crust on a wooden board, served with a lime wedge, with a bowl of rice and peas in the background.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Red Stripe beer or a rum punch with fresh tropical fruit.
tips: Scoring the chicken deeply is essential — the jerk paste needs to penetrate to the bone for full flavour.
Overnight marinating transforms this dish — the flavours develop exponentially.
Authentic jerk should be cooked over pimento (allspice) wood; soaking allspice berries and adding them to charcoal approximates this.

title: Ackee and Saltfish
description: Ackee and saltfish is Jamaica's national dish — tender, creamy ackee fruit sautéed with flaked salt cod, peppers, onions, and Scotch bonnet chilli. The ackee has a delicate, almost egg-like texture that pairs beautifully with the salty, flaked fish. This breakfast staple is served with fried dumplings, breadfruit, or boiled green bananas. Though ackee is a tropical fruit, it is cooked and treated as a savoury ingredient.

steps.instruction: Soak salt cod in cold water overnight, changing water 2-3 times. Boil for 15 minutes until soft. Drain, remove skin and bones, and flake into pieces.
Heat oil in a large frying pan. Sauté onion and red pepper for 3 minutes. Add garlic, thyme, and whole Scotch bonnet pepper. Cook 2 minutes.

Add flaked salt cod, tomato, and spring onions. Stir gently for 3 minutes. Add drained ackee and fold in very gently — ackee breaks apart easily. Season with black pepper (usually no salt needed due to the cod).
Cook for 3-4 minutes until ackee is heated through. Remove the whole Scotch bonnet. Serve with fried dumplings, hard dough bread, or boiled green bananas.

image_placeholder: A plate of golden ackee and flaked saltfish with diced red peppers and spring onions, served alongside fried dumplings and sliced avocado.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Red Stripe beer or a glass of freshly squeezed tropical juice.
tips: Handle ackee very gently — it disintegrates easily. Fold it in at the end and don't stir aggressively.
Keep the Scotch bonnet whole — it imparts flavour without too much heat. Bursting it makes the dish very fiery.
Soak the salt cod for at least 12 hours, changing the water several times, to properly desalt it.

title: Rice and Peas
description: Rice and peas is Jamaica's quintessential side dish — fluffy rice cooked in coconut milk with kidney beans (called 'peas' in Jamaica), garlic, thyme, and Scotch bonnet pepper. It accompanies virtually every Jamaican main course, especially Sunday dinner. The coconut milk makes the rice rich and fragrant, while the kidney beans add protein and substance. It is comfort food in its purest form.

steps.instruction: Combine coconut milk, water, and reserved bean liquid in a pot. Add garlic, thyme, whole Scotch bonnet, spring onions, allspice berries, salt, and pepper. Bring to a boil.
Add rice and kidney beans. Stir once, bring back to a boil, then reduce heat to very low. Cover tightly and cook for 20-25 minutes until all liquid is absorbed and rice is fluffy.
Remove thyme, spring onions, Scotch bonnet, and allspice. Fluff with a fork and serve.

image_placeholder: A bowl of fluffy Jamaican rice and peas with visible kidney beans, garnished with a sprig of thyme, served alongside jerk chicken.
wine_pairing_notes: Accompaniment; typically paired with jerk chicken and a Red Stripe beer.
tips: Keep the Scotch bonnet whole — it infuses flavour without overwhelming heat.
Don't stir the rice after covering — let the steam do the work.
Use the liquid from the tinned kidney beans — it adds colour and flavour to the rice.

title: Doubles
description: Doubles are Trinidad's most beloved street food — two soft, fried flatbreads (bara) sandwiching a curried chickpea filling (channa), topped with tamarind sauce, cucumber chutney, and pepper sauce. Introduced by Indian indentured labourers, doubles have become Trinidad's national snack, sold from roadside stalls from dawn onwards. The combination of soft, slightly sweet bread with spicy, tangy chickpeas is utterly addictive.

substitutions.notes: skip yeast; less traditional but quicker

steps.instruction: Make the bara dough: combine flour, turmeric, yeast, sugar, and salt. Add warm water and mix to a soft, slightly sticky dough. Knead 5 minutes. Cover and prove for 1 hour until doubled.

For the channa: heat 2 tbsp oil, sauté onion for 5 minutes. Add garlic, curry powder, cumin, turmeric, and Scotch bonnet. Cook 2 minutes. Add chickpeas and water. Simmer 15 minutes until the sauce is thick and the chickpeas are soft. Mash a few chickpeas for thickness.
Divide dough into 16 small balls. Flatten each into a disc about 8 cm diameter. Heat oil to 180°C and fry each bara for 30-45 seconds per side until puffed and golden.
Assemble doubles: place one bara flat, spoon channa on top, drizzle with tamarind sauce, top with a second bara. Fold like a taco and eat immediately.

image_placeholder: Two soft, golden bara flatbreads sandwiching curried channa with drizzles of dark tamarind sauce, held in a paper napkin as street food.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Carib lager or a fresh coconut water.
tips: The bara must be soft and pliable, not crispy — underfry slightly if needed.
The channa should be saucy but not watery — it needs to stay on the bara without dripping excessively.
Doubles are meant to be eaten immediately — the bread softens further as it absorbs the channa juices.

title: Callaloo
description: Callaloo is the Caribbean's great communal soup — a thick, verdant stew of dasheen (taro) leaves or amaranth, okra, coconut milk, and often crab. Found across the islands with countless variations, the Trinidadian version is the most celebrated, blended to a smooth, creamy consistency and enriched with coconut milk. It is deeply nourishing, packed with iron, and served alongside rice or provision (root vegetables).

ingredients.name: callaloo leaves (dasheen or amaranth)
ingredients.notes: washed and chopped; or use spinach

steps.instruction: Heat butter in a large pot. Sauté onion and garlic for 5 minutes. Add okra and cook 3 minutes.
Add callaloo leaves, thyme, whole Scotch bonnet, and stock. Bring to a boil, reduce heat, and simmer for 20 minutes until the leaves are very tender and the okra has broken down.
Remove Scotch bonnet and thyme. Add coconut milk. Use a stick blender to blend until smooth (or leave slightly chunky). Season with salt and pepper.
Simmer for 5 more minutes. Serve with steamed rice or boiled provision.

image_placeholder: A bowl of vibrant green callaloo soup with a silky, creamy texture, garnished with a drizzle of coconut milk and served alongside white rice.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Carib beer or fresh coconut water.
tips: Dasheen (taro) leaves are available at Caribbean and Asian markets. Spinach is the most accessible substitute.
The okra acts as a natural thickener — don't skip it even if you're not an okra fan.
Keep the Scotch bonnet whole to infuse flavour without too much heat.

title: Curry Goat
description: Curry goat is Jamaica's celebration dish — bone-in goat meat slow-cooked in a rich, aromatic curry sauce until fall-off-the-bone tender. Introduced by Indian labourers, it has been adopted and adapted into something uniquely Jamaican, with the addition of Scotch bonnet, allspice, and thyme. It is the centrepiece of any Jamaican party or Sunday dinner, served with rice and peas or white rice.

ingredients.name: bone-in goat meat
ingredients.notes: cut into chunks by the butcher

substitutions.notes: easier to find; slightly milder flavour

steps.instruction: Season goat meat with 2 tbsp curry powder, salt, allspice, garlic, ginger, and thyme. Cover and marinate for at least 2 hours, ideally overnight.
Heat oil in a large heavy pot. Brown the goat in batches over high heat for 3-4 minutes per side. Remove and set aside.

Sauté onions and spring onions for 5 minutes. Add remaining curry powder and cook 1 minute. Return goat to pot, add whole Scotch bonnet and water. Bring to a boil, reduce to a low simmer, cover and cook for 1.5-2 hours until the meat is very tender.
Add potatoes and cook a further 20 minutes until potatoes are tender and sauce has thickened. Adjust seasoning and serve with rice and peas.

image_placeholder: A deep plate of rich, dark curry goat with tender pieces of meat and chunks of potato in a thick curry sauce, served with rice and peas.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Red Stripe beer or a rum punch.
tips: Goat meat is available at halal butchers, Caribbean shops, and some farmers' markets. Ask the butcher to cut it into curry pieces.
Marinating overnight is the secret — the curry spices penetrate the meat deeply.
Low and slow is essential — goat needs time to become tender. Don't rush it.

title: Pelau
description: Pelau is Trinidad's answer to jollof rice — a one-pot dish of caramelised chicken, pigeon peas, and rice cooked in coconut milk. The distinctive step is 'browning' the chicken in caramelised sugar, which gives the dish its deep colour and complex sweetness. It is Trinidad's most popular everyday meal, served at lime (social gatherings) and fêtes alike. Each household has its own version, but the browning step is non-negotiable.

steps.instruction: Heat oil and sugar in a large, heavy pot over medium-high heat. When the sugar melts and turns dark brown (careful not to burn), add the chicken pieces and toss quickly to coat. Cook for 5 minutes until caramelised.
Add onion, garlic, thyme, and whole Scotch bonnet. Stir for 2 minutes. Add browning sauce if using.

Add rice and pigeon peas. Pour in coconut milk and water. Season with salt. Bring to a boil, then reduce to very low heat. Cover tightly and cook for 25-30 minutes until rice is fluffy and liquid is absorbed.
Remove Scotch bonnet and thyme sprigs. Fluff with a fork and serve.

image_placeholder: A large serving plate of Trinidadian pelau with dark caramelised chicken pieces nestled in coconut rice and pigeon peas, with thyme sprigs on top.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Carib lager or a rum and coconut water.
tips: The browning step is what makes pelau unique — the sugar must turn very dark but not burnt. It happens fast, so stay vigilant.
Don't lift the lid while cooking — the steam is doing the work.
Pelau is a complete meal — no sides needed, though hot pepper sauce is always welcome.

title: Tostones
description: Tostones are twice-fried green plantain rounds — a beloved snack and side dish found across the Caribbean and Latin America. Green (unripe) plantains are sliced, fried once until soft, flattened, then fried again until golden and crispy. The result is a starchy, savoury chip with a satisfying crunch that pairs with everything from stews to dipping sauces. They are the Caribbean's answer to chips.

steps.instruction: Peel the plantains (score the skin lengthways with a knife and peel away). Cut into 3 cm thick rounds.
Heat oil to 170°C. Fry plantain rounds for 3-4 minutes until pale golden and slightly soft inside. Remove and drain.
While still warm, flatten each round to about 1 cm thickness using a tostonera (plantain press) or the bottom of a glass.
Increase oil to 180°C. Fry the flattened plantains again for 2-3 minutes until deep golden and crispy. Drain on kitchen paper and sprinkle immediately with salt. Serve hot.

image_placeholder: A plate of golden, crispy tostones stacked and sprinkled with coarse salt, with a bowl of garlic mojo dipping sauce on the side.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold beer or a mojito.
tips: The plantains must be green and firm — ripe plantains will be too sweet and soft for tostones.
The double-frying is what creates the perfect crispy exterior and soft interior.
Sprinkle salt immediately after the second fry while the tostones are still hot and glistening.

title: Caribbean Rum Cake
description: Caribbean rum cake is a rich, moist, dark fruit cake soaked in rum — a staple of Christmas celebrations across the islands. Dark fruits are soaked in rum for weeks (or months) before baking, and the finished cake is doused in more rum. The result is intensely flavoured, deeply aromatic, and improves with age. It is a living legacy of British colonial Christmas cake traditions, adapted with Caribbean rum and tropical spices.

ingredients.name: dark rum
ingredients.notes: plus extra for soaking after baking

substitutions.notes: slightly different character but works well

steps.instruction: At least 1 week before (ideally 1-3 months): soak mixed dried fruit in 200 ml rum in an airtight jar, stirring weekly.
Preheat oven to 160°C. Line a 23 cm round cake tin. Cream butter and sugar until light and fluffy. Beat in eggs one at a time. Add vanilla, browning sauce, and lime zest.
Fold in flour, mixed spice, and nutmeg. Fold in the rum-soaked fruit with any remaining liquid. Pour into prepared tin.
Bake for 1.5-2 hours until a skewer comes out clean. If the top browns too quickly, cover with foil. Let cool in tin for 30 minutes.
While still warm, poke holes all over with a skewer and pour remaining 100 ml rum over the cake. Wrap tightly in baking parchment and foil. The cake improves over days and weeks.

image_placeholder: A rich, dark Caribbean rum cake on a cake stand, sliced to show the dense, fruit-studded interior, dusted with icing sugar.
wine_pairing_notes: A glass of aged dark rum, neat.
tips: The longer you soak the fruit in rum, the better the cake. Start months ahead for Christmas.
The cake keeps for weeks wrapped tightly — it actually improves with age as the flavours meld.
Feed the cake with small amounts of rum weekly for an even moister, more flavourful result.

title: Roti with Chickpea Curry
description: Trinidadian roti is the island's most popular lunch — a soft, flaky flatbread (dhalpuri or buss-up-shut) wrapped around a curried chickpea or meat filling. Brought by Indian indentured labourers, roti has become as Trinidadian as soca music. The buss-up-shut (bust-up-shirt) roti gets its name from the torn, layered texture achieved by clapping and beating the cooked bread. Filled with spiced chickpeas, it is comfort food of the highest order.

substitutions.notes: makes a slightly different but authentic roti

steps.instruction: Make the roti dough: combine flour, baking powder, and salt. Add warm water and oil. Knead for 5 minutes until smooth and elastic. Rest for 30 minutes covered.

For the curry: heat 2 tbsp oil, sauté onion 5 minutes. Add garlic, curry powder, cumin, turmeric, and whole Scotch bonnet. Cook 2 minutes. Add chickpeas and water. Simmer 20 minutes, mashing some chickpeas for thickness.
Divide dough into 4 balls. Roll each into a large circle (30 cm). Brush with oil, fold in half, brush again, fold into a quarter. Roll out again into a large circle.

Heat a tava or large flat pan. Cook each roti for 2 minutes per side, brushing with oil. When done, clap vigorously between your palms or with two wooden spatulas to separate the layers (buss it up). Keep warm in a tea towel.
Serve the roti with the chickpea curry spooned inside or alongside.

image_placeholder: A torn, flaky buss-up-shut roti on a plate alongside a bowl of golden chickpea curry, with a whole Scotch bonnet pepper visible in the curry.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Carib lager or fresh lime juice.
tips: The clapping step creates the characteristic torn, layered texture — don't skip it.
The dough must rest to relax the gluten, making it easier to roll thin.
Brush the roti with oil while cooking for extra flakiness.

title: Jollof Rice
description: Jollof rice is West Africa's most celebrated one-pot dish — long-grain rice cooked in a rich, smoky tomato and pepper sauce. The Nigerian version is distinguished by its use of Scotch bonnet peppers, party rice long-grain, and the coveted 'party jollof' smokiness from the bottom of the pot. It is the centrepiece of Nigerian celebrations, weddings, and family gatherings. The 'jollof wars' between Nigeria and Ghana over who makes the best version are legendary.

steps.instruction: Blend tinned tomatoes, red peppers, Scotch bonnet, and 1 onion until smooth. This is the pepper-tomato base.
Heat oil in a large, heavy pot over medium heat. Dice remaining onion and fry for 3 minutes. Add tomato purée and fry for 5 minutes until it darkens in colour and the raw smell disappears.
Pour in the blended pepper-tomato mixture. Cook on medium heat for 15-20 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the sauce reduces by about a third, thickens, and the oil floats on top.
Add chicken stock, curry powder, thyme, bay leaves, seasoning cubes, white pepper, and salt. Bring to a boil. Add rinsed rice and stir once to combine.

Cover tightly (place foil under the lid for a better seal). Cook on very low heat for 30-35 minutes without lifting the lid. The rice should absorb all the liquid and be fluffy. The slightly charred rice at the bottom (party jollof) is a prized delicacy. Fluff and serve.

image_placeholder: A large platter of vivid orange-red Nigerian jollof rice, fluffy and fragrant, served with fried plantain and a piece of grilled chicken on the side.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Star or Gulder lager (Nigerian beers), or a chilled Zobo (hibiscus drink).
tips: Don't lift the lid during cooking — the steam is essential for fluffy, evenly cooked rice.
The smoky 'party jollof' bottom layer is intentional and prized. Let it develop but scrape it up before it burns.
Frying the tomato purée until dark is critical — it removes the tinny taste and deepens the colour.

title: Egusi Soup
description: Egusi soup is Nigeria's most popular soup — a thick, hearty stew made from ground melon seeds, leafy greens, palm oil, and assorted proteins. The melon seeds give the soup its distinctive nutty flavour and thick, rich consistency. It is served with fufu, pounded yam, or eba (garri), using the swallow to scoop up the flavourful soup. Each region and family has their own variation, but the combination of egusi, palm oil, and greens is constant.

steps.instruction: Season meat with onion, seasoning cubes, and salt. Cover with water and boil for 30-40 minutes until tender. Reserve the stock.
Mix ground egusi with a little water to form a thick paste. Heat palm oil in a large pot until it becomes clear. Add diced onion and fry for 3 minutes.
Add egusi paste by dropping small lumps into the hot oil. Allow them to fry for 5-7 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the egusi firms up and becomes slightly crumbly.
Add blended Scotch bonnet, crayfish, locust beans, and 400 ml meat stock. Stir well. Add the cooked meat and smoked fish. Simmer for 15 minutes until the soup thickens.
Add chopped spinach, stir, and cook for 5 minutes until wilted. Adjust seasoning. Serve with pounded yam, fufu, or eba.

image_placeholder: A deep bowl of thick, rich egusi soup with visible chunks of meat, greens, and a glistening layer of palm oil, served with a mound of smooth pounded yam.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Star lager or a glass of palm wine.
tips: Ground egusi (melon seeds) can be found at African shops. If unavailable, grind pumpkin seeds to a fine powder.
Palm oil is essential for authentic flavour and the characteristic orange colour.
The swallow (fufu, pounded yam, or eba) is integral — it's used to scoop the soup.

title: Suya
description: Suya is Nigeria's beloved street food — thinly sliced beef rubbed with a peanut and spice mixture (yaji), then grilled over charcoal. Sold by suya vendors (mai suya) at roadside spots from dusk onwards, the irresistible aroma of grilling spiced meat is a signature of Nigerian nightlife. The yaji spice mix — ground peanuts, cayenne, ginger, garlic, and onion powder — creates a crust that is nutty, spicy, and deeply savoury.

steps.instruction: Make the yaji spice: combine ground peanuts, cayenne, onion powder, garlic powder, ginger, paprika, crushed seasoning cube, and salt. Mix well.
Brush beef slices lightly with oil, then coat generously with yaji spice on both sides, pressing to adhere. Thread onto skewers.
Grill over high heat (barbecue or grill pan) for 2-3 minutes per side until charred and cooked through. Baste with oil while grilling.
Serve immediately on newspaper (traditional) or a plate with sliced onions, tomatoes, and extra yaji spice for dipping.

image_placeholder: Charred suya skewers on newspaper with sliced red onion rings and tomato slices, dusted with extra yaji spice.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Star lager or a chilled Chapman (Nigerian cocktail).
tips: Slice the beef as thinly as possible — partially freezing it first makes this much easier.
The ground peanut in yaji should be very fine — use a food processor or buy pre-ground.
Authentic suya is grilled over charcoal; a very hot barbecue gives the best result at home.

title: Chin Chin
description: Chin chin is Nigeria's most popular snack — small, crunchy pieces of sweetened fried dough spiced with nutmeg and sometimes coconut. It is made for every Nigerian celebration, packed in jars as gifts, and sold in markets across the country. The dough is rolled thin, cut into small strips or diamonds, and deep-fried until golden and crunchy. It keeps for weeks and is utterly moreish.

steps.instruction: Combine flour, sugar, nutmeg, baking powder, and salt. Rub in cold butter until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs.
Beat eggs with evaporated milk. Add to the flour mixture and knead into a firm, smooth dough. Rest 10 minutes.
Roll the dough out to 3-4 mm thickness on a floured surface. Cut into small strips or diamond shapes about 2 cm long.

Heat oil to 160°C (lower than usual frying temperature). Fry chin chin in batches for 4-5 minutes, stirring gently, until deep golden and crunchy. Fry slowly for even crunch. Drain on kitchen paper. Cool completely — it crisps further as it cools.

image_placeholder: A glass jar filled with golden, crunchy chin chin pieces, with some scattered on a colourful Nigerian print cloth.
tips: Fry at a lower temperature (160°C) than most fried foods — this allows the centre to cook through before the outside burns.
Chin chin must be completely cool before storing — any residual heat creates moisture and reduces crunch.
Store in an airtight container for up to 3 weeks.

title: Puff Puff
description: Puff puff is West Africa's beloved doughnut — light, fluffy balls of yeasted dough deep-fried to golden perfection. They are the Nigerian equivalent of beignets or Dutch oliebollen, sold as street food and made for celebrations. The exterior is crispy while the inside is soft, airy, and slightly sweet. They are typically sprinkled with icing sugar or served plain, and are irresistibly poppable.

steps.instruction: Combine flour, sugar, yeast, salt, and nutmeg. Add warm water and mix to a smooth, thick batter (thicker than pancake batter but not a dough). Cover and leave in a warm place for 1-2 hours until doubled and very bubbly.
Heat oil to 170°C in a deep pot. Using wet hands or two spoons, drop small balls of batter into the hot oil. Fry 5-6 at a time.
Fry for 4-5 minutes, turning occasionally, until deep golden all over. They should be evenly coloured. Drain on kitchen paper.
Dust with icing sugar while still warm and serve immediately.

image_placeholder: A pile of golden, round puff puff balls dusted with icing sugar on a plate, with one broken open to show the fluffy interior.
tips: The batter must be well-risen and very bubbly for light puff puff. Don't rush the proofing.
Drop small balls — they expand significantly during frying.
Wet your hands before scooping the batter to prevent sticking.

title: Doro Wat with Injera
title_en: Ethiopian Spiced Chicken Stew with Injera
description: Doro wat is Ethiopia's national dish — a slow-cooked chicken stew in a rich, deeply spiced sauce of berbere (Ethiopian chilli spice blend) and niter kibbeh (spiced clarified butter). Served on injera (a spongy, sour fermented flatbread), it is the centrepiece of Ethiopian celebrations, especially holidays and weddings. The dish is eaten communally — torn pieces of injera are used to scoop up the stew, making it a shared, convivial experience.

substitutions.substitute: 1 tbsp paprika + 1 tsp cayenne + 1 tsp ground coriander + 0.5 tsp fenugreek + 0.5 tsp cardamom
60 g ghee + 0.5 tsp ground cardamom + 0.5 tsp ground fenugreek

steps.instruction: Rub chicken pieces with lemon juice and salt. Set aside for 30 minutes. In a dry, heavy pot, cook the finely diced onions over medium heat without oil for 10-12 minutes, stirring frequently, until very soft, dry, and slightly golden.
Add niter kibbeh to the onions. Stir in berbere, garlic, ginger, and tomato purée. Cook for 5 minutes, stirring constantly, until the spices are very fragrant and the mixture deepens in colour.

Add 200 ml water and bring to a simmer. Add chicken pieces, coating in the sauce. Cover and cook on low heat for 45 minutes, turning occasionally, until the chicken is very tender and the sauce is thick and rich.
Add scored hard-boiled eggs in the last 10 minutes of cooking, nestling them into the sauce. Serve directly on injera with extra injera on the side for scooping.

image_placeholder: A large round plate lined with injera, topped with dark red doro wat stew with chicken pieces and hard-boiled eggs, surrounded by colourful side dishes.
wine_pairing_notes: Ethiopian honey wine (tej), a dark lager, or a glass of cold Zobo (hibiscus drink).
tips: The onions must be cooked slowly and without oil first — this is the foundation of the sauce.
Berbere spice blend is available at Ethiopian shops and online — it makes a huge difference versus improvised substitutes.
Scoring the eggs allows the sauce to flavour them throughout.

title: Tibs
description: Tibs is Ethiopia's most popular quick-cooked dish — cubes of beef sautéed with onions, peppers, garlic, and rosemary in niter kibbeh. Unlike the long-cooked wats, tibs are fast, fresh, and vibrant, often served sizzling in a clay pot. The dish ranges from mild (regular tibs) to fiery (awaze tibs, with berbere). It is the everyday Ethiopian answer to a stir-fry.

steps.instruction: Heat niter kibbeh in a large pan or wok over high heat. Add beef cubes in a single layer and sear for 2-3 minutes without moving, until browned.
Add onion, garlic, and rosemary. Stir-fry for 3 minutes until onion softens.
Add green pepper, tomato, and jalapeño. Stir-fry for 2 minutes. Season with salt and pepper. The vegetables should be slightly crisp.
Serve immediately on a hot plate or clay dish, alongside injera or rice.

image_placeholder: Sizzling beef tibs in a clay pot with peppers, onions, and tomato, garnished with rosemary, served alongside rolled injera.
wine_pairing_notes: An Ethiopian Castel beer or a glass of tej (honey wine).
tips: High heat and a single layer of beef ensures proper searing — overcrowding leads to steaming.
Niter kibbeh adds an incredible aromatic depth — it's worth making or buying from Ethiopian shops.
Serve sizzling hot — tibs lose their charm when lukewarm.

title: Sambusa
description: Sambusas are East Africa's beloved fried pastry — crispy triangular parcels filled with spiced minced meat, onions, and chilli. They are found across the Horn of Africa and East African coast, reflecting the centuries-old trade connections with the Arabian Peninsula and Indian subcontinent. Lighter and crispier than Indian samosas, they are a staple of Ramadan iftars and tea-time snacking.

ingredients.name: vegetable oil
ingredients.notes: 2 tbsp for dough + rest for frying

steps.instruction: Make the pastry: combine flour and salt, add 2 tbsp oil and warm water. Knead for 5 minutes until smooth. Rest covered for 30 minutes.
For the filling: fry onion in 1 tbsp oil for 5 minutes. Add garlic, chilli, cumin, coriander, and cinnamon. Cook 1 minute. Add mince and cook for 8 minutes until browned. Add fresh coriander. Cool.
Divide pastry into 8 balls. Roll each into a thin oval. Cut in half. Form each half into a cone, fill with mixture, seal edges with water.
Deep-fry at 170°C for 4-5 minutes until deep golden and crispy. Drain on kitchen paper. Serve hot.

image_placeholder: Golden, crispy sambusas arranged on a plate with a small bowl of green chilli sauce for dipping, on a patterned Kenyan textile.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Tusker (Kenyan lager) or a cup of sweet spiced tea.
tips: The pastry must be rolled very thin for maximum crispiness.
Ensure the filling is completely cooled before wrapping or the pastry will become soggy.
Seal the edges well with water to prevent them opening during frying.

title: Mandazi
description: Mandazi are East Africa's everyday doughnuts — slightly sweet, coconut-scented fried bread triangles found at every street corner and tea stall from Kenya to Tanzania. Unlike Western doughnuts, mandazi are less sweet and more bread-like, making them perfect as breakfast or a snack with chai tea. They use coconut milk for richness and cardamom for its distinctive aroma.

steps.instruction: Combine flour, sugar, yeast, cardamom, and salt. Add egg and coconut milk. Knead for 8 minutes until smooth and elastic. Cover and prove for 1 hour until doubled.
Roll the dough to 1 cm thickness. Cut into triangles (about 8 cm per side).
Heat oil to 170°C. Fry mandazi in batches for 2-3 minutes per side until golden and puffed. Drain on kitchen paper.
Serve warm with chai tea or sprinkled with icing sugar.

image_placeholder: Puffy, golden mandazi triangles on a plate next to a cup of chai tea, dusted lightly with icing sugar.
tips: The dough should be soft but not sticky — adjust flour or coconut milk as needed.
Don't make the oil too hot — mandazi need time to cook through and puff up.
Best served warm from the fryer with a hot cup of chai.

title: Zanzibar Pilau
description: Zanzibar pilau is a fragrant, spiced rice dish reflecting the island's rich history as a spice trade hub. Infused with cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, and cumin, it is cooked with caramelised onions and meat in a method that echoes Indian biryani and Middle Eastern pilaf. The result is beautifully aromatic, with each grain of rice infused with the spices. It is Zanzibar's signature dish, served at weddings and celebrations.

steps.instruction: Heat oil in a heavy pot. Fry onions for 10-12 minutes until deep golden brown. Remove half and set aside for garnish.
Add cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, and cumin to remaining onions. Fry 30 seconds. Add garlic, ginger, and turmeric. Cook 1 minute. Add meat and brown for 5 minutes.
Add stock, potatoes, and salt. Bring to a boil, then simmer for 20 minutes until the meat is nearly tender.

Add drained rice and stir gently. Bring to a boil, then reduce to very low heat. Cover tightly and cook for 20 minutes until rice is fluffy and liquid absorbed. Let rest 10 minutes, fluff with a fork. Garnish with reserved fried onions.

image_placeholder: A large platter of golden Zanzibar pilau rice studded with whole spices and tender meat, topped with crispy fried onions.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Kilimanjaro (Tanzanian lager) or spiced chai.
tips: The deeply caramelised onions are crucial — they provide colour, sweetness, and depth.
Don't stir the rice once the lid is on — this keeps the grains separate.
The whole spices are for flavour, not eating — warn diners.

title: Bobotie
description: Bobotie is South Africa's national dish — a fragrant, curry-spiced minced meat bake topped with a savoury egg custard. It reflects the Cape Malay heritage of Cape Town, blending Dutch, Malay, and Indian influences. The filling features minced meat enriched with dried fruit, almonds, and chutney, spiced with curry powder and turmeric, then baked under a golden custard. Served with yellow rice and chutney, it is uniquely South African.

ingredients.name: milk
ingredients.notes: 100 ml for bread, 100 ml for custard

steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 180°C. Heat oil and fry onions for 5 minutes. Add garlic, curry powder, and turmeric. Cook 2 minutes. Add mince and cook for 8 minutes until browned.

Squeeze excess milk from bread and add the bread to the meat. Add raisins, almonds, chutney, lemon juice, 1 beaten egg, and salt. Mix well and press into a greased baking dish. Tuck bay leaves into the top.
Whisk remaining 2 eggs with 100 ml milk. Pour over the meat mixture.
Bake for 35-40 minutes until the custard topping is set and golden. Let rest 10 minutes before serving with yellow rice (turmeric rice), chutney, and banana slices.

image_placeholder: A golden-topped bobotie fresh from the oven with bay leaves visible on the custard surface, served alongside yellow turmeric rice and chutney.
wine_pairing_notes: A South African Chenin Blanc or Pinotage pairs beautifully.
tips: The bread soaked in milk gives the filling a soft, mousse-like texture.
Bay leaves on top are traditional and decorative — don't skip them.
Bobotie tastes even better the next day — the spices meld overnight.

title: Chakalaka
description: Chakalaka is South Africa's vibrant, spicy vegetable relish — a colourful mix of peppers, onions, tomatoes, carrots, and baked beans, seasoned with curry powder and chilli. It originated in the mining townships of Johannesburg and has become a beloved accompaniment to braai (barbecue), pap (maize porridge), and bread. It is always served at South African gatherings and improves with time.

steps.instruction: Heat oil in a large pan. Fry onions for 5 minutes until softened. Add garlic, curry powder, and chilli flakes. Cook 1 minute.
Add grated carrots and peppers. Cook for 5 minutes until slightly softened but still with some bite.
Add tinned tomatoes and baked beans. Simmer for 15 minutes until thickened. Season with salt. Serve warm or at room temperature.

image_placeholder: A bowl of vibrant, colourful chakalaka with visible peppers, carrots, and beans, served alongside braai meat and pap on a wooden board.
wine_pairing_notes: A South African Chenin Blanc or an ice-cold Castle Lager.
tips: Chakalaka improves overnight — make it the day before for the best flavour.
Adjust chilli to taste — it should be spicy but not overwhelming.
It's traditionally served at room temperature, not piping hot.

title: Bunny Chow
title_en: Durban Curry in a Bread Loaf
description: Bunny chow is Durban's most iconic street food — a hollowed-out half loaf of white bread filled with curry. Created by the Indian community during apartheid (when eating at restaurants was forbidden for non-whites), the bread served as both plate and utensil. You tear off pieces of the bread shell to scoop up the curry. Bean curry (the original) or lamb curry are the most popular fillings. It is messy, flavourful, and uniquely South African.

steps.instruction: Heat oil in a heavy pot. Brown lamb in batches, remove. Fry onions 5 minutes, add garlic, ginger, curry leaves, curry powder, turmeric, and cumin. Cook 2 minutes.
Add tinned tomatoes, return lamb, add water and salt. Bring to a simmer, cover, and cook for 1.5 hours until lamb is very tender.
Add potatoes in the last 30 minutes. The curry should be thick and rich.
Hollow out the bread loaf halves, leaving a 2 cm wall. Fill with hot curry, placing the bread 'lid' on top. Eat by tearing bread and scooping curry.

image_placeholder: A hollowed-out half loaf of white bread overflowing with rich lamb curry, with the bread lid resting on top, on a sheet of wax paper.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Castle Lager or a South African Pinotage.
tips: Use a firm, unsliced white bread — too soft and it disintegrates.
The curry must be thick, not watery — it needs to be scooped with bread.
Bunny chow is meant to be messy — embrace it.

title: Malva Pudding
description: Malva pudding is South Africa's most beloved dessert — a warm, sticky, sponge-like pudding of Dutch-Malay origin, drenched in a hot cream sauce the moment it comes out of the oven. The sponge gets its distinctive flavour from apricot jam and vinegar, creating a moist, caramelised sweetness. The hot sauce soaks into the sponge, making it irresistibly gooey. Served with custard or cream, it is pure comfort.

substitutions.notes: different fruit flavour but similar sweetness

steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 180°C. Grease a deep baking dish. Beat sugar and eggs until thick and pale. Beat in apricot jam.
Sift flour, bicarbonate of soda, and salt. Fold into the egg mixture alternately with milk, melted butter, and vinegar. Pour into the baking dish.
Bake for 40-45 minutes until risen, golden, and a skewer comes out with moist crumbs.

While baking, make the sauce: heat cream, butter, sugar, hot water, and vanilla until butter melts and sugar dissolves. As soon as the pudding comes out of the oven, poke holes all over with a skewer and pour the hot sauce over. Let it absorb for 10 minutes. Serve warm with custard or extra cream.

image_placeholder: A golden malva pudding in a baking dish, glistening with cream sauce, with one portion scooped out showing the moist, sticky interior, alongside a jug of custard.
wine_pairing_notes: A South African Muscadel or a cup of rooibos tea.
tips: Pour the sauce over while both the pudding and sauce are hot — this ensures maximum absorption.
The vinegar and bicarbonate of soda reaction creates the distinctive lightness of the sponge.
Malva pudding reheats beautifully — microwave individual portions and pour over extra warm sauce.

title: Boerewors with Pap
title_en: South African Farmer's Sausage with Maize Porridge
description: Boerewors ('farmer's sausage') with pap is South Africa's quintessential braai (barbecue) dish. The coiled sausage, made from beef and pork with coriander seed and cloves, is grilled over coals until the casing snaps and the meat is juicy. Served with pap (a stiff maize porridge similar to polenta) and a tomato-onion relish, it is the heart of South African outdoor cooking culture.

steps.instruction: Make the pap: bring 1 litre of salted water to a boil. Gradually whisk in maize meal, stirring constantly to prevent lumps. Reduce to very low heat, cover, and cook for 30 minutes, stirring every 10 minutes, until thick and pulling away from the sides.
Make the relish (sous): heat oil and fry onions for 5 minutes. Add tomatoes and chilli. Cook 10 minutes until thick. Season with salt.

Grill boerewors over medium-high coals (or in a hot grill pan) for 5-6 minutes per side. Do not prick the casing — the juices should stay inside. The casing should be nicely charred and the interior cooked through.
Stir butter into the pap if using. Serve the boerewors coil cut into portions, with pap and the tomato-onion relish.

image_placeholder: A coiled boerewors sausage with char marks on a braai grid, served alongside a mound of smooth white pap and a bowl of chunky tomato-onion relish.
wine_pairing_notes: A robust South African Pinotage or a cold Castle Lager.
tips: Never prick boerewors — the casing holds in the juices. If it bursts, the sausage dries out.
Medium coals, not raging hot — boerewors needs time to cook through without burning the outside.
Pap must be stirred regularly to prevent lumps — a wooden spoon works best.

title: Mushroom Bourguignon
description: This vegan take on the classic French beef bourguignon replaces meat with a hearty mix of mushrooms, braised in red wine with pearl onions, carrots, and thyme. The mushrooms absorb the rich, wine-infused sauce beautifully, creating a deeply satisfying dish that rivals the original. It is perfect proof that plant-based cooking can be luxurious and comforting.

ingredients.name: mixed mushrooms
ingredients.notes: chestnut, portobello, shiitake — quartered or halved

substitutions.notes: use whatever is available and in season

ingredients.name: red wine
ingredients.notes: full-bodied, such as a Burgundy or Côtes du Rhône

substitutions.substitute: vegetable stock + 2 tbsp red wine vinegar

steps.instruction: Heat 2 tbsp oil in a large casserole over high heat. Sear mushrooms in batches for 4-5 minutes until deeply golden. Remove and set aside.
Add remaining oil, reduce heat. Cook pearl onions, carrots, and celery for 5 minutes. Add garlic and tomato purée, cook 1 minute. Sprinkle flour over and stir 1 minute.
Pour in red wine and stock. Add thyme and bay leaves. Return mushrooms. Bring to a simmer, cover, and cook on low heat for 40 minutes until vegetables are tender and sauce is rich and thick.
Season with salt and pepper. Serve with creamy mashed potatoes or crusty bread.

image_placeholder: A rustic casserole dish of mushroom bourguignon with golden mushrooms, pearl onions, and carrots in a rich, dark wine sauce, with crusty bread alongside.
wine_pairing_notes: A medium-bodied Pinot Noir from Burgundy is the natural pairing.
tips: Sear the mushrooms properly — they should be deeply golden, not steamed.
A good red wine makes all the difference — use something you'd drink.
Like all braises, this tastes even better the next day.

title: Jackfruit Tacos
description: Young green jackfruit has a remarkable ability to mimic pulled meat when braised in smoky, spicy sauces. These vegan tacos use jackfruit cooked in a chipotle-adobo sauce until tender and shredded, then served in corn tortillas with pickled red onion, avocado, and coriander. They deliver all the satisfaction of carnitas tacos without any animal products.

ingredients.name: tinned young green jackfruit
ingredients.notes: in water or brine, drained and rinsed

steps.instruction: Drain and rinse jackfruit. Squeeze out excess water and shred with a fork into pulled-meat-like pieces.
Heat oil in a pan. Fry garlic for 30 seconds, add cumin and smoked paprika. Cook 30 seconds. Add jackfruit and toss to coat. Add chopped chipotles, adobo sauce, and tinned tomatoes.
Simmer for 20 minutes, stirring and pressing with a spatula to break down the jackfruit further. The sauce should reduce and the jackfruit should look like shredded meat. Add lime juice and salt.
Warm tortillas. Fill with pulled jackfruit, sliced avocado, pickled red onion, and coriander. Serve with lime wedges.

image_placeholder: Corn tortillas filled with smoky pulled jackfruit, topped with sliced avocado, pink pickled onion, and fresh coriander.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Mexican lager or a lime agua fresca.
tips: Use young green jackfruit in water/brine, NOT ripe jackfruit (which is sweet and fruity).
Press and shred the jackfruit thoroughly for the most meat-like texture.
